Potential mystery: - Sasha has uncovered a local adult exploiting vulnerable teens through a fake “wellness programme,” perhaps coercing them into theft, blackmail or online scams. - The adult has taken Sasha’s phone but she secretly sends messages through an old smartwatch, game console, or scheduled text service. - The emojis are a clue to a location: the old blood bank beneath a former hospital, a red-painted storage unit, or a pub called The Three Crowns where “blood” refers to a cocktail. Dark comedy: - The school initially launches a “Where Is Sasha?” wellbeing initiative with wristbands and a bake sale, while doing very little useful. - Niamh must sneak out during a parent-led neighbourhood watch patrol populated by people who are more interested in someone’s recycling bins. - The villain’s scheme is grandiose but embarrassingly banal: blackmailing pupils over stolen exam papers, botched influencer content, or local property fraud. ## Plot direction 2: The dead boy texts Frankie receives 🩸🩸🩸 from the phone number of Eli Ward, a boy who died two years ago after an accident at an abandoned leisure centre. Frankie was present that night and has carried guilt ever since. The text leads her back to the site. She finds evidence that Eli’s death was not an accident—or that the official story was shaped by adults protecting themselves. Someone is using Eli’s number to make Frankie confess, but they may also be trying to reveal the truth. Potential mystery: - Eli survived longer than everyone believed and tried to call for help, but a responsible adult ignored it. - The school covered up the incident because it occurred during an unofficial initiation event involving staff or senior pupils. - Frankie’s old friends know more than they admitted. - The sender is Eli’s younger brother, who wants revenge, but his threats accidentally uncover a real cover-up. Dark comedy: - Frankie’s mum keeps interpreting every suspicious event as “a phase.” - The local Facebook group turns the mystery into an argument about youths, dog mess and council funding. - Frankie’s former friends are now trying to be influencers, youth councillors, or straight-A students and are wildly unsuited to criminal conspiracy. ## Plot direction 3: The body in the school greenhouse Mara receives the bloody text from a number she does not recognise. The next day, three drops of blood are found on the school greenhouse door. A teacher announces it is “probably foxes,” which is technically possible but emotionally unhelpful. Mara discovers the sender is a shy Year 10 student who has witnessed something in the abandoned horticulture block. They have been hiding because everyone assumes they are lying. The “blood” belongs not to a murder victim but to a notorious school bully who has been badly injured after uncovering an illicit operation. Potential mystery: - The groundskeeper and a teacher are using the old greenhouse to grow cannabis, rare plants, or something comically unprofitable like counterfeit truffles. - A teacher has been falsifying school records and using students’ personal data for fraud. - The greenhouse is a meeting place for a group of students filming an elaborate horror prank that gets out of hand. - The real danger is a violent ex-pupil living in an unused caretaker’s flat. Dark comedy: - Teachers treat blood evidence as a safeguarding inconvenience. - The headteacher is obsessed with the upcoming Ofsted visit and tries to keep the incident “internal.” - Students’ guesses are absurdly specific and spread through group chats within minutes. ## Plot direction 4: Supernatural-but-maybe-not bloodline horror At 12:03 a.m., Theo receives three blood emojis from his older sister, Holly, who disappeared during a local festival a year ago. At the same time, his bathroom tap runs red. His parents tell him it is “probably the pipes,” which feels insufficiently reassuring. The town has an annual blood-themed tradition, supposedly dating from plague times, involving a red lantern procession, a ceremonial “three drops,” and an old quarry. Teenagers treat it as a drinking opportunity. Theo learns Holly became involved with a secret society that believes certain local families are connected to something beneath the quarry. Possible twist: - The supernatural elements are a cover for a very human crime: a wealthy local family is using the legend to terrify people and protect a criminal operation. - Or the supernatural is real, but its main concern is petty and bureaucratic rather than grandly evil: an ancient entity is bound by parish council paperwork and wants to renegotiate its sacrifice terms. - Holly is alive but hiding because she discovered that the “curse” selects people based on family medical records. Dark comedy: - Ancient ritual conducted by people who cannot organise a queue. - A demon speaks in passive-aggressive village-newsletter language. - The protagonist’s mum refuses to believe in ghosts but does believe the council is corrupt enough to conceal one. ## Plot direction 5: A dark comic crime caper The text arrives just after midnight: 🩸🩸🩸. Niamh assumes it is from her dramatic ex-best friend, Callum. In fact, it is a code meaning “third emergency”: a signal they invented as children to mean “I’ve done something catastrophically stupid and need extraction.” Callum has stolen a bag from the wrong person, believing it contains exam papers, only to find it holds evidence of a local councillor’s corruption or a dangerous criminal’s money laundering. He is hiding in an absurd location—a garden centre Christmas display, a motorway service station soft-play area, or a disused miniature railway. The story becomes an overnight-to-next-day chase involving: - A stolen mobility scooter. - A bus driver who becomes accidentally involved. - A school production rehearsing nearby. - A villain whose criminal operation is endangered by teenagers who cannot stop arguing about snacks. Dark comedy: - Stakes are real, but the characters remain painfully adolescent. - The protagonists must evade criminals while lying to parents about being at a sleepover. - Their survival depends on obscure local knowledge: a back entrance to the leisure centre, the timing of the Number 42 bus, and which corner shop sells a phone charger after midnight. ## Plot direction 6: The blood emoji as a false alarm with emotional stakes The message is sent by the protagonist’s younger brother, who has cut himself while attempting an ill-advised DIY procedure or trying to remove a cursed-looking ring. The protagonist races to him expecting a disaster, only to find the immediate injury minor—but discovers he is involved in a much bigger problem: he has been targeted by older teenagers, recruited into a violent online group, or manipulated by an adult. This could become a story about family, shame and asking for help. The dark humour comes from the gap between the melodramatic emoji message and the painfully ordinary reality of A&E, parental panic, and sibling cruelty. Potential angle: - The brother has accidentally obtained a phone containing evidence of abuse or blackmail. - He has been participating in a “challenge” that is actually coercive. - The protagonist’s strained relationship with him means he sent the message only when things became truly frightening. ## Potential central themes - The difference between being dramatic and being in danger. - How teenage girls’ fear and anger are dismissed as attention-seeking. - Guilt over past mistakes and the possibility of repair. - The burden of being the “responsible” friend. - Small-town mythmaking and how communities conceal harm. - Friendship after betrayal. - Family secrets and inherited damage. - The strange comedy of adults pretending they have everything under control. - The danger of communication stripped down to symbols. - What happens when a cry for help is mistaken for a joke. ## Tone possibilities ### Deadpan contemporary noir First-person narrator who observes everything with sarcastic precision. Real emotional pain sits beneath the jokes. Example tonal approach: > At 12:03 a.m., my phone vibrated with three drops of blood. I assumed it was either a murder, a period, or Callum trying to borrow money again. ### Gothic suburban comedy Rain, red-brick estates, old churches, neon signs, unnerving local rituals. Comedy comes from mundane British institutions colliding with horror. ### Fast-paced caper Short scenes, escalating complications, banter-heavy dialogue, physical comedy, real threat beneath it. ### Psychological mystery The narrator cannot remember sending or receiving key messages; the blood emojis reveal buried trauma. Comedy is darker and more defensive. ### Folk-horror satire A rural village is committed to a creepy tradition but cannot agree on parking, catering or health-and-safety forms. ## Potential ending types ### Hopeful but bruised The missing person is found alive. The protagonist exposes the danger, but relationships are altered. The final message is a joke between friends, now carrying different weight. ### Bittersweet The victim cannot be saved, but the truth emerges. The protagonist takes responsibility for their past and rejects the town’s convenient lies. ### Grimly comic The villain is caught because of a ridiculous mistake: a school livestream, a badly timed village raffle, or an incriminating Facebook Marketplace listing. The final line reveals another ominous emoji text, possibly from a parent. ### Supernatural sting The human mystery is solved, but the blood emoji message came from something else entirely. The protagonist receives it again at midnight—this time from their own number. ### Moral complication The sender was not entirely innocent. They engineered the crisis to expose someone, but their methods endangered others. The protagonist must decide whether truth justifies manipulation. ## Eight-chapter pacing models ### Model A: Missing friend mystery 1. **The Text** — Protagonist receives 🩸🩸🩸 and dismisses it; friend vanishes by morning. 2. **The Lie** — School and family explanations do not add up; protagonist finds a clue in the sender’s belongings. 3. **The Code** — The meaning of the emojis emerges; protagonist recruits an unwilling ally. 4. **The Wrong Place** — They investigate a location and narrowly escape or find evidence of violence. 5. **The Suspect** — A trusted adult becomes suspicious; protagonist’s own secret complicates matters. 6. **The Trap** — The missing friend sends another signal; protagonists walk into a controlled situation. 7. **The Rescue** — Truth, confrontation and a chaotic rescue in a distinctly British public place. 8. **The Aftermath** — Consequences, repaired friendship, unresolved unease or darkly comic final image. ### Model B: Dead boy mystery 1. Text from dead boy’s phone. 2. Protagonist revisits the original accident site. 3. Old friend group reunites unwillingly. 4. Evidence reveals official story is false. 5. Someone is stalking/manipulating protagonist. 6. Sender revealed, but they are in danger too. 7. Full truth emerges; adult cover-up exposed. 8. Public confrontation and aftermath. ### Model C: Folk horror 1. Text and impossible sign of blood. 2. Local legend introduced; adults dismiss it. 3. Protagonist identifies a pattern in historical disappearances. 4. Festival preparations; friend becomes involved. 5. Discovery beneath quarry/church/hall. 6. Revelation: supernatural and human conspiracy overlap. 7. Ritual interrupted in absurdly chaotic fashion. 8. Town resumes normality; ominous final sign. ## Recurring motifs - Phone screens glowing in dark bedrooms. - Red stains that are not always blood: ketchup, paint, red dye, rust, cheap squash. - The number three: three drops, three missing people, three lies, three doors, three chances to call for help. - Rainwater in gutters resembling diluted blood. - Emergency services treated simultaneously with fear and boredom. - The local town Facebook group as a Greek chorus of misinformation. - A broken vending machine that repeatedly traps something important. - A cheap red hoodie or school tie as visual evidence. - A recurring phrase: “Don’t be dramatic,” later recontextualised. # Reflection The strongest direction is likely a **contemporary missing-person mystery set in a damp, ordinary British town or sixth-form environment**, with a sharp first-person protagonist and a real emotional connection to the sender. This best serves the prompt because the blood emojis function immediately as an alarming but ambiguous hook, and the story can sustain suspense across eight chapters without needing too much mythology or worldbuilding. The folk-horror idea offers rich atmosphere and natural dark comedy, especially if the supernatural force is tied to parish bureaucracy or a ridiculous annual festival. However, eight chapters of approximately 1,000 words each may not leave enough room to establish a convincing local mythology, develop characters, solve a human mystery and pay off supernatural rules. It could work if kept tightly focused, but risks becoming overstuffed. The dead-boy-text premise is emotionally potent and naturally eerie. It also provides a strong internal arc involving guilt. Its main challenge is that it can become quite bleak, and the comic tone would need careful handling so the death does not feel trivialised. This might work best if the deceased person remains important as a person rather than simply functioning as a spooky plot device. The mystery could be compelling, but it would need a particularly sensitive balance of comedy and grief. The caper approach has enormous potential for pace, dialogue and darkly comic British absurdity. It could be very entertaining, particularly if the protagonists are forced through recognisable places—petrol stations, bus stations, retail parks, school halls—while pursued by people who are much more dangerous than the setting suggests. But it may make the blood emojis feel like a mere launch mechanism rather than a meaningful emotional element. The story could risk becoming broad comedy instead of dark comedy unless the friend relationship or central danger has genuine weight. The missing-friend version offers the clearest combination of plot momentum and character stakes. A protagonist who initially dismisses the message because the sender is known for drama creates immediate guilt once the friend disappears. This gives the text emotional relevance beyond mystery: the protagonist’s first failure is not understanding what the message means. The story can then explore the often-dismissed ways teenagers, particularly girls, communicate distress indirectly when direct communication feels unsafe. A protagonist like Niamh or Mara would suit the tone. Niamh’s controlled, high-achieving personality gives her a strong contrast with a more chaotic missing friend, while Mara’s cynical voice could carry the comedy particularly well. A hybrid might work: a sarcastic, competent girl who is seen as reliable by everyone else, but who privately resents being required to save people. That resentment could make her ignore the text at first, then drive her determination to make amends. A best-friend relationship rather than romance is especially promising. YA fiction often centres romance, but a damaged female friendship can provide intense stakes, history, betrayal and tenderness. The missing friend should not be merely “dramatic”; she should be someone who uses humour, exaggeration or chaos as armour, meaning the protagonist has learned to filter out her crises. The plot then challenges that assumption without suggesting that all emotional volatility is manipulative. For the central threat, a grounded crime involving a trusted local adult is likely more effective than an elaborate conspiracy. The adult might run a supposedly supportive youth programme, tutoring scheme, or community arts project, using it to manipulate vulnerable teens into theft, blackmail or online exploitation. This provides plausible stakes, makes the adult’s respectability unsettling, and allows the story to critique institutions that perform concern while failing young people. It should avoid becoming too grimly realistic for the desired comic tone; the criminal mechanism can be relatively contained, such as coercion and blackmail rather than extreme violence. A school greenhouse or disused community centre could make an excellent climax location. Both are recognisably British, slightly pathetic, and visually eerie at night. The greenhouse has the advantage of combining gothic atmosphere with comic detail: damp compost, failed tomatoes, a “Healthy Schools” banner peeling from the wall, and teachers dismissing everything as fox-related. A community centre offers more room for a public climax, perhaps during a fundraiser, talent show or local council event. The story’s comedy should not come from the victim’s peril. Instead, it should arise from: - The protagonist’s defensive internal narration. - The hopelessness of adult systems and school bureaucracy. - The petty concerns that persist during emergencies. - The bizarre mundanity of British public spaces at night. - The protagonists’ imperfect, argumentative methods of investigation. - Supporting characters who are comically unhelpful but not caricatures. The eight-chapter shape should aim for a clean escalation: message, disappearance, initial clue, investigation, revelation, setback, rescue, aftermath. Each chapter needs a change in information or stakes, rather than simply another clue. At roughly 8,000 words total, there is room for a small cast, one major mystery, a secondary relationship conflict, and a vivid setting—but not multiple villains, complicated timelines, or extensive lore. A particularly effective final note would be hopeful but uneasy. The missing friend is rescued and the culprit exposed, but the protagonist cannot completely undo her initial dismissal. Their friendship is not magically fixed; it becomes more honest. A final text message, perhaps something mundane but sent with one blood-drop emoji as a private joke, could restore humour while showing that symbols have changed meaning. Alternatively, ending on an entirely different ominous notification could offer a darker sting, but that may undermine the emotional closure of the rescue. The strongest overall concept therefore seems to be: a sarcastic, over-responsible sixth-form girl receives three blood emojis from her chaotic best friend just after midnight, assumes it is a joke or another crisis, and wakes to discover the friend has vanished. With the help of an unlikely ally, she uncovers a coercive scheme operating behind a respectable local youth initiative. The mystery unfolds through school corridors, group chats, a rain-soaked town and an abandoned greenhouse or community centre, balancing dread with deadpan British humour.
Great now let's continue with planning the short story. Output in this format: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This short story will be a **darkly comic British YA mystery** set in a damp, unremarkable market town, centred on a sixth-form college housed in an ageing former grammar school. The setting will feel recognisably British: rain-heavy streets, a half-empty retail park, school safeguarding posters, suspiciously fluorescent corridors, a local Facebook group full of misinformation, and adults who want crises to be dealt with “sensibly” until they become impossible to ignore. The story will be told in **close first person** through **Niamh Patel**, aged seventeen. Niamh is clever, controlled, dryly funny and exhausted by her reputation as the sensible one. She has a carefully managed life: good grades, colour-coded revision plans, parents who expect competence, and a friendship with the more unpredictable Sasha Reed that has become strained. Niamh loves Sasha but has grown resentful of always being recruited into late-night disasters, arguments and emotional emergencies. That resentment causes her to dismiss Sasha’s midnight message. The inciting message will be simple and unnerving: > 🩸🩸🩸 Niamh recognises the three blood drops as an old code from childhood. It means: **not a joke, not a normal emergency, come alone.** She has forgotten it—or chosen to forget it—because she has spent the past year convincing herself that Sasha’s chaos is not her responsibility. By the time Niamh understands the message was genuine, Sasha has disappeared. The central plot will involve Sasha uncovering a coercive scheme hidden behind a highly respected local youth initiative called **The Bridge Project**. Publicly, the project offers mentoring, mental-health support, study sessions and community volunteering to struggling young people. Privately, its charismatic director, **Gareth Vale**, identifies isolated teenagers and pressures them into carrying out small criminal tasks: stealing personal data, collecting packages, creating fake social-media accounts, and gathering blackmail material. The operation is deliberately mundane rather than cinematic; its horror lies in how ordinary, respectable and easy to overlook it is. Sasha has discovered evidence that Gareth is escalating the pressure on one of the younger students. When she threatens to expose him, she is trapped and silenced at an unused school greenhouse/storage area used by the project for “gardening outreach.” Her blood emoji text is sent from an old device before Gareth can take her phone. The story will prioritise: - A damaged but meaningful friendship between Niamh and Sasha. - A protagonist learning that “being responsible” is not the same as being emotionally unavailable. - Suspense rooted in uncertainty, isolation and institutional indifference. - Dark humour that comes from Niamh’s voice, awkward British social systems, school bureaucracy, adults’ evasions, and the absurdity of conducting a rescue around ordinary public life. - A contained mystery with one central antagonist, a small supporting cast and a clear emotional arc. Niamh will be joined by **Finn Mercer**, a sarcastic, slightly odd classmate who runs the college’s unofficial gossip account and is therefore much more useful than he looks. Finn is not a romantic lead. His role is to provide a contrasting investigative instinct: he is socially observant where Niamh is emotionally guarded, and he brings comic energy without reducing the danger. Their alliance begins because Finn knows Sasha had been attending The Bridge Project and has seen something strange near the greenhouse. The style will be: - Fast-moving and scene-led. - Witty, deadpan and observant. - Grounded in sensory details: wet uniforms, cracked phone screens, overboiled tea, stale corridors, compost, cheap deodorant and rain. - Emotionally direct at key moments, with humour dropping away when danger requires sincerity. - Built around short revelations at chapter endings to sustain momentum. The story will avoid: - A sprawling conspiracy with multiple villains. - Excessive supernatural elements or folklore. - Comedy that mocks Sasha’s distress or trivialises coercion. - A flawless protagonist or a convenient adult rescue. - A romance plot that distracts from the friendship at the centre. - A completely neat ending in which all trust is instantly restored. The ending will be cautiously hopeful. Sasha will be found alive and Gareth’s operation exposed, but Niamh must live with the fact that she did not respond immediately. Her friendship with Sasha will not return to its old form; instead, both girls will acknowledge the unhealthy pattern in which Sasha only asks for help at breaking point and Niamh only listens when guilt forces her to. The final note will be darkly comic but warmer: a message from Sasha, this time asking for something ordinary, followed by a single blood emoji used as a joke neither of them can quite laugh at yet. # Chapter Planning ## Chapter 1: Three Drops - Introduce Niamh’s voice, home life and pressure to be sensible. - Niamh is awake after midnight, supposedly revising but actually doom-scrolling and avoiding a history essay. - At 12:03 a.m., Sasha texts: **🩸🩸🩸**. - Niamh recognises the old code but hesitates. Sasha has recently been erratic, cancelling plans, starting arguments and sending dramatic messages after drinking or fighting with her mum. - Niamh types several replies, deletes them, then sends something dismissive: “If this is about Callum again, I’m legally asleep.” - Sasha does not respond. - Niamh briefly considers calling but decides Sasha will be fine. She tells herself she cannot keep being available every time Sasha has a crisis. - The following morning, Sasha is absent from college. Her seat is empty; her teachers assume she is ill or skiving. - Niamh receives a message from Sasha’s mother claiming Sasha has gone to stay with an aunt. - Niamh knows the aunt lives abroad and has no spare room, and that Sasha would never leave without her battered red hoodie. - End reveal: Niamh opens their old childhood chat history and finds the original meaning of the three-blood code: **COME ALONE. DON’T TELL ANYONE. I’M SCARED.** ## Chapter 2: The Girl Who Was “Probably Fine” - Niamh becomes increasingly convinced Sasha is missing, but nobody treats the situation as urgent. - The school’s pastoral lead gives Niamh a cereal bar and says Sasha may “need space,” which Niamh finds offensively vague. - Sasha’s mum repeats the aunt story and sounds frightened beneath her forced calm. She says Sasha came home late the previous evening, then left again after receiving a call. - Niamh notices Sasha’s bedroom window has been left open despite rain. Inside, the red hoodie is gone—but Sasha’s passport, charger and favourite trainers remain. - Finn Mercer appears, initially as an irritant. He mentions that Sasha had been spending time at The Bridge Project, a youth mentoring programme operating out of a renovated community centre. - Finn has seen Sasha arguing with Gareth Vale, the project director, outside school two days earlier. - Niamh dismisses Finn’s gossip-account methods but realises he may have useful information. - Sasha’s mother finds Sasha’s smashed phone hidden in a kitchen drawer. It has no SIM card. - End reveal: on the cracked lock screen is a new notification timestamped 12:04 a.m.—a photograph of a greenhouse door, marked with three small streaks of blood. ## Chapter 3: The Greenhouse Problem - Niamh and Finn investigate the unused school greenhouse after lessons. - Their partnership is tense: Niamh thinks Finn treats everything like content; Finn thinks Niamh treats emotions like unpaid admin. - The greenhouse sits beyond the sports hall, near a locked maintenance yard. It is officially used for an environmental club that has not met since 2019. - They find signs that someone has been using it recently: warm soil, new padlocks, discarded energy drink cans, a cheap burner phone box, and a torn scrap from Sasha’s red hoodie. - A few drops of dried blood appear on a workbench. Niamh tries to remain rational but is visibly shaken. - They hear someone approaching and hide among stacked compost bags while a figure enters, removes something from a cabinet and leaves. - Niamh recognises the figure as Gareth Vale. - Finn reveals that Gareth has access to school grounds through The Bridge Project’s “community partnership.” - Before they can report it, Niamh receives a voice note from Sasha’s number. There is no speech, only breathing, a metallic clang and a man’s voice saying, “You’re making this worse.” - End reveal: the voice note location metadata points not to the school but to the old community centre on the edge of town. ## Chapter 4: The Bridge Project - Niamh and Finn visit The Bridge Project under the pretence of asking about volunteering hours for university applications. - The centre is bright, friendly and aggressively wholesome: motivational posters, beanbags, herbal tea and adults who say “young people” as if it is a diagnosis. - Gareth is charming, calm and apparently concerned about Sasha. He says she had been struggling, mentions vague “family difficulties,” and subtly suggests Niamh is overreacting. - Niamh notices several younger students from school looking anxious around Gareth. One, Year Ten student Jayden, avoids eye contact and has a bruised wrist. - Finn distracts a volunteer while Niamh searches an office. She finds folders containing personal details on pupils, including attendance records, family circumstances and private safeguarding notes. - She also finds a coded list of names beside tasks: “phones,” “accounts,” “collection,” “delivery.” - Gareth catches Niamh in the office. He does not threaten her openly; instead, he tells her that Sasha has “always enjoyed attention” and asks whether Niamh really wants to make things harder for her friend. - Niamh leaves rattled, unsure whether she has enough evidence. - End reveal: Jayden slips Niamh a keyring as he leaves. It carries a storage-unit number and a note: **Sasha said you’d know what three meant.** ## Chapter 5: Things Sensible People Do Not Do - Niamh and Finn debate going to the storage unit versus telling adults. Niamh wants police involvement but fears Gareth will destroy evidence or move Sasha. - They approach a trusted teacher, Ms Bains, who is sympathetic but limited by procedure. She says she must report concerns through safeguarding channels and cannot promise immediate action. - Niamh’s frustration rises: every adult conversation produces forms, referrals and reassuring phrases, but no one is physically looking for Sasha. - Finn discovers that the storage unit belongs to a shell company connected to Gareth’s brother-in-law. - That evening, Niamh lies to her parents, saying she is at Finn’s house for group revision. Her parents are suspicious but distracted by work and their own assumption that Niamh would never do anything reckless. - Niamh and Finn travel to the storage site by bus, enduring a wet, miserable journey alongside loud teenagers and an elderly man who loudly narrates his own phone calls. - They use Jayden’s keyring to open the unit. - Inside are boxes of cheap phones, printed school records, fake ID materials and several bags tagged with student names. There is also a makeshift desk with Sasha’s notebook. - In the notebook, Sasha has written names of teenagers Gareth has coerced and an incomplete phrase: **“He keeps them where the plants—”** - Before they can leave, a car pulls up outside. Gareth has found them. - End reveal: Niamh and Finn escape through the back of the unit complex, but Finn is separated from Niamh—and Niamh receives a text from Finn’s phone: **COME BACK. ALONE.** ## Chapter 6: Don’t Be Dramatic - Niamh suspects Gareth has taken Finn, but the text may also be a trap. The message echoes Sasha’s original code and forces Niamh to confront what she ignored. - She calls Finn’s phone; Gareth answers. His tone remains calm, almost disappointed, and he tells Niamh she has involved herself in something she does not understand. - Gareth says Sasha is alive and can be found if Niamh brings the notebook and stops “making allegations.” - Niamh realises Gareth does not know she photographed the storage-unit evidence and uploaded it to a hidden cloud folder. - She finally tells her mother the truth. Her mother is furious, frightened and immediately practical, calling police while trying not to panic. - The police response is more serious now due to the evidence, but Niamh hears that they cannot simply rush in without locating the correct site. - Niamh remembers Sasha’s incomplete phrase and connects it with the greenhouse. “Where the plants sleep” was a joke Sasha made about the old potting shed behind the greenhouse. - Niamh notices that the school is hosting an evening fundraising event in the sports hall, giving Gareth a reason to access the grounds without suspicion. - Against instructions, Niamh heads to the school with her mother’s location-sharing enabled, knowing Gareth expects her to come alone. - End reveal: in the dark greenhouse, Niamh sees Sasha behind the frosted glass of the potting shed, alive but injured—and Gareth standing between them. ## Chapter 7: The Rescue Plan, Which Is Mostly Shouting - Niamh enters the greenhouse carrying Sasha’s notebook, pretending she has come alone and has not involved anyone. - Gareth attempts to manipulate her rather than immediately attack. He frames himself as someone “helping” troubled teenagers and claims Sasha ruined everything by stealing confidential materials. - Niamh stalls him, pushing him to talk. She records audio through her phone and deliberately challenges his self-image. - Sasha, frightened but conscious, manages to communicate that Finn is locked in a nearby equipment room. Gareth took him after catching the pair at the storage units. - The confrontation becomes chaotic when the school fundraising event spills outdoors: parents, teachers and students are released from a fire-alarm drill triggered by Finn, who has escaped or managed to activate an alarm. - Gareth tries to leave through the maintenance yard with the notebook. - Niamh blocks him long enough for Ms Bains, Niamh’s mother and police officers to arrive, guided by Niamh’s shared location and the earlier evidence. - The climax should be tense but darkly comic: Gareth slips in mud, a headteacher attempts crowd control with a megaphone, and the greenhouse’s automated sprinkler system activates at the worst possible moment. - Sasha is freed. Jayden, having attended the fundraiser with his family, sees Gareth being arrested and finally confirms what he was forced to do. - End reveal: as Gareth is taken away, he tells Niamh that she has no idea how many people knew enough to look away—leaving a trace of wider unease without requiring a larger conspiracy. ## Chapter 8: One Drop - Deal with the immediate aftermath: statements, police interviews, school assemblies and the uncomfortable transformation of Sasha’s disappearance into gossip. - The Bridge Project is suspended. Ms Bains admits she trusted Gareth because the programme made the school look supportive while reducing difficult cases. - Niamh’s parents react with anger and relief. Niamh has to face the fact that they did not know how frightened she was because she had spent years making herself seem invulnerable. - Sasha returns home and begins recovering. Her injuries are not severe, but the emotional aftermath is real. She is angry that Niamh did not come when she first texted, and Niamh does not defend herself. - Their reconciliation is cautious. Niamh apologises plainly; Sasha acknowledges that she used crisis as the only way she knew to ask for attention. - Finn continues to be irritating but becomes a genuine friend. His gossip account is shut down—or rebranded into something less harmful, such as documenting the school’s suspiciously bad safeguarding posters. - Niamh reflects that the town will eventually return to normal, because towns are very good at doing that around things they should remember. - Final scene: Niamh receives a text from Sasha asking if she can bring crisps and revision notes to her house. - A second message follows: **🩸** - Niamh stares at it, then receives: “That means urgent. Salt and vinegar. Don’t be dramatic.” - Niamh replies that one blood drop is an unacceptable system, but she goes anyway.
With a view to making the writing more human, discuss how a human might approach this particular piece (given the original prompt). Discuss telltale LLM approaches to writing (generally) and ways they might not serve this particular piece. For example, common LLM failings are to write safely, or to always wrap things up with a bow, or trying to write impressively at the expense of readability. Then do a deep dive on the intention & plan, critiquing ways it might be falling into typical LLM tropes & pitfalls. Brainstorm ideas to make it more human. Be comprehensive. We aren't doing any rewriting of the plan yet, just critique & brainstorming.
Model Output:
# How a Human Might Approach This Piece A human writer approaching this prompt would probably begin less with “What is the most efficient eight-chapter mystery?” and more with an instinctive image, memory, irritation, or voice. They might start with: - The specific nasty glow of a phone screen in a dark bedroom. - The embarrassment of receiving a dramatic text from someone you are sick of rescuing. - The peculiar British experience of standing outside a school in rain while everyone acts as though something serious is happening, but nobody quite does anything. - The way teenage friendships can feel more intense, more damaging and more intimate than most romances. - A town they remember: a dead shopping precinct, the smell of wet uniforms, a leisure centre that looks like it has survived several wars. - The private panic after dismissing a message and then realising it might have mattered. The human version would likely have rough edges. It might make choices that are not perfectly “balanced” or structurally tidy because they are emotionally true. Perhaps the missing friend is genuinely exhausting as well as vulnerable. Perhaps the protagonist is not secretly kinder than she thinks. Perhaps the adults are neither cartoonishly useless nor suddenly competent at the climax. Perhaps the villain is not a clearly designed embodiment of a theme, but someone oddly banal and socially believable. A human writer may also allow the story to spend time in moments that do not advance the plot cleanly but reveal character: Niamh listening to her mum put away washing while pretending she is asleep; Sasha’s room being messy in a way that feels recognisable rather than clue-filled; a bus journey that becomes memorable because the characters are terrified and still argue over whether one of them has stolen the other’s charger. Most importantly, a human writer would treat the initial message as an emotional wound, not simply a mechanism. The horror is not only “Sasha might be in danger.” It is that Niamh saw something that meant fear, chose not to respond, and has to live inside that choice. The story becomes distinctive when the mystery grows out of that emotional fact rather than merely using it as an inciting incident. # Telltale LLM Approaches to Writing ## 1. Over-optimised structure A common LLM tendency is to build stories in clean, textbook-shaped units: - Establish character flaw. - Introduce inciting incident. - Reveal clue. - Add complication. - Introduce false suspect. - Escalate danger. - Stage climax. - Deliver thematic resolution. This can be useful for planning, but it becomes obvious on the page when every chapter has a perfectly placed reversal and every event clearly performs a narrative function. Why it can fail: - Life does not always reveal itself in properly timed units. - The reader can feel the machinery. - Characters start behaving as clue-delivery systems. - A story may feel “competent” but strangely without atmosphere, surprise or personal risk. For this story, the current chapter plan is notably well-shaped—perhaps too well-shaped. Nearly every chapter ends with a neat new piece of evidence: a photo, metadata, a keyring, a storage unit, a text, a visual reveal. That is strong plotting, but if rendered mechanically it could read like a streaming drama outline rather than a lived-in YA story. ## 2. Making every supporting character functional LLM-generated characters often exist only to serve a plot purpose: - The friend gives the emotional stakes. - The sidekick gives banter and information. - The teacher represents failed institutions. - The parent represents emotional distance. - The villain represents the story’s social critique. - The younger student confirms the villain’s crimes. This makes the cast legible but thin. A more human story lets characters have desires and behaviour unrelated to the protagonist’s journey. A teacher may be annoying because she is trying to avoid being sued, but also genuinely lose sleep over students. A mother may not simply be “distracted by work”; she may know more about Niamh’s anxiety than Niamh realises. Finn may not be a convenient “gossip-account boy”; he may have a more uncomfortable reason for knowing everything, such as loneliness, social fear, or a need to be needed. ## 3. Safety and moral clarity LLMs often make characters morally legible: - The protagonist’s flaws are understandable and redeemable. - The victim is imperfect but ultimately sympathetic. - The villain is clearly wrong. - The adults who fail have understandable reasons. - The ending delivers justice and learning. This is “safe” because it avoids leaving readers with unresolved discomfort. But dark comedy often needs discomfort. It thrives on the fact that people can be both funny and awful, wounded and selfish, loving and neglectful. For this story, it may be more compelling if: - Niamh did not merely miss the text because she was tired. Perhaps she deliberately ignored it after a cruel argument. - Sasha may have lied to Niamh about something significant, even before the abduction. - Finn’s gossip account may have materially contributed to Sasha’s vulnerability. - Niamh’s parents may have cause to be angry, not just worried. - Gareth may not need a melodramatic confession to be frightening; he could insist he is helping right up until the end. The story should not become cynical for its own sake, but it should resist flattening the moral landscape into “good teenagers against bad adult.” ## 4. Impressive-sounding prose over actual voice LLM prose often tries too hard to sound literary. Symptoms include: - Metaphors in every paragraph. - Overly polished aphorisms. - Dramatic fragments used too frequently. - Generic sensory description: “rain lashed,” “shadows pooled,” “her heart hammered.” - Vague emotional statements presented as depth. - Repeated use of thematic motifs in a conspicuously designed way. The current plan contains some potentially artificial motifs: blood, rain, red hoodie, three drops, “Don’t be dramatic.” These can work, but they should arise naturally. If every scene contains red imagery and every character repeats the theme, it will feel manufactured. For a British YA comic voice, readability matters more than verbal fireworks. The prose should be specific, observant, quick and occasionally ugly. A funny detail about a school corridor may do more work than a paragraph of carefully crafted gothic weather. ## 5. Generic “Britishness” Another LLM risk is assembling Britishness from recognisable props: - Tesco. - Rain. - Tea. - Buses. - Council estates. - Ofsted. - Greggs. - Passive aggression. - Local Facebook groups. These can be funny, but if used as a checklist, they become tourist-board Britishness. A human writer is more likely to create a place through peculiar, unrepeatable details: the local bakery that sells pink icing doughnuts and nothing else after 3 p.m.; a roundabout mural everyone hates; an overly cheerful youth centre employee; a school rule that exists because of one incident involving a ferret in 2008. The plan’s “damp market town,” “retail park,” “school corridors,” “school safeguarding posters,” and “local Facebook group” are broadly evocative but still generic. The setting needs one or two strange, concrete realities that belong to this town alone. ## 6. “Cinematic” escalation rather than believable escalation LLM mysteries often rise in a smooth line: text → disappearance → clue → villain → secret base → kidnapping → rescue → arrest. This is satisfying, but it can make the plot too convenient. In the current plan: - The photo appears on Sasha’s phone. - The greenhouse clue points directly to Gareth. - Metadata points to the community centre. - Jayden conveniently provides the storage key. - The storage unit contains a complete body of evidence. - Gareth catches them. - Finn is kidnapped. - Niamh identifies the exact final location. - A climactic public event conveniently enables rescue and witnesses. That is a lot of perfectly connected machinery. It risks feeling “written” in the less flattering sense. A human approach might introduce wrong turns, muddled evidence and inconvenient coincidences. People misread things. Phones die. A clue proves emotionally useful but factually wrong. The protagonist discovers something not because the plot gives it to her, but because she knows Sasha’s habits or notices an odd detail no one else would care about. ## 7. Simplified institutions In fiction plans, schools, police and safeguarding systems are often presented in binary: - They dismiss the protagonist, then later help once they receive proof. - They are obstacles until the young protagonists have done enough to earn adult involvement. This is a familiar YA engine, but it can be simplistic. Real institutions are often simultaneously concerned, constrained, defensive, chaotic and unevenly competent. A teacher may report the concern quickly but be blocked by missing information. Police may act but cannot tell Niamh everything. Parents may be trying desperately while still making mistakes. The plan currently risks making adults incompetent mainly so Niamh can be heroic. A human version might make adult intervention messier and more consequential. Niamh could misinterpret an adult’s delay as indifference, then later discover they were working within rules she did not understand—or, conversely, discover that someone genuinely did choose reputation over safety. The point is specificity rather than blanket “adults fail teenagers.” ## 8. Over-neat emotional closure The final chapter currently gives: - Villain arrested. - Project suspended. - Teacher confesses institutional complacency. - Parents and Niamh reconcile. - Sasha and Niamh discuss their toxic dynamic. - Finn becomes a good friend. - A joke text restores warmth. This is satisfying but highly resolved for a story that has involved disappearance, captivity, coercion and betrayal. It risks putting a bow on trauma. The final joke with the blood emoji is clever, but it may feel too polished if Sasha has only just survived something frightening. A more human ending might contain warmth without total closure: - Sasha sends a normal text but neither of them knows how to speak normally yet. - Niamh goes to Sasha’s house and sits outside for ten minutes before knocking. - They make a terrible joke, then immediately regret it. - The police investigation is ongoing and public gossip has already distorted what happened. - Niamh receives no cathartic apology from adults. - Gareth’s arrest does not make Jayden suddenly safe or articulate. The emotional ending should feel earned in its scale. A small gesture can be more persuasive than a full explanatory conversation. # Deep Dive: Critique of the Current Intention ## The premise is strong, but currently too explained The core premise is effective: - A blood emoji text is vivid and instantly modern. - The sender is a friend the protagonist has become tired of rescuing. - The protagonist’s initial dismissal creates guilt. - The setting supports dark comedy. - The mystery has a personal engine rather than a random crime. However, the intention currently explains too much too early. It knows exactly what the emoji means, exactly what the youth project is doing, exactly how Sasha is trapped, exactly who the villain is, and exactly what the emotional lesson will be. That certainty is useful in a plan, but it can become restrictive. Human writing often benefits from allowing some discovery during drafting. The writer may learn that the real story is not “a girl rescues her friend,” but “a girl realises she has been using competence to avoid intimacy.” Or that Gareth is less interesting than Sasha’s home life. Or that Finn is too polished as a sidekick and needs to become more inconvenient. The intention currently has a strong thesis: “being responsible is not the same as being emotionally unavailable.” It is a good thematic direction, but it is close to an explicit moral. The story should dramatise it rather than repeatedly signal it. ## The villain is too designed Gareth Vale is currently: - Charismatic. - Respectable. - Runs a youth programme. - Collects safeguarding notes. - Coerces vulnerable teens. - Uses them for petty criminal tasks. - Has access to school grounds. - Gaslights Niamh. - Kidnaps both Sasha and Finn. - Eventually makes a chilling statement about how many people looked away. He is narratively useful, but he feels assembled from familiar thriller components. He may read as “the respectable predator with a community-facing role,” a type that is immediately suspicious to modern readers. The name “Gareth Vale” also has a slightly invented-antagonist feel: respectable first name, ominous surname. Ways this could become more human: - Make him less conspicuously villainous in public. Perhaps he is not charming in the obvious way; perhaps he is awkward, overworked, funny, and therefore trusted. - Give him a more particular relationship to the town: he coached Niamh’s cousin, helped get funding for the school, is beloved by parents because he drove kids home during lockdown. - Avoid making him explain himself at the climax. - Make his criminal conduct specific and messy rather than broad “coercive scheme.” One contained operation can feel more credible than an entire underground network. - Consider whether he is the sole villain at all. Perhaps someone else has enabled him knowingly, but not in a grand conspiracy way: a school administrator ignored irregularities because the programme brought funding. The story does not need sympathy for Gareth. It needs specificity. ## The youth-project exploitation plot risks tonal mismatch The plan wants dark comedy, but its antagonist plot involves vulnerable teenagers being coerced using safeguarding information. This is emotionally serious material. It can absolutely be written in YA crime fiction, but it changes the story’s tonal obligations. If the criminal scheme is too severe, then jokes about bad school posters, bus rides, sprinklers and the headteacher’s megaphone may feel jarring. The darker the exploitation, the more carefully comedy needs to be placed around it rather than inside it. Potential solutions: - Keep the harm real but narrow. Gareth has been coercing students into low-level identity fraud or parcel collection, and Sasha’s danger arises because she has obtained evidence. This avoids making the story about a vast ring of abuse. - Shift the scheme toward blackmail over school data, fake social accounts and reputational manipulation, which fits the phone-message premise and high-school setting. - Make Sasha’s confinement short and opportunistic, not a prolonged captivity situation. That keeps urgency high without forcing the story into an emotional register too grim for the comic frame. - Focus the threat on coercive control and the fear of not being believed, rather than graphic danger. ## Niamh is at risk of being a “competent, sarcastic protagonist template” Niamh is currently: - Smart. - Controlled. - High-achieving. - Over-responsible. - Dryly funny. - Emotionally guarded. - More capable than adults realise. - Secretly in need of learning vulnerability. This is a very workable YA protagonist, but it is also a familiar one. She risks becoming a polished “sensible girl with hidden feelings” archetype. To make her feel more individual: - Give her an embarrassing obsession or irrational prejudice that has nothing to do with the plot. - Make her sense of humour slightly idiosyncratic rather than generic sarcasm. Perhaps she is obsessed with bad local planning decisions, writes fake obituary notices for school equipment, or has an internal taxonomy of public humiliations. - Give her a flaw that is less flattering than “cares too much.” For example, she enjoys feeling morally superior to Sasha’s mess; she uses private information to win arguments; she has been quietly relieved that Sasha’s social chaos made Niamh look stable. - Let her make a mistake in the investigation that has consequences beyond temporary danger. - Avoid revealing that her parents simply “didn’t know” her; perhaps she has actively trained them not to ask. A human protagonist can be difficult without becoming unlikeable. In fact, the story may gain energy if Niamh is sometimes petty, wrong and funny in ways that make the reader wince. ## Sasha risks being absent and symbolic Sasha drives the plot but is missing for most of it. The plan describes her as “chaotic,” “erratic,” “dramatic,” and vulnerable. That is potentially dangerous because she could become a device: the messy friend whose suffering teaches the responsible friend a lesson. To prevent this: - Give Sasha a strong presence before she disappears. The opening could include a recent argument, old voice notes, a remembered incident, or a physical object that reveals her humour and intelligence. - Let the reader see evidence of her agency. She has investigated Gareth, hidden material, invented the code, protected Jayden, and chosen Niamh for a reason. - Make her not entirely right. Maybe she made a reckless choice in confronting Gareth alone. Maybe she hid evidence because she did not trust anyone, including Niamh. - Avoid having her deliver a tidy confession about “using crisis as the only way I knew to ask for attention.” This sounds like a therapeutic-summary line rather than something a frightened seventeen-year-old would naturally say. - Allow her anger to remain unresolved at the end. Niamh’s apology should not automatically produce forgiveness. The emotional story becomes much stronger if Sasha remains a full character rather than a person to be found. ## Finn is too convenient and too cleanly non-romantic The plan explicitly says Finn is “not a romantic lead,” likely to avoid derailing the friendship story. That is a sensible intention. However, declaring him non-romantic in the plan may indicate an LLM-style attempt to pre-empt every familiar genre problem. The actual issue is not whether romance occurs; it is whether Finn feels real. At present, he: - Runs a gossip account. - Knows useful things. - Is socially observant. - Provides comic contrast. - Helps investigate. - Gets kidnapped to raise stakes. - Escapes or triggers an alarm. - Becomes a genuine friend. That is very functional. He is a mystery-sidekick toolkit. Ways to complicate him: - The gossip account could have been cruel to Sasha. He is helping partly out of guilt. - He might be less brave than Niamh expects, and his fear could be funny and genuine. - He may not want to investigate at all; Niamh blackmails or pressures him into it. - His information network could consist not of cool insight, but of being ignored by people who assume he is harmless. - He could lie about why he was at the greenhouse. - His relationship with Sasha could be unexpected: perhaps she once protected him, perhaps she despises him, perhaps they had a private alliance Niamh knew nothing about. Avoid making him an endlessly witty, available, hyper-competent sidekick. That is a familiar formula. ## The setting needs a singular identity The plan currently has a general “British YA” texture, but not enough local strangeness. “A damp market town” can be anywhere. The danger is that it becomes a montage of British signifiers. Brainstorming ways to make it feel inhabited: - The town has a failed regeneration project: a glass-fronted “Riverside Quarter” with only a vape shop, a charity shop and an estate agent still operating. - The school was built around an old grammar-school wing, and pupils constantly get lost in the newer block because the corridors have been renamed after donors. - The greenhouse was once part of an ambitious eco-scheme launched by a local MP and abandoned after a photo-op. - The town’s annual event is not folklore but something mundanely ridiculous: a scarecrow trail, a “Canal Day,” a Christmas lights switch-on hosted by a former soap actor. - The community centre has a specific smell: bleach, wet coats, microwave noodles and ancient carpet. - A local landmark has personal relevance to Niamh and Sasha, not just atmospheric value. - The school has one infamous staff member or rule that becomes part of the plot in an incidental, lived-in way. These details should not be inserted simply to prove Britishness. They should shape what characters notice and how they move through the town. # Deep Dive: Critique of the Chapter Plan ## Chapter 1: Strong hook, but the code may be too explicit The midnight text is excellent. The delayed recognition of an old code is emotionally potent. However, the exact original code—“COME ALONE. DON’T TELL ANYONE. I’M SCARED.”—is extremely convenient and very expository. Potential issues: - It makes the mystery too immediately literal. - It explains the significance too cleanly. - “Come alone. Don’t tell anyone” is the sort of instruction that makes readers ask why the protagonist would obey it after someone disappears. - It risks making Sasha’s message feel designed by the author rather than invented by children. More human possibilities: - The three drops had a more childish, less direct meaning: “red alert,” “no parents,” “bring plasters,” “I’ve done something bad,” or “meet at the third place.” - Niamh and Sasha invented it during a very specific childhood incident, such as hiding after breaking into an abandoned building or dealing with Sasha’s dad’s drunken outbursts. Its meaning has changed over time. - Niamh misremembers what it means. That ambiguity can create guilt and tension. - The code could initially mean something embarrassingly mundane, making Niamh’s dismissal more plausible. Also, Niamh’s dismissive reply is good, but it may be more emotionally effective if it follows a recent fight. That would make her choice not simply fatigue but anger. ## Chapter 2: The adults may be too conveniently dismissive The “probably fine” chapter is a familiar missing-person beat. Sasha’s mother lying about an aunt is plausible, but the reason needs to be precise. Is she protecting Sasha’s reputation? Is she afraid of Gareth? Does she think Sasha has run away? Is she being coerced? If she is simply lying to create mystery, it will feel artificial. Similarly: - A pastoral lead offering a cereal bar is a good comic detail. - But if a student is missing and a parent is contacted, the school should plausibly do something. - The story can show Niamh’s frustration without making every adult absurdly negligent. A more nuanced version might show: - The school knows Sasha is absent but assumes her parent has authorised it. - Niamh does not share the bloody text at first because she fears being blamed for ignoring it. - The pastoral lead takes her seriously but cannot act on a vague message alone. - Sasha’s mum is frantic, but tells Niamh the aunt lie because she is following advice from someone she trusts. - Niamh mistakes adults’ secrecy for indifference. That gives the story more moral complexity and preserves Niamh’s agency. ## Chapter 3: Too much evidence in one location The greenhouse currently provides: - Recent use. - Energy-drink cans. - Burner-phone box. - Hoodie scrap. - Blood. - Gareth physically appearing. - A voice note. - Metadata for the next location. This is a classic clue-dump. It efficiently advances the plot but may feel implausible. A criminal who is careful enough to coerce students and control them may not leave a complete trail of incriminating items in one accessible greenhouse. Ways to roughen it: - The hoodie scrap could be a false lead, planted or explainable. - The blood could later turn out to be from a mundane injury, making Niamh’s fear feel more subjective. - Gareth’s presence could be innocuous at first: he is collecting donated tools, not obviously skulking. - The greenhouse clue may not reveal a crime; it reveals that Sasha was there recently. - The voice note could be corrupted, accidental or from a different time. - The metadata may be unreliable, which gives the pair a wrong turn. A human mystery often gains credibility when clues are partial and interpretation matters. ## Chapter 4: Gareth is immediately suspicious The Bridge Project scene gives Gareth a polished villain presentation: wholesome setting, vague concern, gaslighting, confidential folders, coded task lists, confrontation. He effectively announces himself as the bad guy. This lowers mystery tension. If readers know Gareth is guilty by Chapter 4, the remaining story becomes a straight rescue operation. That can work, but then the story should lean more into danger and emotion rather than detective plotting. Alternatives: - Make Gareth plausibly innocent for longer. The evidence might point to another staff member or former volunteer. - Let the project contain genuinely good people and outcomes, complicating Niamh’s certainty. - Have Gareth be suspicious for reasons unrelated to the actual crime. - Give the folders a legitimate reason to exist, so Niamh’s discovery is not immediate proof. - Let Niamh make an unfair assumption because she dislikes the project’s language or suspects adults who are too friendly. The keyring note is another highly convenient hand-off. Jayden could instead offer a strange clue that only later becomes meaningful, or he could refuse to help until Niamh earns his trust. ## Chapter 5: The storage unit is a thriller shortcut Storage units are common in modern crime plotting because they are convenient containers for evidence and danger. They can easily feel TV-derived. The unit currently contains: - Cheap phones. - School records. - Fake ID materials. - Bags with student names. - Sasha’s notebook. - A direct partial clue. - A confrontation with Gareth. This is almost a villain’s evidence museum. It removes the need for inference. Potentially more human: - The unit is disappointing at first: boxes of donated equipment, old flyers, broken laptops. - One mundane item is wrong in a way only Niamh notices. - Sasha’s notebook is not openly there; perhaps it is hidden in a bag of compost or inside an old school play script. - The “evidence” is ambiguous enough that Niamh cannot simply hand it over and be believed. - Gareth does not conveniently arrive; the danger arises because Finn posts or messages something careless, or because Niamh triggers an alarm. The chapter’s emotional centre could be Niamh deciding to lie to her parents, which is stronger if the lie is not merely a standard YA rebellion beat but a betrayal of her self-image as the responsible daughter. ## Chapter 6: The kidnapping escalation is potentially melodramatic Gareth taking Finn after catching him may be an escalation too far. It risks making Gareth unusually bold and impractical. A man running a covert operation based on manipulation is unlikely to improve his situation by abducting multiple teenagers. The “come back alone” text also mirrors the original code very directly. This is structurally satisfying but may be overly symmetrical. Alternative stakes: - Finn is not kidnapped but goes missing because he panics, runs, gets injured, or returns to the school alone. - Gareth has Finn’s phone and uses it to lure Niamh, but Finn is hidden nearby rather than held captive. - Finn has betrayed Niamh in some way, perhaps under pressure, creating an emotional rather than purely physical complication. - Niamh gets the text from Sasha’s phone, but it is an automated scheduled message, making her question the timeline. The moment where Niamh tells her mother is important. It should not be too cleanly cathartic. Her mother might react with disbelief, anger, practical action and a hurt sense that Niamh did not trust her. Niamh may have to accept that involving an adult means losing control of the story. ## Chapter 7: The climax has too many designed elements The greenhouse confrontation includes: - Niamh carrying the notebook. - Pretending to come alone. - Recording Gareth. - Sasha communicating Finn’s location. - A school fundraiser. - A fire alarm. - Finn escaping. - A crowd spilling outside. - Police arriving. - Niamh blocking Gareth. - Mud slip. - Megaphone. - Sprinklers. - Jayden witnessing arrest. - Gareth delivering a thematic final warning. This is busy enough to become farce. Dark comedy can use chaos, but every element must not compete for attention. The danger is that the climax turns into “everything happens at once because endings need spectacle.” A human approach might choose one or two comic intrusions and let the rest stay frightening. For example: - The school fundraiser is happening nearby, and Niamh can hear a bad cover band through the glass. - The headteacher’s attempt to keep the event calm is absurd. - But there is no sprinkler gag, no mud slip, no extended villain speech. - The rescue is ugly, confusing and not especially cinematic. Also, the plan gives Niamh a clever recording strategy and a successful public rescue, which can make her feel unrealistically composed. She might be brave but frightened, make mistakes, freeze, say the wrong thing, or fail to record anything useful. The rescue could depend on a less heroic but more human action: she calls loudly for Sasha, breaks a glass panel, refuses to leave, or tells the truth in front of people who would rather not hear it. ## Chapter 8: The neatness needs breaking The last chapter is thoughtful, but it currently lists all the right consequences. It reads like a postscript designed to assure the reader that every thematic thread was addressed. The specific line about Sasha using crisis “as the only way she knew to ask for attention” is particularly likely to sound scripted. It reduces a complex friendship to a therapeutic slogan. A more human ending could: - Keep the girls’ conversation shorter, more defensive and unfinished. - Show care through action rather than diagnosis. - Let Niamh apologise without receiving a clear answer. - Let Sasha make a cruel joke because she cannot bear seriousness. - Let Finn’s role remain awkward; perhaps his gossip account has consequences he cannot shrug off. - Let the school issue an awful email that sanitises the whole incident, giving the story a final bite. - End with the ordinary text from Sasha but make the blood emoji’s use uneasy rather than fully reclaimed. The existing final exchange has warmth and a recognisable YA rhythm. It could work, but it should avoid turning trauma into a neatly reusable in-joke too soon. # Brainstorming Ways to Make the Story More Human ## Make the initial text emotionally messier Instead of Niamh simply being tired of Sasha’s drama, give the two girls a sharp recent rupture. Possible versions: - Niamh has accused Sasha of lying about something serious. - Sasha exposed a private fact about Niamh in anger. - Niamh has deliberately muted Sasha’s chat. - Sasha sent several messages before the emojis, but Niamh deletes them unread. - Niamh recognises the code and deliberately chooses not to answer because she thinks Sasha is manipulating her. This makes the guilt more uncomfortable and less conventionally sympathetic. ## Let Sasha be strange, funny and difficult on the page Ways to make Sasha present despite being absent: - Her voice notes are funny, overlong and full of odd local observations. - She leaves terrible labels on objects in her bedroom. - She has an obscure fixation, perhaps old game-show clips, crime scene photography, karaoke, bargain-bin perfume, abandoned buildings, or conspiracy podcasts. - Niamh finds evidence of a side of Sasha she did not know: careful records, a protective relationship with Jayden, secret work at the community centre. - Sasha’s investigation is clever but flawed, not just brave. The reader should miss Sasha, not merely fear for her. ## Give Niamh a less flattering private life Possible complications: - Niamh has been quietly monitoring Sasha’s location or social media, then feels guilty about it. - She has used Sasha’s unreliability as an excuse to avoid her own loneliness. - She has a private academic misconduct problem: plagiarism, leaked revision materials, a competitive obsession. - She has become close to someone Sasha dislikes, which contributed to their split. - She is not universally “the sensible one”; she is known at school for being intimidating, cold or weirdly competitive. - She cannot stand bodily mess, making the blood emojis and evidence physically difficult for her. These do not need to become extra plots. They can create texture and make her decisions less predictable. ## Make Finn inconvenient Instead of the ideal bantering ally: - Finn has posted a rumour about Sasha in the past. - He believes the blood emojis are a stunt until he sees evidence. - He is funny because he is panicking, not because he always has a line. - He may be hiding that he was involved in The Bridge Project. - He knows Gareth because Gareth once helped his family, making him reluctant to accuse him. - He has a practical weakness: cannot drive, gets migraines, freezes in confrontation, talks too much when afraid. - His investigative skill comes from a morally questionable habit—screenshots, fake accounts, lurking in group chats—which creates consequences. ## Complicate the adult world Potential adult portraits: - Ms Bains is genuinely trying but has too many students and too little authority. Her failure is not indifference; it is overload and institutional limits. - Niamh’s mum works in a field where she has seen real emergencies, so she initially mistakes Niamh’s fear for ordinary teenage panic—then is horrified by that mistake. - Sasha’s mum knows more than she says because Gareth has helped her with housing paperwork, debt advice or food parcels. Her silence has a cost. - The headteacher cares primarily about reputation, but has also received threats about funding. - A police officer is kind but irritatingly procedural, while a local PCSO is more attuned to the kids and spots the emotional truth. This avoids the blanket “adults are useless” trope while preserving the young characters’ isolation. ## Make the evidence less clean Ideas: - The blood is from Sasha, but only because she cut herself climbing a fence. The true danger is elsewhere. - The blood is not Sasha’s at all. - The greenhouse photo was sent before Sasha was taken, not from captivity. - The phone metadata is misleading because the message was uploaded later. - Jayden’s clue is incomplete or initially misread. - Sasha has staged part of her disappearance to draw Gareth out, without telling Niamh. - The storage unit contains evidence that can be interpreted as legitimate youth-project administration unless paired with something else. - A key clue comes from a personal detail: Sasha’s habit of writing dates backward, a song lyric she repeats, a joke only Niamh understands. The emotional relationship should solve the mystery at least as much as physical evidence does. ## Use dark comedy from pressure, not gags The story can be funny because people remain themselves under stress. Examples: - Niamh’s mum insists she take a coat during an emergency because “you’ll catch your death,” which is ridiculous but loving. - Finn brings the wrong tool for breaking into somewhere because he has watched too many crime documentaries. - A school receptionist insists on visitor badges during a crisis. - A public-address announcement interrupts a tense moment with something banal: “Could whoever has taken the Year 8 hockey bibs please return them to PE.” - Niamh and Finn argue over wording while trying to send an emergency text. - The town’s Facebook group identifies the wrong suspect with extraordinary confidence. - The only person who has seen something important is a child who refuses to talk until someone buys them chips. The comedy should emerge from character, environment and bureaucratic absurdity—not from treating immediate danger as slapstick. ## Consider a less triumphant ending Potential final images: - Niamh stands outside Sasha’s house with a packet of crisps, unable to knock. - Sasha sends a single thumbs-up, not the emotional conversation Niamh wants. - The school circulates a bland email about “a recent safeguarding matter,” and Niamh deletes it in disgust. - Finn’s old gossip posts are still being shared, and he realises an apology does not erase them. - Niamh receives a blood emoji from Sasha, but this time she calls immediately. Sasha does not answer. Then, after a terrible pause, sends: “Sorry. Cut my thumb opening a tin.” - The girls sit in silence watching awful television, their repaired friendship communicated through proximity rather than a speech. These endings preserve hope while refusing perfect closure. # Key Questions to Carry Into the Next Planning Stage - What does the three-blood code mean emotionally, not just functionally? - What exactly happened between Niamh and Sasha before the text, and how does that make Niamh’s choice difficult rather than merely careless? - What does Sasha want besides rescue? - What does Niamh do that is genuinely ugly, selfish or mistaken? - Why does Finn help, and what has he done that makes him morally compromised? - What does Gareth gain from his scheme, and is it specific enough to feel real? - Which adults are constrained, which are complicit, and which are simply wrong? - Which clues come from plot convenience, and which come from the girls’ shared history? - What is funny because it is true to the setting and characters, rather than funny because the plan demands comic relief? - What remains unresolved at the end—and how can that unresolved element make the final note feel more honest rather than incomplete?
Ok now with these considerations in mind, formulate the final plan for the a humanlike, compelling short piece in {n_chapters} chapters. Bear in mind the constraints of the piece (each chapter is just 1000 words). Above all things, the plan must serve the original prompt. We will use the same format as before: # Intention <State your formulated intentions for the piece, synthesised from the the parts of the brainstorming session that worked, and avoiding the parts that didn't. Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This will be an approximately 8,000-word darkly comic British YA mystery, told in close first person by **Niamh Patel**, seventeen, who considers herself practical, observant and above other people’s mess. In reality, she is frightened of needing people and privately enjoys the moral superiority of being the sensible friend beside Sasha Reed’s chaos. The story begins with the entire force of the original prompt: > The text came just after midnight: 🩸🩸🩸 The message is from Sasha, Niamh’s former best friend. The three blood drops are an old code the girls made up at twelve, after Sasha cut herself climbing through the fence behind the abandoned garden centre: **red alert—come, don’t ask questions, don’t bring adults.** It was childish, melodramatic and, in their friendship, used too often. Niamh sees it, understands it, and consciously does not go. This choice is complicated by a recent argument. Sasha has accused Niamh of treating her like a broken appliance: something to be fixed, unplugged or complained about. Niamh has accused Sasha of manufacturing emergencies because ordinary affection is not enough for her. Both have said cruel things. Niamh has muted Sasha’s chat. When the message arrives, she thinks Sasha is attempting to win the argument by making herself impossible to ignore. By morning, Sasha is missing. The mystery will remain contained and credible. It will not involve a large criminal network or a grand conspiracy. Sasha has been volunteering at **The Bridge**, a community youth project operating from a converted church hall. The project has real benefits and many decent volunteers, but its manager, **Gareth Vale**, has been using a small number of vulnerable teenagers to carry out low-level fraud: collecting bank cards and phones, creating fake accounts, and receiving parcels for a local scammer. He gains access to teenagers’ personal pressures through the project’s safeguarding work, then presents his manipulation as practical help. Sasha discovered that Gareth had pressured a younger boy, **Jayden**, into collecting a package and had threatened to expose information about Jayden’s family if he refused. Sasha stole Gareth’s notebook and tried to arrange a meeting with Niamh. Gareth intercepted her. He does not run a dungeon or lead a cinematic criminal empire; he panics, takes Sasha to an unused potting shed on school grounds, and tries to retrieve what she took while buying time to remove evidence. The danger comes from his entitlement, his ability to appear respectable, and the fact that he expects young people not to be believed. The story’s emotional centre is not “Niamh becomes more caring.” It is that Niamh learns she cannot treat care as a transaction in which she only helps people who ask perfectly, calmly and conveniently. At the same time, Sasha is not romanticised. She is funny, brave, reckless and exhausting; she has made Niamh responsible for too much, and Niamh is allowed to be angry about that. Their friendship will not be fully repaired by the ending. The rescue creates an opening for honesty, not a magical restoration. **Finn Mercer** will be Niamh’s uneasy ally. He runs an anonymous school gossip account, partly because he is lonely and partly because watching other people is easier than being noticed himself. He previously posted something cruel about Sasha, and he is helping partly from guilt. He is not a romantic lead and will not become a slick comic sidekick. He is useful because he knows who attends The Bridge and because people speak freely around him, assuming he is harmless. He is also anxious, occasionally cowardly, and prone to making bad decisions under pressure. The adult world will be mixed rather than uniformly useless: - Niamh’s mother, a pharmacy technician who works irregular shifts, is loving but has accepted Niamh’s performance of competence too easily. - Ms Bains, the school safeguarding lead, is overworked and procedural but takes Niamh seriously once she knows the facts. - Sasha’s mum is frightened and evasive because Gareth has helped her access food-bank vouchers and housing advice; she is ashamed of how dependent she has become on him. - The headteacher is overly concerned about reputation, but not a cartoon villain. - Police involvement will be present but limited by timing, incomplete evidence and the speed of events. The British setting will be specific rather than assembled from generic props. The town, **Mereford**, has a failed riverside regeneration scheme called The Quays: glazed shop units mostly empty except for a vape shop, a nail bar and a charity furniture warehouse. The school’s greenhouse was part of a long-abandoned “Grow the Future” initiative, launched by a local MP and now used for broken furniture, compost and equipment no department will claim. The Bridge operates out of a converted Methodist hall that smells of wet coats, instant coffee and old carpet. These locations will shape the story rather than merely decorate it. The voice will be sharp, grounded and readable.
