Niamh’s narration will be funny because she notices petty absurdities even while afraid: safeguarding posters with smiling stock-photo teenagers; the town Facebook group identifying the wrong suspect; adults saying “let’s not catastrophise” while holding emergency meetings. The prose will not chase literary grandeur. It will use occasional vivid images, but favour precise, concrete observations over generic thriller language. The humour will never come from Sasha’s captivity, Jayden’s fear or the coercion itself. It will come from bureaucracy, awkwardness, character habits, failed attempts at normality and the way ordinary British life persists around danger. The story will avoid: - A perfectly assembled chain of clues. - A villain who explains every motive in a climactic speech. - Adults who are uniformly foolish. - A hero who solves everything alone. - A climactic pile-up of convenient gadgets, alarms, police and comic mishaps. - Total emotional closure. - A final joke that makes trauma feel tidily reclaimed. The ending will be hopeful but unfinished. Gareth will be stopped because several people act imperfectly but together: Sasha’s preparation, Niamh’s knowledge of her, Finn’s evidence, Jayden’s courage, Ms Bains’s response and Niamh finally telling her mother the truth. Niamh will apologise to Sasha without demanding forgiveness. The final text will echo the opening, but in a way that shows Niamh has changed: when Sasha sends a message, Niamh responds immediately—not because every message signals catastrophe, but because she has learned not to decide in advance that someone’s fear is inconvenient. # Chapter Planning ## Chapter 1: Red Alert - Open immediately with Niamh’s phone lighting up at 12:03 a.m.: **🩸🩸🩸** - She has muted Sasha’s chat after their argument earlier that day. The message appears only because emergency notifications override the mute. - Briefly establish their old code: three blood drops meant “red alert.” It began as a joke after a childhood trespass that ended with Sasha bleeding and both girls too frightened to tell their parents. - Niamh is angry. Sasha has recently been erratic, cancelling plans, asking for help and then rejecting it, becoming involved with The Bridge and insisting she has “something real” to show Niamh. - Niamh writes: *Are you actually hurt?* Then deletes it. She sends: *If this is another emergency, try an adult for once.* - Sasha does not answer. - The following morning, Niamh notices Sasha is absent from college. The absence is unsettling not because Sasha never misses school, but because Sasha has left a presentation half-finished and has not posted anywhere online. - Niamh receives a curt message from Sasha’s mum saying Sasha is “with family” and needs space. - At school, Finn casually mentions Sasha had been at The Bridge every evening that week and had been asking strange questions about “people who collect things for other people.” - Niamh dismisses him, then checks the old chat. Sasha’s last typed message, unsent or cut off, reads: **“I’ve got it. Meet me at—”** - Chapter ends with Niamh seeing that the blood emoji text was sent from a location pin she never opened. ## Chapter 2: The Place Nobody Uses - Niamh opens the location pin: it identifies the old greenhouse behind the school sports hall. - She tells herself it could be a stupid joke, but goes there after lessons. Finn follows because he saw her leave and assumes, correctly, that she is about to do something unwise. - Their dynamic is tense. Niamh knows Finn’s anonymous account posted a rumour about Sasha months ago; Finn claims he deleted it, which is not the same as never posting it. - The greenhouse is derelict but not spooky in a polished way: cracked panes, dead tomato vines, rainwater buckets, dusty “Grow the Future” posters, bags of compost with mice in them. - They find evidence Sasha was there: a coffee cup with her particular aggressive handwriting on it, a torn page from her notebook, and a small smear of blood on a broken pane. - The notebook page contains half a list of names, including Jayden’s, and the phrase: **“G.V. knows too much.”** - They hear someone using the nearby potting shed. Rather than a clear villain reveal, they glimpse a figure moving equipment and hear a van door slam. By the time they look, the person has gone. - Niamh tries calling Sasha’s mum. Sasha’s mum finally admits Sasha did not go to family. She came home frightened, said someone was “sorting it,” then left again. - Niamh reports the text and the greenhouse clue to Ms Bains. Ms Bains listens, photographs the notebook page, calls Sasha’s mother and begins making safeguarding reports. - Niamh feels impatient with the process, but is shaken when Ms Bains says quietly, “You did the right thing telling me now.” - End beat: Jayden, who has overheard part of the conversation, slips past Niamh and whispers, “Don’t tell Mr Vale I saw you.” ## Chapter 3: The Bridge - Niamh and Finn visit The Bridge the next afternoon under the pretence of asking about volunteering opportunities. - Establish the converted Methodist hall: cheerful murals, free biscuits, donated coats, wellbeing leaflets and a smell of wet wool and instant coffee. It is not sinister on the surface; teenagers genuinely use it and some clearly benefit from it. - Gareth Vale is introduced as ordinary rather than theatrical: polite, tired-looking, capable of remembering names, mildly funny with adults. His apparent normality unsettles Niamh more than obvious creepiness would. - Gareth says Sasha has been stressed and “got involved in something she didn’t understand.” He suggests she may have run away because of trouble at home. - Niamh notices Jayden avoiding Gareth and sees Gareth gently but firmly take Jayden’s phone “to help him stay focused.” - Finn distracts a volunteer by asking intrusive questions about the snacks. Niamh sees an open drawer in Gareth’s office containing project paperwork and a sheet of young people’s names marked with practical notes: “phone,” “mum nights,” “needs cash,” “easy to frighten.” - Before she can photograph it, Gareth returns. Niamh lies badly about looking for a toilet. - Outside, Finn admits he has seen Gareth’s van at the school greenhouse before, but did not think it mattered because The Bridge officially runs a gardening scheme. - Jayden does not agree to speak, but drops a crumpled bus ticket at Niamh’s feet. On the back is written: **Quays, Unit 14. Ask for red bag.** - End beat: Niamh gets home to find her mother waiting. Ms Bains has called. Niamh has to admit she received Sasha’s message and ignored it. ## Chapter 4: Unit 14 - Niamh’s mother is frightened and angry. Rather than a clean heart-to-heart, the conversation is awkward: Niamh expects a lecture; her mother asks why she did not wake her; Niamh says because her mum was working; her mum says that is not the same as being unavailable. - Niamh is forbidden from investigating further. She agrees, then immediately messages Finn, because she is seventeen and terrified and thinks disobedience is the only form of agency left to her. - They go to The Quays in daylight, not at night: empty glass units, rain blowing under the covered walkway, a charity warehouse, delivery vans and a youth dance studio. - Unit 14 is not a dramatic storage lock-up. It is an unmarked back room behind a discount furniture warehouse, used for sorting donations. - They find nothing obviously criminal at first. There are boxes of old phones, laptops awaiting repair and bags of donated clothing—plausible materials for a youth project. - Niamh notices a red school backpack under a table: Sasha’s. Inside is a packet of plasters, a broken nail file, and a folded page from her notebook. - The note is written in Sasha’s messy shorthand: **“Not greenhouse. He uses it to scare them. Real stuff moves through Quays. Jayden made collection. If I don’t come back, tell Niamh I was right.”** - Finn discovers a burner phone taped beneath the table. It contains messages arranging collections but no names, only initials and locations. - A warehouse worker enters. He is not Gareth; he is confused, angry and says they should not be there. He recognises Gareth’s name but thinks he is collecting donated devices for The Bridge. - Niamh and Finn leave before they can be caught trespassing, carrying only photographs and Sasha’s note. - End beat: Niamh receives a call from Sasha’s number. She hears Sasha breathing and then a scrape, but no words. A man says, off-phone, “You can stop making this difficult.” The call ends. ## Chapter 5: What Counts as Proof - Niamh takes the photos and burner-phone evidence to Ms Bains. The school and police now have enough to take the situation seriously, but Niamh discovers “seriously” still involves interviews, calls and waiting. - Jayden is collected from school by an aunt after Ms Bains contacts his family. He initially refuses to say anything, terrified that Gareth will tell people private details about his mum’s debt and his brother’s arrest. - Finn admits the gossip account posted something about Jayden’s family weeks earlier. He claims he did not know it came from Gareth, but Niamh is furious: he helped create the exact fear Gareth exploits. - Their partnership fractures. Finn leaves, saying Niamh only wants him around when he is useful. - Niamh visits Sasha’s house with permission from Sasha’s mum. The house is tense, half-cleaned as though tidiness could solve anything. - Sasha’s mum admits Gareth helped her apply for emergency support after her hours were cut. He knew about Sasha’s difficult home situation because Sasha had spoken to him at The Bridge. Sasha’s mum did not trust him exactly, but she trusted the help. - In Sasha’s room, Niamh finds a childhood tin of “emergency supplies”: cheap torch, old phone charger, plasters, silly codes written on the lid. Inside is a new clue Sasha hid for Niamh: a key to the greenhouse potting shed, tied to a tag reading **NOT THE GREENHOUSE. THE SHED.** - Niamh realises Sasha anticipated that Gareth might use the greenhouse as intimidation and concealment rather than as the real base of his operation. - End beat: a new message arrives from Sasha’s number: **🩸🩸🩸**. This time it includes a blurry photograph of wet greenhouse glass from inside. ## Chapter 6: Come Anyway - Niamh calls Ms Bains and her mother immediately. This is the behavioural change from Chapter 1: she does not dismiss, delay or handle it alone. - The image is enough to confirm Sasha is likely on school grounds, but police are still en route and the greenhouse is locked behind the maintenance yard. - Niamh’s mother insists they wait. Niamh agrees verbally, then sees that the photo has a timestamp from several minutes earlier and panics that Gareth will move Sasha. - Finn appears outside Niamh’s house. He has brought a screenshot archive from the gossip account, having realised Gareth fed rumours through anonymous messages to isolate particular students. He apologises without asking to be forgiven. - Niamh’s mother, furious but realistic, drives them to the school rather than letting them go alone. This preserves adult involvement while maintaining immediate tension. - At the school, a parents’ evening is underway in the main hall. The car park is full, lights are on, and nobody notices three teenagers and one furious mother slipping around the side. - The potting-shed key works, but the shed is empty except for Sasha’s phone taped beneath a bench and a fresh smear of blood. - On the phone is a draft voice note from Sasha. She says Gareth has taken her “to the place with the red bags,” then the recording cuts out. - Niamh remembers the Quays red bag clue and realises the furniture warehouse has a delivery loading bay that connects to Unit 14. - End beat: as they turn to leave, Gareth steps out from behind the greenhouse. He has followed them, and he says Niamh’s name with exhausted disappointment rather than overt menace. ## Chapter 7: The Loading Bay - Gareth tries to keep control through calmness. He says Sasha is “safe,” that Niamh has misunderstood a difficult safeguarding situation, and that involving police will ruin lives—including Jayden’s. - Niamh’s mother does not debate him; she calls emergency services openly, putting the phone on speaker. Gareth realises he cannot talk his way out cleanly. - He leaves rather than attacking them, driving off toward The Quays. Niamh, her mother and Finn follow at a distance while updating police. This is reckless but not secret; Niamh’s mother is part of the decision. - At The Quays, the furniture warehouse is closing. The loading bay is noisy, wet and lit by flickering security lights. A delivery lorry is due to leave. - They find Sasha locked in the back office, not dramatically bound but trapped behind a jammed fire door with Gareth’s van blocking the external exit. She has a cut hand from forcing the greenhouse glass and is dehydrated, frightened and furious. - Gareth has been trying to retrieve the notebook and destroy the devices before police arrive. He attempts to leave through the loading bay with a box of phones. - The confrontation is messy rather than choreographed. Niamh shouts; Finn pulls the fire alarm; a warehouse manager finally realises something is wrong; Gareth tries to claim Sasha is a troubled volunteer. Jayden’s aunt, who has been contacted by Ms Bains, arrives with Jayden after he insists on coming. - Jayden, shaking, says clearly that Gareth made him collect packages and threatened his family. This does not solve everything, but breaks Gareth’s control over the story. - Police arrive as Gareth is prevented from leaving by warehouse staff and the blocked delivery van. There is no grand confession. Gareth keeps insisting it is all a misunderstanding. - End beat: Niamh reaches Sasha. Sasha’s first words are not gratitude but: “You took your time.” ## Chapter 8: One Reply - Cover the immediate aftermath in fragments rather than a comprehensive wrap-up: police statements, a school email full of bland phrases, whispered corridor gossip, The Bridge temporarily closed, and the town Facebook group confidently getting every detail wrong. - The story acknowledges that some volunteers at The Bridge were doing real good, which makes Gareth’s exploitation more complicated and upsetting. The institution was not fake; its trust was used. - Finn’s gossip account is identified during the investigation. He has to shut it down and face the fact that deleting posts is not the same as undoing them. Niamh does not comfort him neatly, but she does not abandon him either. - Niamh’s mother is quieter after the crisis. She apologises for mistaking Niamh’s competence for independence. Niamh does not instantly become open, but tells her about the original text and the recent argument. - Niamh visits Sasha at home. Their conversation is hesitant, funny in places and not a full reconciliation. - Niamh apologises specifically for seeing the message and choosing not to come. - Sasha says Niamh was right that she cannot keep making every bad feeling an emergency. - Niamh says that does not make it acceptable to ignore a red alert. - Neither girl promises perfection. - Sasha gives Niamh back the old emergency tin. The codes on its lid are childish, embarrassing and still meaningful. - Days later, Niamh is revising when her phone lights up. A message from Sasha reads: **can you answer** - Niamh answers at once. - After a pause, Sasha writes: **cut my thumb opening beans. one blood. not three.** - Niamh replies: **I’m coming round. Bring the tin opener nowhere near me.** - End on Niamh leaving the house with plasters and crisps, not because the situation is necessarily catastrophic, but because this time she refuses to decide that it is not worth showing up.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Niamh Patel **Age:** 17 **Role:** Narrator and protagonist; sixth-form student; Sasha’s former best friend. Niamh lives above her parents’ pharmacy on the high street, in a flat that is warmer than it should be because the radiator has only two settings: tropical and legally questionable. Her mum, Leena, works long shifts as a pharmacy technician; her dad, Prakash, manages stock and deliveries for a builders’ merchant outside town. They are affectionate, practical parents who have always described Niamh as “easy,” meaning she does not make noise about what she needs. Niamh has learned to reward that description. She gets good grades, makes her own packed lunches, remembers dental appointments, and keeps an emergency £20 note inside the cover of a revision guide. She is proud of this competence but also trapped by it. If people see her as capable, they do not ask whether she is coping. She prefers that arrangement right up until she doesn’t. She has an internal hierarchy for problems: 1. Problems that can be solved by doing something. 2. Problems that can be solved by making a list. 3. Problems caused by people who refuse to do either. Sasha, in Niamh’s private categorisation, has always belonged to the third group. Niamh and Sasha became friends at eleven after Sasha was sick in a school corridor and Niamh, the only child nearby with tissues, water and a spare hair tie, helped clean up. Sasha later told everyone Niamh had “saved her life,” which Niamh found embarrassing. The friendship endured because Sasha made Niamh laugh and Niamh made Sasha feel safe. Over the years, though, Niamh became the person Sasha rang after fights, panic attacks, missed buses, family rows and bad decisions. Niamh’s resentment did not appear suddenly; it accumulated in ordinary, mean little moments—waiting outside parties while Sasha sobbed over someone who did not deserve it, lying to teachers, pretending not to mind when plans were cancelled again. Niamh’s flaw is not simply that she is guarded. It is that she often confuses judgment with insight. She is very good at noticing when people are being foolish, but less good at noticing when they are frightened. She believes that if she can identify the irrational thing someone is doing, she has somehow dealt with their pain. Her sarcasm is both a talent and an escape hatch. She is also quietly competitive. She does not need to beat people loudly; she needs to know she could have. She keeps old marked essays in a drawer and occasionally rereads the teachers’ praise when she is angry. She tells herself she wants to study law because she likes argument, but privately she likes the idea that rules could be made to mean something if a sufficiently organised person took charge. Her idiosyncrasies include: - She makes lists in the Notes app but refuses to call them lists; they are “operational outlines.” - She cannot sleep without checking that the front door is locked, even if she has watched someone lock it. - She reads the ingredients on everything while eating it. - She hates bananas because the smell reminds her of primary-school packed lunches. - She carries a mini stapler in her school bag. Nobody knows why, including Niamh. - She has memorised the opening hours of nearly every shop in Mereford because she dislikes being caught without a contingency plan. - When she is anxious, she cleans her phone screen on her jumper until it is streakier than before. Niamh does not think of herself as lonely. She has friends, classmates, group chats, family. But she tends to make herself useful rather than visible. Her relationship with Sasha unsettles her because Sasha knows how to make her visible. Sasha notices when Niamh is upset even when Niamh has carefully arranged her face into neutrality. This is partly why their friendship has become difficult: Niamh resents Sasha for needing her, but she also resents Sasha for seeing her. Physically, Niamh is small and compact, with a posture that suggests she is always ready to leave a room if it becomes inefficient. She has thick dark hair that she wears in a low plait most days, not because she particularly likes it but because it stays put. Her uniform is always nearly immaculate, though there is usually pen ink on the side of her hand. She favours oversized jumpers, black trainers and a battered navy waterproof that smells faintly of rain and the pharmacy storeroom. When she is thinking, she presses her thumbnail into the side of her index finger. When someone says something stupid, one eyebrow rises before she can stop it. She gestures sparingly; when she does use both hands, it means she is genuinely furious. She moves quickly through public spaces, weaving around people rather than asking them to move, then getting annoyed that they have made her weave. Her motives at the start are tangled: - She wants Sasha found because she loves her and is terrified. - She wants proof that she was right to be worried, because guilt is easier to survive if it can turn into action. - She wants to undo the moment she ignored the text. - She wants to prove to adults, particularly Ms Bains and her parents, that she is not being hysterical. - She wants Gareth exposed, partly because he is dangerous and partly because he makes her feel manipulated in a way she cannot tolerate. Her primary allegiance is to Sasha, even when she insists it is not. She is loyal to her parents but habitually keeps them outside the boundary of her real fear. She is suspicious of Finn but recognises, reluctantly, that he is useful—and later that usefulness is not the only reason she wants him around. **Voice experiments:** > I had muted Sasha three hours earlier, which felt like a mature boundary until her emergency text arrived and I discovered that maturity is mostly just guilt with better branding. > Adults love the word “process.” It makes doing nothing sound like a profession. > “I’m not panicking,” I said, while holding my phone so tightly it was probably applying for asylum. > Mum looked at me in that particular way parents do when they have realised you are no longer a child but are still, somehow, their administrative responsibility. --- # Sasha Reed **Age:** 17 **Role:** Missing friend; catalyst for the mystery; Niamh’s former best friend. Sasha lives with her mum, Donna, in a cramped council flat above an off-licence near the bus station. Her dad left when she was nine and remains theoretically in contact through birthday cards that arrive late, with handwriting from someone else on the envelopes. Donna works unpredictable hours through a cleaning agency and has recently had her shifts cut. Their finances are not a secret exactly, but they are the kind of problem everybody knows exists and avoids naming because naming it would mean doing something. Sasha grew up learning that adults could be overwhelmed, absent or useful only after the worst bit had happened. She does not ask for help cleanly. She arrives in a room loudly, makes a joke, starts a fight, sends a series of increasingly strange voice notes, or behaves as though nothing is wrong until the pressure becomes unbearable. She has often relied on Niamh because Niamh reacts. Even when Niamh is angry, she responds. To Sasha, anger can feel safer than silence. Sasha is funny in a way that can initially seem careless. She remembers bizarre details about people: what crisps they eat, what they were wearing during a breakup, which teacher’s shoes squeak. She does impressions that are technically cruel but so precise that people laugh before they can object. She can turn a bleak bus stop or broken vending machine into a story worth telling. Her charm is real, but it has become armour. She is skilled at making people want to look at her, then furious when they do not look closely enough. Sasha started volunteering at The Bridge partly because it offered food, a warm room, an adult who seemed to listen and a way to get out of the flat after school. She was good with younger kids. She helped them make posters, find lost bus passes and deal with forms they did not understand. She initially admired Gareth because he treated her as competent rather than troublesome. The discovery that he was using that trust—particularly against Jayden—hits her hard because she recognises the tactic: making help feel conditional. She investigates Gareth not because she is naturally reckless in a glamorous way, but because she sees Jayden’s fear and cannot stand it. She also wants to be the person who gets proof. Being useful is not just Niamh’s problem; Sasha wants to matter too. Her mistake is believing that she can control the danger once she has named it. Her flaws: - She is impulsive and sometimes mistakes intensity for honesty. - She can be manipulative without fully recognising it, especially when afraid of being abandoned. - She makes promises in the heat of a moment and then struggles to keep them. - She has a tendency to turn private hurt into public spectacle if she feels ignored. - She is suspicious of calm people, partly because she associates calm with withdrawal. - When she feels ashamed, she becomes funny or cruel before anyone can pity her. Her idiosyncrasies: - She keeps every cinema ticket, wristband and receipt from a day that mattered, shoved into an old biscuit tin. - She hates smooth pens and only writes with cheap black biros that drag slightly on paper. - She calls every adult woman over forty “boss” and every adult man “mate,” regardless of context. - She eats crisps by opening the bag from the bottom because she claims the flavour dust collects there. - She knows the lyrics to old pop songs from the 2000s because Donna plays them while cleaning. - She has an inexplicable fear of ornamental dolls. - She takes photos of ugly buildings, particularly empty shops and badly designed roundabouts, and gives them dramatic captions. - She has a red hoodie with a bleach mark on one cuff, which she insists makes it “customised.” Sasha’s relationship with Niamh is the defining relationship of her life at the story’s beginning. She loves Niamh’s steadiness but feels judged by it. She has become dependent on the fact that Niamh will pick up, turn up, organise and fix. At the same time, Sasha sees Niamh’s loneliness and rigidity more clearly than most people do. Their recent fight has hurt her because it confirmed her fear that she is a burden rather than a person Niamh chooses. Sasha’s allegiance shifts through the story: - First, she is trying to protect Jayden and herself. - Then she is trying to get evidence to Niamh because Niamh is the person she trusts to make sense of it. - Even after Niamh ignores the original text, Sasha still sends her clues. This is not because Sasha is saintly; it is because trust is stubborn, even when it is damaged. - By the end, she wants Niamh to understand what she did wrong, but she also wants her to stay. Physically, Sasha is taller than Niamh and seems to occupy more space than she technically does. She has bleached-blonde hair grown out to show dark roots, usually clipped up with whatever is available: claw clip, pencil, pen, once a spoon handle. She dresses in layers that do not match but somehow look intentional: oversized hoodies, charity-shop coats, short skirts over tights, scuffed boots, cheap rings and borrowed earrings. She speaks with her whole body. When she tells a story, she reenacts the annoying person in it. When she is happy, she leans into people without asking. When she lies, she makes eye contact for too long. When she is truly frightened, she goes very still—a detail Niamh knows and has failed to notice often enough. She has a small scar through one eyebrow from falling off a bike at eight and claims it makes her look “like a divorced Russian spy.” She often has chipped nail varnish in a different colour on each hand. **Voice experiments:** > “I’m not being dramatic. If I was being dramatic, there’d be smoke. Or at least a cloak.” > “You’re acting like I’ve asked you to hide a body. I’ve asked you to come with me to the Quays. There’s a nail bar there that looks like it’s been condemned by God.” > “Niamh, babe, you can’t make a spreadsheet about a feeling. I mean, you can. You probably have. But it won’t improve the feeling.” > “People only say ‘calm down’ when they’ve done something that would make a normal person bite them.” --- # Finn Mercer **Age:** 17 **Role:** Niamh’s uneasy ally; student gossip-account administrator; reluctant participant in the investigation. Finn lives with his dad in a narrow semi-detached house near the edge of Mereford, close enough to the ring road that his bedroom vibrates when lorries go past. His dad is a self-employed electrician who is usually either working, asleep after working, or loudly watching YouTube tutorials about things he already knows how to do. Finn’s mum moved to Leeds when he was thirteen. They still exchange messages, mostly photos of her new dog and questions about school that Finn answers with “fine.” Finn has spent years becoming easy to overlook. He is not unpopular exactly; he is the sort of person people recognise as “that lad from history” but cannot immediately name. His anonymous gossip account, **Mereford Sixth Spills**, gives him an artificial version of connection. People send him tips, secrets and screenshots. For once, they come to him. He tells himself he only posts what people already know, but he knows that is not true. He posted a rumour about Sasha months before the story begins: that she had been caught stealing from a local shop. It was not fully invented; it came from a message he received, likely planted or encouraged by Gareth. But Finn never checked it, because checking would have meant giving up the thrill of being the first person to know. Sasha was never charged with anything, but the rumour followed her for weeks. Finn deleted it once he understood the damage, then pretended deletion was an apology. He is not naturally brave. He does brave things when there is no time to decide not to, then spends the next hour physically shaking and making jokes about dying. He knows how to get information because people underestimate him, not because he is socially masterful. He listens. He notices who changes their phone wallpaper after a breakup, which teachers are suddenly friends with which parents, which students are lying about where they were at lunch. Finn is clever, though his intelligence is scattered. He can remember a username from a screenshot six months ago but loses his PE kit weekly. He knows a surprising amount about data privacy, fake accounts and social-media settings, but has never changed the cracked screen protector on his own phone because he is convinced he will ruin it. His flaws: - He mistakes access to information for understanding. - He has used other people’s vulnerability as entertainment. - He avoids direct confrontation and then resents people for not knowing what he wants. - He talks too much when nervous, often making a situation worse. - He can be defensive when ashamed, joking before he apologises. - He is tempted to turn frightening things into a story because stories give him distance from them. His idiosyncrasies: - He carries a multipack of mint gum but never offers it; he just chews noisily while thinking. - He has a notes app folder titled “possible crimes,” which mostly contains suspicious behaviour by teachers and one entry that says “Mr Hargreaves definitely has a second family.” - He gives his devices names. His phone is called Deborah. - He owns three nearly identical black hoodies and claims this is “a system.” - He knows all the bus timetables but cannot read an analogue clock quickly. - He is allergic to cats but keeps trying to befriend them. - He makes tea too strong because he thinks weak tea is “morally evasive.” - His handwriting is tiny and slanted, like he is trying not to take up paper. Finn’s relationship with Niamh begins in mutual irritation. Niamh sees him as a parasite on other people’s lives. Finn sees her as someone who thinks decency is a personality. Both judgments have some truth in them. They work together because each has something the other lacks: Niamh can act when frightened; Finn can see social patterns and obtain information. Their growing friendship should be hesitant and based less on banter than on moments of mutual recognition. His relationship with Sasha is troubled. Sasha knows—or strongly suspects—that Finn ran the gossip account. She has never had proof. He has spent months avoiding her because guilt is easier to manage at a distance. His involvement in finding her is partly an attempt to repay something that cannot really be repaid. Physically, Finn is lanky and slightly hunched, as if he expects low ceilings. He has pale skin, dark curly hair he cuts himself badly, and glasses that slide down when it rains. He is usually in a black hoodie under his uniform blazer, despite repeated warnings. His trainers are always cleaner than the rest of him because cleaning them is a manageable ritual. He does not make strong eye contact unless he is trying too hard. He fiddles with his sleeves, twists his rings and chews the inside of his cheek while reading messages. When he is actually angry, his voice goes unexpectedly quiet. When he lies, he becomes excessively detailed. **Voice experiments:** > “I’m not saying it’s definitely a crime. I’m saying if it isn’t a crime, it’s got a very crime-adjacent vibe.” > “You know what your problem is? You hear one sentence and immediately put it in a labelled folder. Some of us have to live in the swamp.” > “I deleted the post.” > “That’s not the same thing.” > “I know. I know it’s not. I just didn’t know what the same thing was supposed to be.” > Internal monologue: *There were moments when silence seemed mature. This was not one of them. This was the sort of silence that got people murdered in documentaries, just before the presenter said nobody had heard from them for three days.* --- # Gareth Vale **Age:** Early 40s **Role:** Manager of The Bridge; antagonist; trusted local adult exploiting that trust. Gareth grew up in Mereford and has spent enough time in the town’s voluntary sector to know how institutions behave when they are underfunded. He understands that people are grateful for anyone who fills a gap. The Bridge exists because local services have been cut back, school pastoral teams are overwhelmed and parents need somewhere warm for teenagers to go after school. Gareth has done genuine good through it. He has helped young people fill in college applications, driven them home after late sessions, found donated coats, spoken to angry parents and negotiated with council staff. This matters because Gareth does not see himself as a monster. He sees himself as practical. He believes the town runs on favours, unofficial arrangements and people doing what they have to do. At some point, probably through debt, financial pressure or a relationship with a local scammer, he began using The Bridge’s access to recruit young people for small tasks. First it was framed as harmless: collect a parcel, receive a phone, set up a new account, pick something up from a locker. Then he learned which teenagers could be frightened, which needed money, which had parents too distracted to notice, and which had secrets they could not afford exposed. He tells himself he is not exploiting them because he gives them money, lifts, food and advice. This self-justification is essential to him. He does not need to be secretly sadistic to be dangerous. He is dangerous because he feels entitled to define what harm counts. Gareth’s flaw is not simply greed. It is control disguised as care. He resents young people who refuse the role he has assigned them: grateful, manageable, dependent. Sasha alarms him because she understands relationships as leverage but is not willing to remain leveraged. Niamh alarms him because she is difficult to charm and notices when language becomes evasive. He is not physically theatrical. He does not leer, shout constantly or make threats in villainous phrases. He uses the tone of someone settling an administrative dispute. He says things such as: - “Let’s be realistic.” - “You don’t know the whole context.” - “You’re making a difficult situation worse.” - “Do you understand what happens if people start asking questions?” These statements are frightening because they are plausible, calm and designed to make others feel childish. His idiosyncrasies: - He always carries mints and offers them to anxious young people, which initially makes him seem considerate. - He refers to teenagers as “the kids” even when speaking directly to them. - He wears practical outdoor fleeces in muted colours and has a permanent pen mark on one cuff. - He is good at remembering small details—someone’s preferred hot chocolate, a sibling’s name, a college course—which makes people feel seen. - He cannot tolerate being interrupted. His jaw tightens, though his voice remains gentle. - He keeps The Bridge’s kitchen unnaturally tidy, lining up mugs by colour. - He uses too much hand sanitiser and smells faintly of citrus and cheap coffee. Gareth’s relationships are based on utility and reputation: - He is trusted by local parents because he has helped in visible, ordinary ways. - He is respected by school staff because The Bridge takes pressure off them. - He has access to Sasha’s family because Donna accepted practical assistance. - He sees Jayden as a problem to manage rather than a person. - He sees Niamh as an inconvenience who might still be persuaded to become reasonable. - He sees Finn as negligible until Finn’s archived messages become dangerous. Physically, Gareth is average height, broad through the shoulders, with thinning brown hair and a face that can look kind at a distance. He dresses for practical work: fleece, jeans, worn boots, lanyard with too many keys. He moves quietly, almost economically. He does not pace. He stands slightly too close when speaking to people, creating a sense that they are being boxed in without obvious aggression. He is careful with touch in public: a hand briefly on a shoulder, an arm steered toward a chair, gestures that can be read as supportive unless the person receiving them does not want them. In private, his stillness is more unsettling than anger. **Voice experiments:** > “Sasha has a habit of making every difficulty feel immediate. I’m not criticising her. I’m saying that urgency can make people careless.” > “You think the world is divided into victims and villains because you’re seventeen. Give it a few years. Mostly it’s people trying to keep things from getting worse.” > “I have spent years helping young people who nobody else had time for. If you want to throw that away because you found a messy drawer and heard half a conversation, that’s your choice.” > Internal rationale: *They came to him because nobody else answered. He gave them somewhere to sit, something to eat, a lift home. There were always costs. The only difference was that he had been honest enough to understand this.* --- # Jayden Okafor **Age:** 15 **Role:** Year Ten student; target of Gareth’s coercion; crucial witness. Jayden is in Year Ten at the same school, though Niamh only knows him by sight at the beginning: small for his age, always in a coat even indoors, usually moving through corridors with one earbud in. He lives with his mum, older brother Malik and little sister Amara in a flat near the ring road. His mum works nights in a care home. Malik has recently been arrested for something minor but serious enough to become local gossip, leaving Jayden terrified that anything involving his family will become proof that they are “that sort.” Jayden came to The Bridge because it had a gaming club, free toast and adults who did not make him feel stupid for needing help with homework. Gareth identified quickly that Jayden was anxious, short of money and protective of his family. The first task Gareth asked was to take delivery of a package at a locker. Then another. Gareth paid him cash and implied he was doing Jayden a favour. When Jayden wanted to stop, Gareth made it clear he knew details Jayden had shared during support sessions. Jayden is not passive; he is calculating. His silence is an active survival strategy. He watches adults carefully, notices exits, and knows which teachers will ask too many questions. He gives Niamh clues in tiny increments because he does not know whether she can be trusted and because speaking directly feels impossible. His flaw is that fear makes him sometimes misleading. He may deny what he knows, redirect suspicion or give partial information that puts Niamh on the wrong path. This is not because he enjoys the mystery; it is because he has been taught that information is dangerous. His idiosyncrasies: - He draws elaborate maps of imaginary bus systems in the margins of schoolwork. - He keeps coins in his pocket and counts them by touch when anxious. - He knows every shortcut through Mereford but hates being asked for directions. - He has a bright green backpack that he pretends not to care about, though his sister picked it for him. - He watches repair videos online and can fix cheap headphones with tape, wire and patience. - He likes horror games but cannot cope with jump scares in films because he cannot control when they happen. - He has a strange, fierce loyalty to the school’s elderly caretaker, who once let him wait in the boiler room during a panic attack. Jayden’s main motive is keeping his family safe. He wants Gareth stopped, but he does not trust the promise that reporting him will improve anything. His allegiance shifts toward Sasha because she listens without demanding a full disclosure, and then toward Niamh when she proves she will act on partial information without punishing him for being afraid. Physically, Jayden is slim, with closely cropped hair and an expression that makes adults call him “quiet” when he is actually observing everything. He wears his uniform slightly too large, sleeves pulled over his hands, and keeps his shoulders rounded as though trying to take up less space. When nervous, he taps the side of his trainer against the floor in sets of four. He avoids eye contact when lying, but when he finally decides to tell the truth, he looks directly at the person he is speaking to and does not look away. **Voice experiments:** > “You lot think telling someone is like pressing a button. You tell them and then what? They go home. I still have to be here.” > “He doesn’t shout. That’s worse, isn’t it? If he shouted, people might hear.” > “Sasha said you were horrible but useful.” > “That sounds exactly like her.” > “She said it like it was a compliment.” --- # Leena Patel **Age:** Early 40s **Role:** Niamh’s mother; practical adult ally; emotionally imperfect parent. Leena works as a pharmacy technician at a busy chemist in the retail park. She has spent years dealing with shortages, frightened customers, prescription errors and people who expect her to fix problems she did not create. This has made her extremely competent under pressure and occasionally too brisk at home. She carries work’s problem-solving habits into family life: tea, paracetamol, a checklist, a phone call, a clean towel. She and Niamh have a close relationship built on routines rather than confessions. They watch quiz shows together, argue over whether leftovers count as dinner, and send each other photos of discounted things in shops. Leena has assumed Niamh’s apparent capability means she is fine. She does not realise how much Niamh has learned to curate her own needs. Leena’s flaw is not neglect but interpretation. She sees Niamh coping and mistakes it for ease. When Niamh was younger, Leena praised her for being “no trouble,” not understanding that Niamh heard it as an instruction. She is suspicious of institutions but not cynical; she knows systems can be slow and still contain people trying their best. This makes her a useful contrast to Niamh’s impatience. She does not dismiss danger once she understands it, but she is initially angry that Niamh withheld the original message because she knows delay can matter. Her idiosyncrasies: - She keeps carrier bags folded into tiny triangles under the sink. - She believes every problem is improved by a hot drink, including vomiting, grief and criminal investigations. - She owns several half-used tubes of hand cream and can never find one when she wants it. - She says “have you eaten?” as a greeting when worried. - She saves elastic bands from medicine deliveries in a mug labelled “MUG.” - She has a highly developed dislike of people who block supermarket aisles. - She can identify most tablets by shape and colour but forgets the names of actors instantly. Physically, Leena is medium height, with dark hair usually twisted into a clip and faint lines around her eyes from squinting at labels. She wears practical shoes, long cardigans and a gold watch that is always five minutes fast. When upset, she becomes more efficient: tidying, charging phones, filling water bottles, checking car fuel. She does not cry easily in front of Niamh. Her love appears as preparation. **Voice experiments:** > “You do not get points for handling a crisis alone, Niamh. You get tired, frightened and sometimes hurt. That is all.” > “I am not saying we do nothing. I am saying we do not turn you into another missing child because you have watched too many programmes where teenagers have excellent torch batteries.” > “Eat something. No, not crisps. I know they are technically made of potatoes. So is vodka.” --- # Ms Bains **Age:** Mid-30s **Role:** School safeguarding lead; constrained but caring adult. Ms Bains teaches English two days a week and spends the rest of her time as the school’s designated safeguarding lead, a role that has quietly swallowed most of her working life. She has a cupboard full of cereal bars, donated deodorant, spare tights, phone chargers and forms. The pupils call her office “the cave,” partly because it is windowless and partly because people go in looking miserable and come out holding snacks. She is often seen by students as frustratingly calm. This is partly professional training and partly exhaustion. She cannot react visibly to every disclosure because if she did, she would never stop reacting. But she does remember details. She knows who is avoiding lunch, who has stopped wearing their glasses because they broke, who is suddenly getting picked up by someone new. Ms Bains has trusted Gareth because The Bridge genuinely relieved pressure on the school. That trust becomes a source of guilt, but she should not be presented as someone who simply “looked away.” She had incomplete information, too many cases and a partner organisation with a good reputation. Her failure is systemic and personal: she wanted The Bridge to be as helpful as it seemed. Her flaw is an overreliance on procedure. She believes correct documentation is protective, and often it is. But in this story she must recognise that Niamh’s urgency is not merely teenage melodrama. She has to act before she has the complete, tidy account institutions prefer. Her idiosyncrasies: - She wears trainers with formal dresses because she claims she has “made peace with dignity.” - She has an enormous travel mug containing tea so strong it looks medicinal. - She calls students by their surnames when worried, not angry. - She hates motivational quotes and crosses them out on posters when nobody is looking. - Her office printer is permanently broken, but she still presses the button before remembering. - She keeps a plastic plant that pupils have named Gerald. Physically, Ms Bains has cropped hair, tired eyes and a face that becomes unexpectedly warm when she smiles. She moves fast through school corridors, always carrying too many folders. She often kneels rather than standing over students when they are distressed, but does not force eye contact. **Voice experiments:** > “I believe you. That is not the same as having every answer this minute, and I know those are not the words you want.” > “Safeguarding is not a magic spell, Niamh. It is mostly paperwork done by frightened people who are trying not to miss the important bit.” > “You are allowed to be angry with your friend. You are also allowed to be scared for her. Human beings are irritatingly capable of both.”

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
## Chapter 1: Red Alert The text came just after midnight: 🩸🩸🩸 I was awake because I had a history essay due on Monday and had reached that stage of revision where you decide reorganising your stationery is a form of learning. My desk was covered in colour-coded flashcards, three highlighters with no lids and a mug of tea cold enough to develop its own opinions. Sasha’s chat had been muted. Not blocked. I wasn’t a psychopath. Muted. There is a difference, apparently. One is a decisive act of cruelty. The other is a boundary, which is what people call cruelty when they’ve bought a scented candle and listened to a podcast about healing. The emergency notification came through anyway, lighting the ceiling of my bedroom blue-white. I stared at it for a second too long. Three blood drops. At twelve, Sasha and I had invented what we called the Red Alert system. It started behind the old garden centre, when she climbed through a broken bit of fencing to retrieve a football she hadn’t actually kicked, because it had been Callum Hoyle’s football and Callum had said girls couldn’t climb. She got halfway over, tore her palm open on a wire spike and dropped into a nettle patch swearing with impressive creativity for someone who still had a Beano subscription. She rang me from the bushes. “I’m bleeding,” she said. “Call your mum.” “I can’t. She’ll kill me.” “You’re literally bleeding.” “That’s why it’s urgent.” So I went. I took plasters from our bathroom cabinet and two digestive biscuits, because I thought injured people probably got hungry. We sat behind the garden centre while I pressed kitchen roll to Sasha’s hand and she invented the Red Alert system. One blood drop meant *bring plasters*. Two meant *come now but don’t make it weird*. Three meant *come, don’t ask questions, don’t bring adults*. At twelve, this was thrilling. It made us feel like spies instead of two frightened children sitting beside a pile of fly-tipped tyres. By seventeen, the system had been used for a missed bus, a period leak in Primark, Sasha hiding in a pub toilet after kissing someone’s boyfriend, a panic attack outside a dentist’s, and one occasion when her mum’s ex had come round shouting through the letterbox. The last one had been real. The others had been real too, in their way. That was the problem. Sasha could make anything feel like the end of the world. And I was tired of living in it. I picked up my phone. Her name was at the top of the screen, beneath a photo of us at fourteen in which Sasha was pulling my face sideways while I tried to look annoyed. I had kept it because changing it felt like admitting things had changed. The message sat there. 🩸🩸🩸 No words. No location. Nothing else. I typed: **Are you actually hurt?** Then I deleted it. I typed: **Where are you?** Deleted that too. Earlier that evening, we had had what was technically an argument and emotionally a small industrial fire. Sasha had cancelled on me for the third time that week. We were meant to be revising at mine. She had promised she’d bring the biology notes she’d borrowed in September, which I no longer expected to see again but enjoyed mentioning because I am, at heart, a bad person with a labelled folder. Instead, at half six, she’d sent a voice note. “Can’t come. Bridge thing. Don’t start.” I had started. The Bridge was the youth project she’d been volunteering at—the one in the old Methodist hall near the ring road, with the free toast and the murals painted by teenagers who had clearly been given one afternoon and no artistic supervision. Sasha had been going there most evenings lately. She said it was good. She said the manager, Gareth, actually listened. She said I wouldn’t understand. This was annoying because I did understand. I understood exactly why a warm room with free food and adults who remembered your name might matter. I just did not understand why it had suddenly become more important than every plan we made. So I’d texted: **Sorry your new best friend with a lanyard is taking up so much of your time.** She replied: **At least he answers when something’s wrong.** That had landed. I sent back: **Maybe because you make everything into something wrong.** There had been a pause. Long enough for me to feel pleased with myself, which should have been a warning. Then Sasha wrote: **You treat me like a broken appliance. Either fix it or complain about the noise.** I stared at that for ages. Then I muted her. Now, three hours later, she wanted me to come alone. I looked at the clock. 12:03. Mum was asleep in the next room. Dad was away for work, doing some delivery route in Birmingham that involved getting up at four and calling the Travelodge breakfast “a crime scene.” The flat was quiet except for the fridge humming through the kitchen wall and a car going past on the high street, tyres slicing through rain. I could call Sasha. I could wake Mum. I could put on my waterproof and walk to the bus station, the Quays, the Methodist hall, anywhere Sasha wanted. Instead I felt the familiar, ugly rise of anger. Why did it always have to be me? Why did she only need me when she needed rescuing? My thumbs hovered over the screen. **If this is another emergency, try an adult for once.** I sent it before I could make it kinder. The typing dots appeared. Then disappeared. No reply came. I waited five minutes, because five minutes is apparently the length of time it takes to become a worse version of yourself. Then ten. I called once. It rang until voicemail. Sasha’s recorded greeting came on: “Hi, it’s Sasha. Leave a message unless you’re selling something, in which case I hope your trousers rip in public.” I hung up without speaking. At 12:21, I told myself she had fallen asleep. At 12:40, I told myself she was with someone else. At 1:06, I put my phone face-down and went to bed. The next morning, I woke late, having dreamed I was trapped inside a greenhouse where every plant had my face. My phone was dead. This felt unfair. Phones always died at the moment you most needed them, as though they had been waiting quietly for revenge. I charged it while brushing my teeth, got dressed in the sort of hurry that leaves you wearing one sock inside out, and ran to college through rain that had settled into the air like a personal insult. Sasha’s seat in Politics was empty. It was not unusual for Sasha to be late. She operated on a private time zone in which buses were always delayed and clocks were oppressive. But by the time Mr Hargreaves had finished mispronouncing “referendum” and moved on to a graph nobody could read, she still wasn’t there. I checked my phone under the desk. Nothing from Sasha. One message from her mum. **Sasha is with family. Needs some space. Please don’t worry.** I read it twice. Then a third time. Sasha did have family, technically. She had an aunt in Dubai who sent birthday cards with camels on them and had once called England “charming but damp” in a way that made Sasha want to start a war. She was not with her. I knew because Sasha had told me, three weeks earlier, that her aunt’s flat had one bedroom, two cats and “an air-con unit that sounds like a dying helicopter.” Also, Sasha had left her passport in my bedroom last summer after we tried to apply for jobs at an airport café and discovered neither of us had the qualifications to sell a muffin. My phone buzzed again. A message from Finn Mercer. **you know she’s been at the bridge every night yeah** I looked across the classroom. Finn sat two rows over, hoodie under his blazer despite the uniform rules, chewing gum like it had personally offended him. He did not look at me. Another message arrived. **she was asking questions about people collecting stuff. proper weird ones.** I typed: **Why are you telling me?** His reply came almost at once. **because you look like you’re about to throw up** I hated that he was right. I opened Sasha’s chat. Scrolled past the argument. Past the voice notes I had not listened to. Past a photo she’d sent on Tuesday of a badly painted roundabout near The Quays, captioned: **britain has given up.** There, beneath my message—the one about trying an adult—was something I had missed. A draft message, marked unsent. **I’ve got it. Meet me at—** That was all. Underneath it, the location icon glowed blue. I had not opened it. Not last night. Not until then.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is a strong opening chapter that demonstrates considerable craft. Let me work through the key elements systematically. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Niamh's voice is the chapter's greatest strength. The narration is sharp, self-aware and consistently funny without being smug. Lines like "muted. Not blocked. I wasn't a psychopath. Muted. There is a difference, apparently. One is a decisive act of cruelty. The other is a boundary, which is what people call cruelty when they've bought a scented candle and listened to a podcast about healing" are genuinely witty and reveal character simultaneously. The voice is distinctly British, distinctly teenage, and distinctly Niamh as profiled: someone who uses precision and sarcasm as emotional management. The self-awareness ("I sent it before I could make it kinder") is earned rather than performed. The backstory of the Red Alert system is handled elegantly. The garden centre scene is specific, funny and emotionally resonant without being overwrought. The escalating list of previous Red Alert uses (missed bus, period leak in Primark, pub toilet, panic attack, the real one) is excellent: it establishes the pattern of Sasha's emergencies while quietly noting that the last one was genuinely serious, which complicates Niamh's dismissiveness without excusing it. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT: The chapter succeeds at making the reader feel Niamh's guilt accumulating in real time. The timestamps (12:03, 12:21, 12:40, 1:06) are a smart structural choice, showing the slow erosion of her rationalizations. "At 12:21, I told myself she had fallen asleep. At 12:40, I told myself she was with someone else." This is economical and devastating. The reader understands exactly what Niamh is doing and why, and feels the weight of it. METAPHOR AND PROSE QUALITY: Most of the figurative language lands. "Rain that had settled into the air like a personal insult" is good. "A small industrial fire" for the argument is apt. The dream of being trapped in a greenhouse where every plant has her face is a nice foreshadowing touch. However, "cold enough to develop its own opinions" for the tea is slightly strained—it reaches for wit and lands just short. "Tyres slicing through rain" is a solid image. The prose generally avoids purple excess; it's controlled and purposeful. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is sparse but effective. The argument via text is rendered with economy. Sasha's voicemail greeting ("I hope your trousers rip in public") is perfectly in character. The exchange with Finn via text is appropriately terse. The childhood phone call from the bushes is charming and establishes both characters efficiently. PLOT AND STRUCTURE: The chapter follows the plan closely and competently. The opening hook is immediate. The Red Alert backstory is integrated naturally. The argument is established. The ignored message is shown. The morning discovery of Sasha's absence, the suspicious text from Sasha's mum, Finn's information, and the discovery of the unsent draft and unopened location pin all land in the right order. The ending is a proper hook. CONSISTENCY WITH PROFILE: Niamh is exactly as described: the operational outlines, the labelled folder reference, the emergency £20 note implied by her general competence, the self-awareness about being a "bad person." The mini stapler doesn't appear but that's fine for a first chapter. Sasha's voicemail is perfectly in character. Finn's observation ("because you look like you're about to throw up") is consistent with his profile as someone who notices things. WEAKNESSES: The chapter is perhaps slightly long for what it accomplishes structurally—the middle section covering the argument could be trimmed without loss. The "dream of a greenhouse" is a little on-the-nose given the greenhouse's later plot significance; it risks feeling like a planted clue rather than an organic detail. The Finn introduction is slightly abrupt—he appears via text with no prior establishment in the chapter, which works but feels slightly convenient. The "colour-coded flashcards" opening is a familiar YA establishing beat, though it's executed well enough not to feel stale. OVERALL: This is accomplished, confident work. The voice is the standout achievement—it's genuinely funny, genuinely specific, and genuinely revealing of character. The emotional mechanics are well-constructed. The chapter does exactly what a first chapter should: establishes voice, character, situation, and stakes while ending on a hook that makes the reader want to continue. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
## Chapter 2: The Place Nobody Uses The location pin said the greenhouse. Not a greenhouse in the normal sense, with tomatoes and elderly people in sun hats. The school greenhouse was behind the sports hall, past the fenced-off maintenance yard and the bit of grass everyone called the field despite it being mostly mud and a broken goalpost. Officially, it belonged to the Grow the Future initiative. There was still a poster about it in the science corridor: smiling children in gardening gloves, holding carrots the size of medieval weapons beneath the slogan **OUR PLANET, OUR PATCH, OUR FUTURE**. The project had lasted one term. The greenhouse had lasted because nobody had worked out whose budget it was supposed to come from. I spent the rest of Politics pretending to take notes while staring at the blue pin on my phone. My stomach had developed a hard little knot somewhere behind my ribs. Every time Mr Hargreaves said “democratic mandate,” I imagined Sasha sitting in the greenhouse in the dark, waiting for me to turn up. At break, I went looking for Ms Bains. I got as far as the corridor outside her office and stopped. There were two Year Eights on plastic chairs outside the door. One was crying into his sleeve. The other was eating a cereal bar with the focused misery of someone who had been told it counted as lunch. Ms Bains was inside, saying, “No, I’m not cross. I just need you to tell me where you slept.” I stood there with my phone in my hand. Then I walked away. It was cowardly, obviously. But I had not yet worked out how to say: *My friend sent me three blood emojis last night and I told her to try an adult, which is a sentence I will probably hear in my own head until I die.* Instead, after last lesson, I went to the greenhouse. Finn followed me. I noticed him just as I was pushing through the side gate by the sports hall. He was ten metres behind, hands in the pockets of his black hoodie, attempting to look like he had always intended to walk through a puddle behind a PE building at four in the afternoon. “You’re following me,” I said. “No,” he said. “I’m walking in the same direction as you, repeatedly, for unrelated reasons.” “There is nothing here.” “Exactly. Very popular route.” I kept walking. Rain had started again, not proper rain, just the thin, persistent kind that gets under your collar and makes you hate weather as a concept. The sports hall windows were fogged from the inside. Somewhere, a whistle shrieked. A Year Seven shouted, “That was my shin, you animal,” with surprising dignity. “You can go away,” I said. “I could,” Finn said. “But then if you get murdered by compost, I’ll feel responsible.” “I’m not going to get murdered by compost.” “You don’t know what fertiliser is capable of.” The greenhouse stood at the far end of the maintenance yard, behind a chain-link fence with a sign reading **NO UNAUTHORISED ACCESS**. The sign had been laminated, which made it feel less like a warning and more like a disappointed suggestion. The gate was not locked. That was not reassuring. I pushed it open. Inside, the greenhouse smelled of wet soil, rotten leaves and something sourer underneath. The glass was clouded with algae. Several panes were cracked, including one at waist height with brown tape slapped over it. Dead vines climbed the metal frames. A broken plastic chair lay upside down in the corner as though it had given up. Finn stepped in behind me and immediately sneezed. “Bless you,” I said automatically. “Don’t be nice to me now. It’s confusing.” I ignored him. The place had been used recently. Not lovingly, but used. There were fresh tyre marks in the mud outside the door and a stack of black plastic plant trays that hadn’t yet collected dust. A small electric heater sat on an overturned crate, plugged into an extension lead that vanished through a hole in the wall. “Gardening scheme,” Finn said quietly. “Maybe.” “You think teenagers volunteered to garden in November?” “I don’t know what I think.” That was the worst bit. I usually did. Near the back bench, someone had left a cardboard coffee cup. I picked it up. Across the side, in black biro, was written: **NOBODY SPEAK TO ME UNTIL I AM A PERSON AGAIN.** Sasha’s handwriting. The capitals tilted aggressively to the right, as if each letter was attempting to leave the page. My throat tightened. Finn saw it over my shoulder. For once, he did not make a joke. On the floor beneath the bench lay a torn scrap of lined paper, damp around the edges. I crouched and picked it up. Half a list. **J. Okafor** **T. Mills** **R— phone** **G.V. knows too much** **not—** The final word had been ripped away. “Jayden?” Finn said. “Year Ten. Green backpack.” “You know everyone’s backpack?” “I know the green one because it’s offensively green.” I folded the paper carefully and put it inside my planner. Then I saw the blood. It was only a smear, no bigger than my thumb, on the jagged edge of the broken glass pane. Dark red-brown against the dirt. For a second, I could not breathe properly. Finn leaned closer. “Could be anything.” “It’s blood.” “Probably.” “That doesn’t help.” “I’m trying to be balanced.” “You’re trying to say words.” A sound came from outside. Metal scraping. Both of us froze. Someone was in the maintenance yard. Finn grabbed my sleeve and pulled me down behind the potting bench. I nearly swore at him, but then a shadow crossed the greenhouse wall. Through the cloudy glass, I saw the outline of a person near the potting shed. Broad shoulders. A lanyard swinging against a dark fleece. Keys jingled. The person opened the shed door. Finn’s fingers tightened around my sleeve. I could hear movement: boxes shifting, something dragged across concrete, a cupboard shutting. Whoever it was did not come into the greenhouse. They stayed next door for less than a minute. Then came the slam of a van door. Engine. Tyres grinding through wet mud. When the sound had gone, Finn let go. “That was Gareth Vale,” he said. “You can’t know that.” “I saw his stupid fleece.” “Lots of men have stupid fleeces.” “Not that one. It’s got the Bridge logo on the chest. Like he’s sponsored by damp.” I got up too quickly and hit my shoulder on the underside of the bench. “Brilliant,” Finn said. “Injury acquired. We can add it to the evidence.” I pressed a hand to my shoulder, furious mainly because it hurt. Gareth Vale. Manager of The Bridge. The person Sasha had called “actually useful” the night before. I thought of his name beside the words on the paper. G.V. knows too much. My phone started ringing. Sasha’s mum. I answered at once. “Hello?” There was a pause. Then Donna Reed said, “She’s not with family.” Her voice was rough, as if she had been crying or smoking or both. “I know,” I said. “I didn’t know what to say.” “Where is she?” “If I knew that, love, I wouldn’t be ringing you.” I looked at the broken pane, the smear of blood, the torn page in my planner. “She came home last night,” Donna said. “About eleven. She was upset. Properly upset, not just…” She stopped. “Not just Sasha upset,” I said. There was another pause. “No,” she said quietly. “Not just Sasha upset.” “What did she say?” “That someone was sorting it. That she’d made it worse. She kept asking if I’d told anyone about the rent stuff.” “The rent stuff?” “She’d been talking to that Bridge man. Gareth. He helped me fill in forms when my hours got cut. I thought he was helping.” “You thought he was helping,” I repeated. “I did. Then she left. Took that red hoodie. I told her not to go. She said she just had to meet someone.” “Did she say who?” “No.” My eyes went to the coffee cup. NOBODY SPEAK TO ME UNTIL I AM A PERSON AGAIN. “I’m going to tell someone,” I said. “Who?” “School. Police. Someone.” Donna made a small sound that might have been agreement or fear. “Please,” she said. “Please find her.” The call ended. For the first time, Finn looked properly worried. “Right,” he said. “We should probably not be doing this alone.” “I know.” It came out sharper than I meant it to. We found Ms Bains in her office. The Year Eights had gone. Gerald the plastic plant was on the filing cabinet, leaning sideways as if he had also had a difficult afternoon. Ms Bains listened without interrupting. I showed her the messages. The location pin. The torn notebook page. I told her about Donna’s call and the greenhouse, leaving out only the part where I had ignored Sasha’s text. Not because I was trying to protect myself. Not entirely. Ms Bains took photographs of the paper and the blood smear through the pictures on my phone. “Have either of you touched anything else?” she asked. “The coffee cup,” I said. “The paper,” Finn added. “Fine. That’s fine. We’ll note it.” She did not say it was fine like it was fine. She said it like she was trying to stop us both from falling apart. “I need to call Sasha’s mum,” she said. “And the police. I need you both to stay on site for a little while.” “Are they going to look?” I asked. “Yes.” “When?” “Now.” The word should have made me feel better. It didn’t. Outside the office, as Finn and I waited by the lockers, someone brushed past us. Jayden Okafor. Green backpack. Sleeves pulled down over his hands. He looked at the closed door to Ms Bains’s office. Then at me. “Don’t tell Mr Vale I saw you,” he whispered. Before I could ask what he meant, he was gone.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter demonstrates considerable craft and largely succeeds at what it sets out to do. Let me examine it across the relevant dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTER: Niamh's voice is consistently rendered throughout. The internal hierarchy she applies to problems, her self-aware sarcasm, and her tendency to confuse judgment with insight all come through without being stated explicitly. The line "It was cowardly, obviously. But I had not yet worked out how to say: My friend sent me three blood emojis last night and I told her to try an adult, which is a sentence I will probably hear in my own head until I die" is excellent — it captures her self-awareness, her guilt, and her dark humour simultaneously without any of those elements cancelling the others out. The decision to walk away from Ms Bains's office the first time is a small, precise character beat that shows rather than tells her avoidance. Finn is handled well. His banter is calibrated correctly — present but not overwhelming, and the chapter resists making him a slick comic foil. His genuine worry at the end ("For once, he did not make a joke") is earned because the chapter has established his default mode. The detail about him identifying Gareth by the Bridge logo on the fleece is both plausible and characterful. DIALOGUE: The dialogue is one of the chapter's stronger elements. The exchange about "walking in the same direction for unrelated reasons" is funny without being too polished. The Donna Reed phone call is particularly well-handled — her voice is rough and evasive, and the line "Not just Sasha upset" lands because it's Niamh completing Donna's sentence, showing she knows Sasha well enough to fill in the gaps. Ms Bains's "That's fine. That's fine. We'll note it" followed by the narrator's gloss ("She said it like she was trying to stop us both from falling apart") is a good example of dialogue doing emotional work without being on-the-nose. SETTING: The greenhouse is rendered with specific, unglamorous detail — algae-clouded glass, dead vines, a broken plastic chair "as though it had given up," the Grow the Future poster. These details are British and credible without being assembled from generic props. The smell of wet soil and something sourer underneath is appropriately understated. The maintenance yard scene with the shadow and the van is tense without being melodramatic. PLOT MECHANICS: The chapter follows its plan closely. The location pin, the greenhouse visit, Finn's involvement, the evidence found (coffee cup, torn page, blood smear), the glimpsed figure, Donna's call, the Ms Bains scene, and Jayden's whispered warning all land in the right order and at roughly the right weight. The chapter doesn't rush or pad. The clue-finding is plausible rather than convenient — the coffee cup with Sasha's handwriting is a particularly good touch because it's so specific to her character as established in the profiles. HUMOUR: The darkly comic register is maintained well. "Murdered by compost" and "fertiliser is capable of" are funny without undercutting the tension. The Grow the Future poster with its medieval-weapon carrots is a good satirical detail. The chapter doesn't overdo the jokes — they appear at natural breathing points rather than being inserted to signal genre. WEAKNESSES: A few moments are slightly over-written. "Rain had started again, not proper rain, just the thin, persistent kind that gets under your collar and makes you hate weather as a concept" is good, but "makes you hate weather as a concept" is the kind of slightly too-clever formulation that appears once or twice too often in this register of YA fiction. It works here, but it's a familiar move. Similarly, "Gerald the plastic plant was on the filing cabinet, leaning sideways as if he had also had a difficult afternoon" is charming but feels like it's reaching slightly for whimsy at a moment of genuine tension. The chapter's ending — Jayden's whispered warning — is effective but the setup is slightly convenient. He happens to walk past at exactly the right moment. The plan called for him to overhear part of the conversation, which is marginally more plausible than the execution, where he seems to appear from nowhere. This is a minor structural issue. The blood smear is handled with appropriate restraint, but the moment where Niamh "could not breathe properly" is the one instance where the narration tells us an emotional response rather than showing it through action or thought. Given how well the rest of the chapter manages this, it stands out slightly. FAITHFULNESS TO PLAN: The chapter follows the plan very closely. All the major beats are present: the location pin, the greenhouse, Finn following, the evidence, the glimpsed figure, Donna's call, Ms Bains, and Jayden's warning. The chapter plan's note about the greenhouse being "derelict but not spooky in a polished way" is honoured. The dynamic between Niamh and Finn — tense, mutually irritated, grudgingly functional — is correctly established. OVERALL: This is a strong chapter. The voice is consistent, the humour is earned, the plot mechanics are clean, and the emotional stakes are present without being overwrought. It's not a masterpiece — there are small moments of over-reach and one or two slightly convenient plot mechanics — but it's well above average for the genre and the task. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 17 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
## Chapter 3: The Bridge The Bridge smelled of wet coats, instant coffee and the kind of carpet that had absorbed every disappointment in Mereford since 1987. It occupied the old Methodist hall on Darnley Road, a squat brown-brick building tucked between a tyre place and a boarded-up estate agent’s. Someone had painted a mural along the side wall: hands holding up a rainbow-coloured bridge over a river. The hands had too many fingers. It was trying its best. Finn stood beneath the awning beside me, rain dripping off his fringe. “This is where people come for help?” he said. “Don’t start.” “I’m not starting. I’m assessing. It looks like a church hall and a hostage situation had a baby.” “Maybe try not to say hostage situation outside the building where my missing friend volunteered.” “Fair.” Ms Bains had told us not to investigate. More specifically, she had said, “You have done enough for today. Let adults take it from here.” That had been yesterday. Sasha had now been missing for most of a day and a half. The police had come to school, taken statements and looked around the greenhouse. They had also said things like *we are treating this seriously* and *we are following several lines of enquiry*, which sounded reassuring until you realised neither phrase involved telling me where Sasha was. I had not slept. I had listened to every noise in the flat and checked my phone every time it buzzed. At six in the morning Mum had found me sitting at the kitchen table in my school jumper, staring at a bowl of cereal that had gone soft. “You’re not going in today,” she said. “I have a mock.” “You have a missing friend.” “They’re not mutually exclusive.” “Niamh.” That tone. The one that made my name sound like a warning label. I went in anyway. Mum went to work after extracting a promise that I would go straight home.
