Then Finn sent me a message at lunch. **bridge has drop in 4-7. gareth is there most afternoons.** I replied: **how do you know?** **because i have eyes and a deeply regrettable online presence** So now we were standing outside The Bridge pretending we were normal sixth-formers interested in volunteering. Finn had even brought a tote bag, which made him look like he was about to sign a petition against cutlery. “Try to act normal,” I said. He looked offended. “I am normal.” “You named your phone Deborah.” “That was private information.” “You told me.” “Under pressure.” Inside, the hall was warmer than I expected. There were beanbags against one wall, a table covered in leaflets about anxiety, college applications and debt advice, and a kitchen hatch where a woman with purple glasses was buttering toast. Three younger kids were playing Mario Kart on an old console. Two girls sat at a table filling in application forms. A boy in a school blazer was asleep upright in a plastic chair, his head tipped back and his mouth open. Nothing looked criminal. That was the irritating thing about places that mattered to people. They did not arrange themselves around your suspicions. “Hiya,” said the woman with the toast. “You two after the homework club?” “Volunteering,” I said. “Lovely. We always need volunteers. You any good with children?” “No,” said Finn. “Yes,” I said at the same time. The woman laughed as though this was charming. “Gareth’s in the office,” she said. “He’ll talk you through it.” The office was at the end of a narrow corridor, past a noticeboard covered in photos from charity fun-runs and a poster saying **YOU BELONG HERE**. Someone had drawn a moustache on the word *belong*. The door was open. Gareth Vale sat behind a desk with a paper cup of coffee and an ancient laptop. He looked up as we knocked. He was not what I had expected, though I did not know what I had expected. Someone taller, perhaps. Sharper. More obviously the sort of person who appeared in warning stories. Instead, he looked tired. Mid-forties, maybe. Brown hair thinning at the front. Navy fleece with The Bridge logo embroidered over the chest. A lanyard with enough keys to run a prison. He had the kind of face that made adults say “he’s lovely” after meeting him once at a school fundraiser. “Niamh,” he said. My stomach dropped. He smiled, apologetically. “Sasha’s mentioned you.” Of course she had. “Good things?” Finn asked. Gareth looked at him. “And you are?” “Finn.” “Ah. Finn.” His smile shifted slightly, as if he had found a familiar item in a drawer. “You’re at Mereford Sixth too, aren’t you?” “Allegedly.” Gareth gave a small laugh. It was not a bad laugh. That annoyed me more. “We were wondering if there was any news about Sasha,” I said. The smile left his face. “None yet,” he said. “I spoke to the police yesterday. Awful situation.” “You saw her on Tuesday?” “I see lots of young people most days.” “She was here every evening.” “Sasha did some volunteering, yes. Helped with the younger ones. She was very good with them.” The phrase landed oddly. Not because it was wrong, but because it sounded like something people said after someone died. “What was she doing?” I asked. “Helping out. Homework, food parcels, admin. Whatever needed doing.” “Did she say anything about people collecting things?” Gareth leaned back in his chair. His expression did not change much, but the room did. It felt suddenly smaller. “Sasha had been under a lot of pressure,” he said carefully. “I don’t think it would be fair to repeat private conversations.” “She’s missing.” “I know.” “Then privacy is maybe not the main issue.” Finn made a tiny noise beside me. Not quite a cough. More a warning. Gareth folded his hands on the desk. “She had some worries about home,” he said. “About money. About people talking. She could become very… immediate, when she was upset.” There it was. The neat version of Sasha. Dramatic. Difficult. Not quite reliable. My face went hot. “You mean she panicked,” I said. “I mean she was frightened.” The fact that he said it gently made me hate him. A voice sounded in the corridor. Jayden. He came past carrying a cardboard box of tinned soup. When he saw Gareth’s office door open, he stopped. Gareth stood. “Jayden, can you put those in the kitchen, mate?” Jayden did not move. “Phone,” Gareth added, holding out his hand. Jayden’s face changed so quickly I almost missed it. His mouth went flat. His shoulders pulled in. “I’m using it,” he said. “I know. That’s why I’m asking.” “It’s my mum.” “Then she can call the office.” Gareth’s voice stayed mild. Jayden handed over his phone. Gareth slipped it into his fleece pocket. “Thanks. Kitchen, please.” Jayden walked away with the box. As he passed us, he did not look at me. Gareth sat down again. “You were saying?” he asked. “I need the toilet,” I said. Finn blinked at me. “Of course,” Gareth said. “End of the corridor, left.” I walked out. The toilet was on the left. So was the office supply cupboard. I waited until Finn started talking loudly about volunteer insurance, which was not difficult for him. Then I slipped into Gareth’s office. The desk drawer was half open. I knew I should not touch it. I touched it anyway. Inside were stacks of forms, pens, two packs of mints, a roll of bin bags and a folder labelled **SUPPORT NOTES — CONFIDENTIAL**. Under the folder was a loose sheet. Names in columns. Initials. Notes. **T.M. — phone / mum nights** **J.O. — needs cash / easy to frighten** **R.H. — account set up** **S.R. — asking questions** My skin prickled. I lifted my phone. The office door opened. Gareth stood there. For one stupid second, I was holding the sheet in one hand and my phone in the other, as if I had been caught doing exactly what I was doing. “I got lost,” I said. He looked at the toilet door, visible three feet away. “Did you?” “Yes.” His gaze dropped to the paper. Then back to me. “You know,” he said, still calm, “when people are frightened, they often decide information means something before they understand the context.” “What context makes ‘easy to frighten’ normal?” His jaw tightened almost invisibly. “That’s not what it says.” “I can read.” “I’m sure you can.” For a moment, neither of us moved. Then Gareth stepped aside. “You should probably go home, Niamh.” It was not a threat. That was what made it feel like one. Outside, Finn was waiting beneath the mural. “You were in there forever,” he said. “He has notes on people.” “Like actual notes?” “On Jayden. On Sasha. ‘Easy to frighten.’” Finn stopped chewing. “I’ve seen his van at school,” he said. “By the greenhouse. A few times.” “Why didn’t you say?” “Because he runs the gardening scheme. I thought it was gardening. I’m sorry I didn’t immediately suspect the youth worker of being a criminal mastermind.” “He’s not a mastermind.” “No,” Finn said. “That’s worse, isn’t it?” A movement near the gate caught my eye. Jayden stood there with his green backpack on one shoulder. He looked as if he might run. Then he dropped something at my feet and walked away. A bus ticket, damp at the corners. On the back, in faint biro, were six words. **Quays. Unit 14. Ask for red bag.**
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 3)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 3 of this darkly comic British YA fiction demonstrates considerable craft and largely fulfills its plan with intelligence and restraint. Let me examine it across the relevant dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTER CONSISTENCY Niamh's voice is sharply rendered throughout. The narration maintains her characteristic blend of competence, suppressed fear, and dry observation. Lines like "Nothing looked criminal. That was the irritating thing about places that mattered to people. They did not arrange themselves around your suspicions" are genuinely good: they reveal character, advance theme, and avoid the trap of making the setting cartoonishly sinister. Her internal hierarchy for problems, established in the character profile, manifests naturally in how she processes the scene. Finn is well-handled. His tote bag detail, the "Deborah" callback, his instinct to talk loudly about volunteer insurance as a distraction — these are consistent with his profile as someone who uses social performance as a coping mechanism. His observation "That's worse, isn't it?" about Gareth not being a mastermind is one of the chapter's best lines: it's funny, thematically resonant, and character-true. Gareth is introduced with exactly the right register. The chapter plan called for him to be "ordinary rather than theatrical," and the execution delivers. His mildness is more unsettling than overt menace. The detail of taking Jayden's phone with patient, reasonable-sounding language is chilling precisely because it's so mundane. "Then she can call the office" is a perfect line — bureaucratic, controlling, utterly plausible. Jayden's brief appearance is economical and effective. His face going "flat" when asked for the phone, the bus ticket dropped without eye contact — these are consistent with his profile as someone who communicates in increments because direct speech feels dangerous. DIALOGUE The dialogue is largely strong. The exchange about naming the phone "Deborah" is light without being frivolous. The Gareth-Niamh confrontation in the office is well-paced: his calm is genuinely unsettling, and Niamh's "I can read" / "I'm sure you can" exchange captures the power dynamic without melodrama. The chapter avoids the common YA pitfall of having characters explain their feelings to each other. One minor weakness: Finn's "I'm not starting. I'm assessing. It looks like a church hall and a hostage situation had a baby" is slightly too polished — it reads as a writer's joke rather than something a nervous seventeen-year-old would produce spontaneously. The chapter catches this with Niamh's immediate rebuke, which partially redeems it, but the line itself is a touch too constructed. PLOT AND PLAN ADHERENCE The chapter follows its plan closely and competently. The plan called for: establishing The Bridge as genuinely beneficial on the surface (achieved), introducing Gareth as ordinary and unsettling (achieved), Niamh noticing Jayden avoiding Gareth and the phone confiscation (achieved), Finn distracting a volunteer (achieved, though it's Gareth rather than a volunteer he distracts), Niamh seeing the drawer with names (achieved), being caught before photographing it (achieved), Finn's van revelation (achieved), and Jayden dropping the bus ticket (achieved). The one deviation is that Finn distracts Gareth rather than a separate volunteer, which actually works better dramatically — it puts Niamh in the office alone with the stakes higher. The plan's end beat — Niamh getting home to find her mother waiting after Ms Bains called — is absent. The chapter ends on the bus ticket clue instead. This is a meaningful omission; the confrontation with Leena is an important emotional beat that the chapter plan positioned here. Whether this was a deliberate structural choice or an oversight, it means the chapter's emotional arc feels slightly incomplete. The chapter ends on plot momentum rather than the personal reckoning the plan intended. PROSE QUALITY The opening paragraph is excellent: "The Bridge smelled of wet coats, instant coffee and the kind of carpet that had absorbed every disappointment in Mereford since 1987." This is specific, funny, and grounded — exactly the register the plan called for. The mural with too many fingers "trying its best" is a lovely, economical image. The prose avoids purple excess throughout. Metaphors are sparse and earned. "The room felt suddenly smaller" is a cliché, but it's deployed quickly and doesn't linger. The writing is clean and purposeful. There are occasional moments where the prose reaches slightly too hard. "The neat version of Sasha. Dramatic. Difficult. Not quite reliable." — this is good, but the fragmented sentences here feel slightly more stylized than the rest of the narration. It works, but it's a minor inconsistency in register. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT The chapter earns its emotional weight. The detail of Sasha being described in the past tense ("She was very good with them") landing "oddly" is genuinely affecting. The list in the drawer — particularly "S.R. — asking questions" — is a quiet horror. The chapter doesn't overplay these moments, which is the right choice. The Jayden phone confiscation scene is the chapter's emotional peak and is handled with real skill. It shows rather than tells the coercive dynamic, and Jayden's compliance is more disturbing than resistance would be. LIGHTNESS AND DARKNESS The balance between comedy and menace is well-maintained. The humor (tote bag, Deborah, volunteer insurance) never trivializes the danger. The chapter earns its lighter moments because they're character-based rather than situation-based — the comedy comes from who Finn is, not from the situation being treated as a joke. OVERALL ASSESSMENT This is a confident, well-executed chapter that largely delivers on its considerable ambitions. The main weaknesses are: the missing end beat (the confrontation with Leena), one slightly over-constructed joke from Finn, and a minor register inconsistency in the fragmented internal narration. These are real but not severe flaws in an otherwise accomplished piece of YA writing. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 16 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 5 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 14 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 4

Ok now chapter 4. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
## Chapter 4: Unit 14 Mum was waiting for me in the kitchen. This was worse than if she’d shouted. If she shouted, I could have shouted back. We could have had an argument with edges. Instead, she was sitting at the table in her work cardigan, still wearing her lanyard, a mug of tea untouched in front of her. My dead history flashcards were stacked neatly beside the sugar jar. That was how I knew she was furious. “Hello,” I said. “Take your coat off.” “I’m not five.” “No. You’re seventeen and apparently investigating a missing-person case with a boy who runs a gossip account. Coat.” I took it off. The kitchen was too warm. Mum always turned the heating up when she was worried, as though central heating could stop bad news getting in through the walls. “Ms Bains called,” she said. “I know.” “She told me you’d found evidence at the school.” “I found a piece of paper.” “You went to The Bridge.” I said nothing. Mum shut her eyes for a moment. When she opened them, they were shiny but dry. “Why didn’t you wake me last night?” I looked at the mug. A skin had formed on the tea. “Because you’d been at work.” “That is not the same thing as being unavailable.” “I know.” “No, I don’t think you do.” Her voice stayed low. That was worse too. “You had a message from Sasha that frightened you. You were alone with it. And instead of waking me, you decided you had to deal with it yourself.” “I didn’t deal with it.” “That isn’t the point.” It was, slightly. But I didn’t say that. Mum got up and started doing things with unnecessary purpose: putting the kettle on, wiping a perfectly clean bit of counter, folding a carrier bag into one of her tiny triangles. “You are not going anywhere else,” she said. “The police are involved. Ms Bains is involved. You come home from school and you stay home.” “There’s a clue.” “There is a missing girl, Niamh.” “I know that.” “And you are not the person responsible for finding her.” The phrase caught somewhere in my chest. Because I was. Not officially. Not sensibly. But the first thing Sasha had done was text me, and I had looked at the screen and decided I had better things to do. “I’m going upstairs,” I said. Mum looked at me. “Have you eaten?” “Yes.” “You haven’t.” “I had crisps.” “That is not food.” “It’s potato.” “So is vodka.” I went upstairs before she could make me have toast. My phone buzzed before I reached my room. **finn:** are you alive **me:** unfortunately **finn:** same **finn:** quays tomorrow? **me:** mum said no **finn:** is that a no no or a niamhs-mum-no **me:** don’t be annoying **finn:** that’s not an answer I stared at the bus ticket photo I’d taken. The Quays. Unit 14. Ask for red bag. The Quays had been Mereford Council’s attempt to make the town “a destination.” They’d spent three years building glass-fronted units along the river, put up decorative lamps that never worked and opened a restaurant called Dock & Dram, which closed after six months because nobody in Mereford wanted to pay fourteen pounds for a burger served on a slate. Now it had a vape shop, a nail bar, a charity furniture warehouse and a corridor of empty shops with LETTING signs in every window. Not exactly Bond territory. Still. **me:** daylight. an hour. we don’t touch anything. **finn:** this sounds like touching things in advance **me:** shut up The next afternoon, I told Mum I was staying late for a Politics catch-up. This was not my best lie. Mum knew I hated Politics catch-ups, mainly because they involved Mr Hargreaves saying “interesting point” before explaining why your point had been wrong. She looked at me for a long moment while I put my shoes on. “Straight home,” she said. “Yeah.” “I mean it.” “I know.” She sighed. “You always say that like it means something.” The Quays looked even more depressing in daylight. Rain blew under the covered walkway. A delivery van was parked half on the pavement outside the furniture warehouse, its back doors open. Across the river, a row of new-build flats stared down at the empty units with the blank confidence of things built for people who would never arrive. Finn was waiting outside the vape shop, hood up, tote bag over one shoulder. “What’s in the bag?” I asked. “Water. Charger. Two cereal bars. Gloves.” “Why gloves?” “Because you said we don’t touch anything.” “You brought gloves so we can touch things.” “I brought gloves in case you touch things and I have to stop you doing it badly.” We walked along the rear service road behind the units. Unit numbers were painted above metal doors: 10, 11, 12. Unit 14 had no sign. Just a grey door beside the loading bay of the charity furniture warehouse. A man in a fluorescent vest wheeled a trolley of flat-pack wardrobes past us without looking up. “Ask for red bag,” Finn said. “Don’t say it like that.” “How should I say it?” “I don’t know. Less like you’re buying drugs in a GCSE drama exam.” The warehouse entrance was open. Inside, fluorescent lights buzzed over donated sofas, mismatched dining tables and rows of wardrobes with handwritten price tags. A woman at the till was arguing with a customer about whether “collection only” meant collection only. Behind the counter, a man with a shaved head and a grey beard was sorting receipts. “Hi,” I said. He looked up. “You after anything?” “Red bag?” Finn said. I shut my eyes. The man stared at him. “Sorry,” I said quickly. “We’re looking for Gareth Vale. From The Bridge.” “Oh. Gareth.” The man nodded toward the rear of the warehouse. “He’s not here. Comes in Tuesdays mostly. Collects old devices. Youth project, innit?” “Does he use Unit 14?” I asked. The man frowned. “Back room. Sorting space. Not for customers.” “We’re not customers.” “That’s usually the bit people say right before they become a problem.” Finn smiled. “We’re students.” “Congratulations.” A woman near the till shouted, “Darren, this wardrobe is haunted.” Darren rubbed his forehead. “Five minutes,” he said. “You don’t touch anything. You don’t take anything. You don’t make me regret this.” The back room was through a fire door beside the warehouse toilets. Unit 14, apparently, was not a unit at all. It was a narrow sorting space full of donation sacks, broken office chairs and cardboard boxes marked **CABLES**, **KITCHEN**, **MISC.** and, ominously, **DON’T BIN**. At first, it looked exactly like what Darren had said it was. Old phones in a crate. Laptops with stickers peeled off their lids. Piles of school blazers. A rack of red charity-shop bags hanging on hooks by the door. “This is nothing,” Finn said. “Don’t say that yet.” “Why?” “Because if it is nothing, I’ve lied to my mum and dragged you here for a room full of dead chargers.” “I’ve done worse for less.” I moved between the boxes. On a shelf, someone had lined up plastic tubs of donated cables. Underneath was a red school backpack, shoved behind a stack of printer paper. My stomach dropped before I touched it. The bag had a black marker line on the front pocket where Sasha had tried to write **SASHA** and run out of space. I unzipped it. Inside: a packet of plasters, an old nail file, a crushed cereal bar and a folded page torn from a notebook. My hands shook as I opened it. Sasha’s handwriting, hard and rushed. **Not greenhouse. He uses it to scare them. Real stuff moves through Quays. Jayden made collection. If I don’t come back, tell Niamh I was right.** I read it twice. Then Finn said, “Niamh.” He was crouched by the underside of a sorting table. Taped beneath it was a cheap black phone. He peeled it free. “Don’t turn it on,” I said. “I wasn’t going to.” “You were absolutely going to.” “I was considering it.” The screen lit anyway. A message thread was already open. **red bag at 14. 5pm.** **same route. no school uniform.** **tell J he owes me.** No names. No explanation. Just enough to make my skin crawl. The fire door opened behind us. Darren stood there, looking furious. “I said five minutes.” “We found this,” I said, holding up Sasha’s note. His expression changed when he saw the backpack. “Where did you get that?” “It was here.” “Gareth said he was sorting donations,” Darren said. “He said—” His phone rang. He looked at the screen and swore quietly. “We need to go,” I said. Darren looked between us, the bag, the burner phone. “You kids need to leave.” “Are you calling Gareth?” “No.” “Are you?” “I’m calling my manager.” “Don’t call Gareth.” “I said I’m not calling Gareth.” But he did not sound certain. Finn grabbed my elbow. “Niamh.” My phone was ringing. Sasha’s number. I answered so fast I nearly dropped it. “Sasha?” At first, there was only breathing. Thin and uneven. Then a scrape. Metal against concrete. I heard a man’s voice somewhere nearby, muffled but clear enough. “You can stop making this difficult.” The call cut off.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 4)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 4 of this darkly comic British YA fiction demonstrates considerable craft and largely delivers on its ambitious plan. Let me examine it across the relevant dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTER CONSISTENCY Niamh's voice is sharply rendered throughout. The opening kitchen scene captures her defensive competence perfectly: "I'm not five." / "No. You're seventeen and apparently investigating a missing-person case with a boy who runs a gossip account." The exchange about crisps and vodka lands as genuinely funny without feeling forced. Niamh's internal logic—"Because I was. Not officially. Not sensibly. But the first thing Sasha had done was text me, and I had looked at the screen and decided I had better things to do"—is emotionally precise and consistent with her established character profile. She is self-aware enough to feel guilt but not so reformed that she immediately becomes open. Finn's characterisation is also consistent: the gloves bit ("I brought gloves in case you touch things and I have to stop you doing it badly") captures his particular brand of anxious practicality. His inability to resist turning on the phone despite saying he wasn't going to is a small but telling character beat. Leena Patel reads authentically. "So is vodka" is exactly the kind of dry, practical maternal wit the profile establishes. Her anger expressed through efficiency—folding carrier bags, wiping clean counters—is shown rather than told, and consistent with her character notes. DIALOGUE The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. The exchange about the gloves is genuinely funny without being sitcom-broad. Darren the warehouse worker is a minor character who nonetheless feels real: "Congratulations" in response to "We're students" is a perfectly calibrated working-class deadpan. The "this wardrobe is haunted" interruption is a nice piece of ambient British comedy that doesn't overstay its welcome. The dialogue avoids the trap of being too witty or too expository; it feels like people actually talking. PLOT EXECUTION The chapter follows its plan closely. The confrontation with Leena, the trip to The Quays, the discovery of Sasha's bag and note, the burner phone, and the climactic phone call all land in the right order. The plan called for Darren to be "confused, angry" and to recognise Gareth's name while thinking he's collecting donated devices—this is executed faithfully. The chapter ends on the phone call cliffhanger as planned. The discovery sequence is well-paced. The red bag reveal is earned rather than melodramatic. Sasha's note—"If I don't come back, tell Niamh I was right"—is emotionally effective because it captures Sasha's voice (competitive, brave, slightly performative) while also being genuinely moving. SHOWING VS TELLING The chapter largely shows rather than tells. The kitchen scene establishes Leena's emotional state through action: the untouched tea with a skin on it, the stacked flashcards, the unnecessary counter-wiping. The description of The Quays—"a restaurant called Dock & Dram, which closed after six months because nobody in Mereford wanted to pay fourteen pounds for a burger served on a slate"—is specific, funny and world-building without being laboured. There are occasional moments where the narration becomes slightly more explanatory than necessary. The paragraph explaining The Quays' history is informative but slightly interrupts the chapter's momentum. It's not egregious, but it's the kind of passage where the author is doing work the reader could do themselves. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT The emotional core of the chapter—Niamh's guilt about ignoring the original text—is handled with restraint. "Because I was. Not officially. Not sensibly." This is the right level of interiority: present, honest, not overwrought. The chapter doesn't milk the guilt; it lets it sit underneath the action. The phone call ending is effective. "Thin and uneven" breathing, the scrape, the muffled voice: "You can stop making this difficult." This is Gareth's voice exactly as the profile describes—calm, administrative, entitled. The horror comes from the ordinariness of the phrasing. CREATIVITY AND SURPRISE The chapter is largely unsurprising in its plot mechanics—the discovery of the bag, the burner phone, the cliffhanger call are all telegraphed by the plan. However, the execution contains enough specific, observed detail to feel fresh. The "wardrobe is haunted" line, Darren's resigned competence, the description of The Quays as a failed regeneration scheme—these are the kinds of specific British details that elevate genre material. The "GCSE drama exam" line ("Less like you're buying drugs in a GCSE drama exam") is a good example of Niamh's voice being genuinely funny rather than generically witty. WEAKNESSES The chapter's main weakness is that it is somewhat mechanical in its plot delivery. The plan is followed closely, which means there are few genuine surprises. The warehouse scene, while well-written, proceeds fairly predictably from entry to discovery to near-capture. The chapter does what it needs to do without quite transcending it. The Quays description paragraph, while specific and well-observed, slightly slows the chapter's momentum at a point where it should be building. Darren's final uncertainty—"But he did not sound certain"—is a slightly weak note to end his scene on. It's telling rather than showing his ambivalence. PURPLE PROSE / FORCED METAPHOR The prose is clean and largely free of purple passages. There are no strained metaphors or overwrought imagery. The writing trusts its observations to do the work. "A skin had formed on the tea" is a precise, unglamorous image that earns its place. OVERALL This is a competent, often excellent chapter that executes its plan faithfully, maintains consistent character voices, and delivers genuine comedy alongside genuine tension. Its weaknesses are primarily those of a chapter that is doing its job rather than exceeding it—the plot mechanics are visible, the discoveries proceed as expected, and there are few moments of genuine surprise. But the voice is strong, the dialogue is sharp, and the emotional underpinning is handled with appropriate restraint. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 8 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 2 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 5

Ok now chapter 5. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
## Chapter 5: What Counts as Proof By the time we got back to school, the burner phone had become the most important object in the world. It sat inside a clear sandwich bag on Ms Bains’s desk because that was what she had available. Beside it were Sasha’s note, photographs of the list from Gareth’s office, and my phone, open to the missed call from Sasha’s number. The office smelled of tea and the lemon disinfectant the cleaners used in every room where someone had cried. Ms Bains had stopped saying things were fine. That was how I knew they weren’t. A police officer called PC Dwyer sat in the chair opposite her desk. He was younger than I expected, with rain on his shoulders and a notebook balanced on one knee. He had a patient face, which I immediately distrusted. “So,” he said, “you found the phone at the warehouse?” “In a room Gareth uses,” I said. “A room he has access to,” Ms Bains corrected gently. “In a room where Sasha’s bag was.” “That is significant,” PC Dwyer said. “It’s proof.” “It’s evidence,” he said. “We need to establish what it proves.” I stared at him. This was apparently the difference between adults and everyone else. Adults could look at a missing girl’s bag, a phone full of coded messages and a note saying *if I don’t come back*, then use a word like *establish*. “There was a call,” Finn said. He had been unusually quiet since The Quays. “From Sasha’s phone. We heard a man.” “Could you identify the man?” PC Dwyer asked. “Yes,” I said. Finn looked at me. I could not identify him. Not really. I could identify a calm voice in a bad situation. I could identify the way it had made my skin tighten. “It sounded like Gareth,” I said. PC Dwyer wrote something down. “Did he say his name?” “No.” “Did he make a threat?” “He said she was making things difficult.” “Could that have referred to—” “To what?” I said. “A book club?” Ms Bains put her hand flat on the desk. Not touching me. Just there. “Niamh.” I hated that I needed the reminder. PC Dwyer did not look offended. That was annoying too. “We’ve requested access to the warehouse CCTV,” he said. “We’re speaking to Mr Vale. We’re also looking at the phone and the messages you found. You did the right thing bringing these in.” The right thing. As if I had not spent the previous night doing the wrong thing in bed with my phone face-down. “What about Sasha?” I asked. “We’re looking for her.” “Where?” “If I knew that, I’d tell you.” It was the first useful thing he had said. Outside the office, Jayden was sitting on the plastic chair where the crying Year Eight had been yesterday. His green backpack was on his lap. His aunt stood nearby in a supermarket uniform, arms folded tight across her chest. Jayden looked at the floor. Ms Bains had called his family. Apparently his mum was asleep after a night shift, so his aunt had come instead. “Niamh,” Ms Bains said quietly, “perhaps give him some space.” But Jayden looked up. “You found the red bag?” he asked. “Yes.” His fingers tightened around the backpack straps. “Was there a phone?” “Yes.” He nodded once, like he had expected that. PC Dwyer moved closer, careful not to loom. “Jayden, we’d like to talk to you about The Bridge. You’re not in trouble.” Jayden laughed. It was a small sound. Not amused. “Everyone says that first.” His aunt put a hand on his shoulder. He flinched before he could stop himself. “What did Mr Vale ask you to do?” she said. Jayden’s face went blank. “Nothing.” “Jayden.” “Nothing.” “Nobody is angry,” Ms Bains said. “You’ll tell my mum,” he said. “Yes,” Ms Bains said. “We will need to.” “And everyone else.” “We will only share what we have to.” “He said you would.” Jayden’s voice cracked on the last word. “He said if I told anyone, you’d all find out about Malik. And Mum. And then it would be my fault.” Nobody spoke. Finn shifted beside me. I looked at him. His face had gone pale. “I’m sorry,” he said. Jayden did not look at him. Finn swallowed. “I posted something. About your brother. A while ago. I didn’t know where it came from, exactly, but that’s not—” He stopped, rubbing at his sleeve. “I deleted it. I know that doesn’t fix it.” Jayden looked up then. “You posted it?” Finn nodded. For one second, I thought Jayden might hit him. Instead, he stood so abruptly that the chair scraped the floor. “You’re all the same,” he said. Then he walked out with his aunt before anyone could stop him. Finn stared at the empty corridor. “Don’t,” I said. “Don’t what?” “Make it about you.” His mouth tightened. “I wasn’t.” “You always do. You collect people’s worst moments and then act surprised when they don’t want you near them.” “That isn’t fair.” “No? You knew Gareth was at the greenhouse. You knew he had a van. You knew things about Sasha, about Jayden, about everyone. You just didn’t do anything until it got interesting.” Finn looked as though I had slapped him, which was satisfying for about half a second. Then he said, quietly, “You ignored her.” The corridor seemed to tilt. He took a step back. “I know,” I said. “Yeah.” He left before I could say anything else. I went to Sasha’s house after school. Donna had texted Mum, who had texted me, because apparently every adult in Mereford had now joined a communication network designed specifically to ruin my life. Mum had said I could go for half an hour as long as I sent my location and did not leave the flat. Sasha’s building smelled of frying onions and damp concrete. The lift was broken, as usual, so I climbed three flights of stairs while rehearsing things I could say that were not *sorry I didn’t come*. Donna opened the door wearing leggings and one slipper. “You look soaked,” she said. “It’s raining.” “Come in, then. Don’t drip on the carpet. It’s already depressed.” The flat was too tidy. That was the first thing I noticed. Donna had cleaned in the way people clean when they need their hands to do something. The cushions were straight. The mugs had been washed. A washing basket sat in the hallway full of clothes nobody had folded. Donna made tea. I did not want tea. I drank it anyway. “He helped me,” she said, looking at the kettle rather than me. “Gareth?” She nodded. “When my hours got cut. He knew about grants and food parcels and all that. He filled in forms. Came round once with a bag of stuff from the warehouse. Pasta, cereal, toilet roll.” Her mouth twisted. “I felt grateful. That’s the stupid bit.” “It’s not stupid.” “It is if it means you don’t see what’s in front of you.” I thought of my phone glowing in the dark. Donna sat down opposite me. “Sasha told me he wanted her to do favours,” she said. “Pick things up. Take things places. I told her to stay away from him. She said she was sorting it.” She gave a short, angry laugh. “Like she was thirty-five and had a filing cabinet.” “That sounds like her.” “She said you’d know what to do.” The sentence hurt more than it should have. Donna wiped at her face with the heel of her hand. “Her room,” she said. “If there’s anything in there. Look.” Sasha’s room was exactly as she had left it: clothes over the chair, makeup scattered across the desk, a dead plant in the window. Her red hoodie was not there. On the shelf above her bed sat an old biscuit tin with a dent in the lid. I knew it immediately. Our emergency tin. At twelve, we had filled it with plasters, sweets, a torch, two pound coins and a list of codes in purple pen. Sasha had kept it. Of course she had. Inside, beneath a dead battery and a tiny plastic whistle, was a key. A luggage-tag label was tied to it. In Sasha’s handwriting: **NOT THE GREENHOUSE. THE SHED.** My phone buzzed. A new message from Sasha’s number. 🩸🩸🩸 Below it was a photograph. Blurry wet glass. The inside of the greenhouse.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 5)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 5 is a strong piece of YA fiction that largely delivers on its plan and maintains the voice established in the character profiles. Let me examine it carefully across the relevant dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTER CONSISTENCY Niamh's voice is well-maintained throughout. The narration is sharp, observational and self-aware without being smug. Lines like "PC Dwyer did not look offended. That was annoying too" and "Adults could look at a missing girl's bag, a phone full of coded messages and a note saying if I don't come back, then use a word like establish" capture her character precisely: the frustration with institutional language, the impatience with process, the dark humour. The internal acknowledgment "I could not identify him. Not really" shows her self-awareness without being heavy-handed. Finn's confrontation with Jayden and then with Niamh is handled well. His apology to Jayden is awkward and insufficient in exactly the right way, and Niamh's attack on him ("You collect people's worst moments and then act surprised when they don't want you near them") is both fair and unfair simultaneously, which is true to her character. His quiet counter-punch—"You ignored her"—is the chapter's best moment of dialogue. It lands because it's earned, brief, and devastating. Jayden's response to Finn's confession is excellent. The near-violence, the "You're all the same," the departure—these feel authentic to a fifteen-year-old who has been systematically betrayed by adults and peers alike. His earlier line "Everyone says that first" in response to "You're not in trouble" is a small gem. Donna's scene is well-executed. Her shame about accepting Gareth's help is rendered without melodrama. "I felt grateful. That's the stupid bit" is exactly the kind of self-recrimination that feels real. The detail of the too-tidy flat—"Donna had cleaned in the way people clean when they need their hands to do something"—is a good show-don't-tell moment. DIALOGUE The dialogue is generally strong. PC Dwyer's procedural language ("We need to establish what it proves") is used effectively to dramatise institutional frustration without making him a cartoon. The exchange about "establish" versus "proof" is sharp. The Finn/Niamh confrontation is the chapter's emotional peak and is handled with restraint—neither character gets a clean win. One minor weakness: Niamh's attack on Finn ("You always do. You collect people's worst moments...") is slightly over-articulated for the heat of the moment. It reads a touch like a prepared speech rather than something said in anger. Real anger tends to be less perfectly formulated. This is a small flaw in an otherwise strong scene. PLOT AND STRUCTURE The chapter follows its plan closely. The evidence-gathering aftermath, Jayden's refusal to speak, Finn's confession about the gossip post, the fracture between Finn and Niamh, Donna's scene, the discovery of the tin and key, and the final blood-emoji text with the greenhouse photo—all are present and executed in roughly the planned order. The chapter earns its ending because the tin is set up with genuine emotional weight (Sasha kept it; of course she did), and the final image of the blurry greenhouse glass is appropriately ominous without being overwrought. The pacing is good. The police interview section moves efficiently, the Jayden scene escalates naturally, and the Donna scene provides necessary emotional texture before the final discovery. SHOW VS TELL The chapter mostly shows rather than tells. The too-tidy flat, Jayden's flinch at his aunt's touch, Finn's pale face—these are concrete details doing emotional work. There are occasional moments where the narration explains what we've already understood ("The sentence hurt more than it should have" after Donna says Sasha thought Niamh would know what to do), but these are minor. PROSE QUALITY The prose is clean and controlled. There are no purple passages or forced metaphors. The one slightly strained image is "The office smelled of tea and the lemon disinfectant the cleaners used in every room where someone had cried"—this is trying a little hard, though it's not egregious. "The corridor seemed to tilt" after Finn's counter-accusation is effective and restrained. The chapter avoids the trap of over-poeticising. The humour is present but not forced—the "book club" line, Donna's "it's already depressed" about the carpet, the observation about PC Dwyer's patience being annoying. These feel organic to the voice. DARKNESS AND LIGHTNESS The chapter handles its tonal balance well. Donna's dark humour about the carpet is exactly the kind of British deflection that feels true. The chapter doesn't make Sasha's captivity funny, but it allows ordinary life to persist around the fear. The balance is earned. WEAKNESSES The main weakness is that Niamh's speech attacking Finn is slightly too polished for the moment. Additionally, the chapter's emotional beats are somewhat predictable in sequence—the institutional frustration, the confession, the fracture, the maternal scene, the discovery—though each is executed with enough specificity to feel fresh. The chapter is perhaps slightly too tidy in its emotional architecture for a story that explicitly aims to avoid tidiness, but this is a minor criticism. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 6

Ok now chapter 6. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
## Chapter 6: Come Anyway For about three seconds, I could not move. The photograph was dark and blurry, rain silvering the glass. But I knew the greenhouse. I knew the cracked pane with the brown tape, the dead vines, the yellow warning sign by the maintenance gate. Sasha was there. Or she had been there. The timestamp said 6:14 p.m. It was now 6:23. “Niamh?” Donna said from the doorway. “What is it?” I showed her. She went pale in a way that seemed to drain the room of colour. “Call your mum,” she said. “I’m calling Ms Bains.” “Call your mum as well.” The fact that she said it twice made me listen. My fingers shook so badly I hit the wrong contact first. Then Ms Bains answered on the second ring. “Niamh?” “I’ve got another message. Sasha’s at the greenhouse. I think. There’s a photo.” “Send it to me now. Have you told your mother?” “I’m doing it.” “Do not go there.” “I know.” “Promise me.” “I promise.” It was not technically a lie. I had not gone there yet. Mum answered with, “Have you eaten?” then heard my silence. “What’s happened?” I told her. Fast, badly, leaving out none of it this time: the photo, the timestamp, the key, the note in the emergency tin. By the end, I was standing in Sasha’s hallway with Donna beside me and the biscuit tin pressed against my ribs like it could keep something from getting worse. Mum’s voice changed. “Stay where you are.” “But—” “Stay. Where. You. Are. I am leaving now.” “Police?” “Ms Bains has called them. I’m calling too. I want you in that flat.” “I know.” “No, you don’t. You keep saying that.” The line went dead. Donna looked at me. “You’re going anyway,” she said. I hated that she knew. “I just need to check.” “Your mum said no.” “She’s not here.” “Neither is Sasha.” That stopped me. Donna picked up her coat from the hook. “I’m coming.” “No. You should stay in case—” “In case she comes home?” Donna’s mouth wobbled. “Love, if she was coming home, she’d have come home.” Her phone buzzed. She looked at it, then cursed. “It’s work,” she said. “I’ve got to call in. If I don’t—” She stopped, disgusted with herself. “I’m sorry. I’m sorry.” “You don’t have to be sorry.” “I do.” She sat down hard on the arm of the sofa, dialling her manager with one hand and covering her face with the other. I took my coat. Outside, rain slapped cold against my cheeks. I had got as far as the pavement when a figure detached itself from the bus shelter opposite. Finn. He held up both hands before I could speak. “I know. You’re annoyed.” “I’m busy.” “I know. That’s why I’m here.” “You’re here because you saw my location?” “No.” He glanced away. “I was walking past.” “Finn, you live nowhere near here.” “I was walking past in an emotionally complicated direction.” I should have told him to go away. I should have obeyed Mum, waited for the police, sat in Sasha’s flat with Donna and watched the clock crawl. Instead I said, “She sent a photo from the greenhouse.” His face changed. “Recent?” “Ten minutes ago.” He looked at the road. “We need to tell someone.” “I did.” “And?” “They’re coming.” “So are we, apparently.” “Don’t make that sound noble. It’s stupid.” “Fine. It’s stupid. But you’re going, and I don’t fancy reading about you on my own account tomorrow.” It was a terrible joke. He knew it. I knew it. He looked down at the pavement. “I brought something,” he said. From his backpack, he took out a folded stack of printed screenshots. “What is that?” “Messages sent to the account. Tips. Anonymous stuff.” He swallowed. “Some of them came from numbers I didn’t recognise. They were always about the same people. Sasha. Jayden. A girl called Talia Mills. Things that made them look bad. Like they were stealing, lying, skipping school.” “Gareth sent them?” “I don’t know. But the timing matches when people started going to The Bridge. And I checked the number from one of the messages against a screenshot from the burner phone. Same last four digits.” I stared at the papers, rain soaking the edges. “You could have brought this to Ms Bains.” “I did. I emailed it. Five minutes ago.” That surprised me enough that I said nothing. “I’m not asking you to forgive me,” Finn added. “I just thought you should know.” Mum’s car turned the corner too fast and stopped half on the kerb. The passenger door flew open. “Get in,” she said. Finn blinked. “Me?” “You too. Unless you’re going to stand there looking decorative.” We got in. Mum had changed out of her work cardigan but still wore her pharmacy lanyard, swinging from the rear-view mirror like a very stressed air freshener. Her face was set in the particular way it did when someone at the chemist tried to argue that antibiotics were “basically vitamins.” “I spoke to Ms Bains,” she said as she pulled away. “Police are on their way to the school. We are not entering anything. We are checking the gate and waiting.” “Okay,” I said. She looked at me in the mirror. “That was too quick.” “I mean it.” “You had better.” The school car park was full. Parents’ evening. Rows of cars packed around the main entrance, teachers visible through the lit windows, gesturing at folders while parents held wet coats over their arms. The normality of it made me furious. Somewhere inside, somebody was probably discussing predicted grades while Sasha sat in a shed. We parked by the sports hall. Mum locked the car. “Stay with me.” The maintenance gate was shut but not padlocked. Rain ran down the fence in bright lines under the security light. Beyond it, the greenhouse hunched at the end of the yard, its glass glowing faintly from the sports hall lights. The potting-shed key was cold in my hand. Mum saw it. “Niamh.” “Sasha left it.” “That does not mean you open the door.” “It might.” Finn hovered beside us, soaked through and very quiet. We waited. One minute. Two. A car door slammed somewhere beyond the sports hall. I looked at the photo again. 6:14. The message. 6:18. What if she had only had a few minutes? What if Gareth had moved her already? What if waiting was the sensible thing and sensible things were how people disappeared? “I’m just checking,” I said. Mum caught my wrist. “Niamh.” “I have to.” Her grip loosened. Not agreement. Not permission. Just fear. The key slid into the shed lock. It turned. Inside, the potting shed was empty. There were shelves of cracked plant pots, bags of soil and a broken wheelbarrow tipped on its side. The air smelled damp and metallic. “Sasha?” I called. Nothing. Then Finn said, “Phone.” Under the bench, taped to the wood, was Sasha’s phone. The screen was cracked across the middle. A draft voice note sat open. I pressed play. Static. A breath. Then Sasha, whispering. “If you get this, he’s taking me to the place with the red bags. Not the unit. The—” The recording cut off. “The warehouse,” Mum said. “The loading bay,” I said. I remembered the red bags hanging by the fire door. The rear entrance. The grey door beside the delivery area. Behind us, someone said my name. Not loudly. Not angrily. “Niamh.” We turned. Gareth Vale stood at the greenhouse door in his navy fleece, rain darkening the shoulders. He looked exhausted. Not guilty. That was somehow worse.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 6)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 6 is a tightly constructed piece of YA thriller writing that largely succeeds on its own terms. Let me examine it across the relevant dimensions. VOICE AND CHARACTER Niamh's voice is consistent and well-calibrated throughout. The narration maintains her characteristic blend of self-awareness and self-deception: she promises Ms Bains she won't go, then immediately qualifies it internally ("It was not technically a lie. I had not gone there yet."). This is exactly the kind of precise, slightly self-serving reasoning the character profile demands. Her competence-as-armour is present without being laboured. Finn's appearance is handled well. His explanation of the screenshots—arriving with evidence already emailed to Ms Bains—represents genuine character development from the fractured partnership of Chapter 5. The line "I'm not asking you to forgive me. I just thought you should know" is earned and consistent with his profile: defensive, guilt-ridden, but capable of action when it matters. His joke about reading about Niamh on his own account is exactly the kind of terrible-joke-he-knows-is-terrible that the character profile describes. Leena Patel is rendered with real economy. The pharmacy lanyard "swinging from the rear-view mirror like a very stressed air freshener" is a genuinely good image—specific, funny, character-revealing without being forced. Her "Have you eaten?" greeting under stress is drawn directly from the character profile and lands naturally. Her grip loosening—"Not agreement. Not permission. Just fear."—is the chapter's most emotionally precise moment. Donna's brief appearance is effective. Her line "In case she comes home? Love, if she was coming home, she'd have come home" is quietly devastating and shows a mother who has moved past hope into a different kind of grief. Her being pulled away by work is a small, realistic detail that avoids making her either heroic or villainous. PLOT AND STRUCTURE The chapter follows its plan closely. The key beats are all present: Niamh calls Ms Bains and her mother immediately (the behavioural change from Chapter 1), Finn arrives with the screenshot archive and his apology, Leena drives them to school rather than letting them go alone, the potting shed is found empty with Sasha's phone, the voice note redirects them to the Quays, and Gareth appears at the end. The chapter plan is executed with fidelity and intelligence. The pacing is well-managed. The chapter moves quickly without feeling rushed. The parents' evening detail—"somebody was probably discussing predicted grades while Sasha sat in a shed"—is exactly the kind of British-mundane-against-danger observation the writing plan calls for, and it works. The ending is strong. Gareth's appearance is rendered with the correct register: "Not loudly. Not angrily." The final line—"Not guilty. That was somehow worse."—is effective and consistent with the plan's instruction that his danger comes from entitlement and apparent normality rather than theatrical menace. DIALOGUE The dialogue is generally strong. The exchange between Niamh and her mother on the phone is particularly good—the rhythm of "Stay where you are / But— / Stay. Where. You. Are" feels authentic. Finn's "emotionally complicated direction" is a nice character beat. The dialogue avoids the twin pitfalls of being either too expository or too clipped. One minor weakness: Donna's "You're going anyway" followed by "I'm coming" is slightly too neat—it resolves her character's tension a little too efficiently before the work call interrupts. But this is a small complaint. IMAGERY AND PROSE The prose is clean and controlled. Images are used sparingly and tend to land: the lanyard as stressed air freshener, rain running down the fence "in bright lines," the greenhouse "hunching." The writing avoids purple prose almost entirely. There are no strained metaphors or associative riffs that don't earn their place. The one moment that slightly overreaches is "the biscuit tin pressed against my ribs like it could keep something from getting worse"—this is a touch on-the-nose in its symbolism, though not egregiously so. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT The chapter is emotionally engaging. The tension between Niamh's promise not to go and her going anyway is handled with enough self-awareness to avoid making her seem simply reckless. The voice note from Sasha—brief, cut off, whispering—is more affecting than a dramatic confrontation would have been. The empty shed is the right kind of disappointment: not a dead end, but a redirection that maintains urgency. LIGHTNESS AND DARKNESS The darkly comic register is maintained appropriately. The humour (the lanyard, Finn's "emotionally complicated direction," Leena's antibiotics-as-vitamins face) never trivialises the danger. The chapter earns its lighter moments because they are character-specific rather than tonal decoration. WEAKNESSES The chapter is perhaps slightly too smooth in its execution. Every beat lands, every character behaves consistently, every piece of information arrives at the right moment. This is a strength in terms of craft but occasionally feels slightly mechanical—the chapter plan is followed so faithfully that there are few genuine surprises within the chapter itself. The Gareth ending, while well-executed, is exactly what the plan promised, which slightly reduces its impact. The voice note from Sasha cutting off mid-sentence is a well-worn thriller device, though it's deployed competently here. Overall, this is confident, controlled YA writing that serves its characters, its plot and its tonal brief with considerable skill. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 3 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 4 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 17 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 16

Chapter 7

Ok now chapter 7. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
## Chapter 7: The Loading Bay For a second, nobody moved. Rain ticked against the greenhouse roof. Somewhere in the sports hall, a child laughed too loudly and was shushed. Gareth stood in the doorway with one hand on the frame, blocking the only easy way out. He looked at Mum first. “Leena,” he said, as if they had arranged to meet for coffee. “I’m glad you’re here.” Mum’s face changed when he used her name. I realised, with a cold little jolt, that of course he knew her. Mereford was small. He had probably collected prescriptions from the chemist, asked for advice about hay fever, made her feel like a useful person doing a useful job. “Where is Sasha?” Mum said. Gareth exhaled through his nose. “She’s safe.” “Where?” “This has got out of hand.” “That is not an answer.” He looked at me then. His expression was tired, disappointed, almost kind. “Niamh, you’ve misunderstood a few things.” “You took her.” “No.” “You locked her in here.” “I didn’t.” “You told her to stop making things difficult.” “I was trying to stop her making a bad situation worse.” The words came out smooth and ready. He had said them before. Maybe to Jayden. Maybe to Sasha. Maybe to himself in the mirror. Mum took out her phone. “I’m calling the police,” she said. “I think that would be a mistake.” She stared at him. “I’m sorry?” “Not for you. For these kids. Once this becomes public, people start saying things. Jayden’s family. Sasha’s mum. You know what it’s like in a town like this.” “You are not concerned about their privacy,” Mum said. “You are concerned about being caught.” Gareth’s jaw tightened. Finn shifted beside me. I could hear his breathing, too fast and shallow. Gareth looked at him. “And you. I would have thought you’d understand the damage rumours do.” Finn went still. It was a clever thing to say. That was the problem with Gareth. He could find the exact soft bit in someone and press it with one finger. Mum put the phone on speaker. “Police,” said a woman’s voice. “What is your emergency?” Gareth stepped back. Not running. Not threatening us. Just taking one calm step out of the doorway. “I’m going to make sure Sasha is all right,” he said. “You’re not going anywhere,” Mum said. But he was already moving. He crossed the maintenance yard quickly, climbed into his van and reversed hard enough to spray mud against the fence. By the time Mum got through to give the operator the registration number, he was gone. Finn looked at me. “The Quays.” “I know.” Mum ended the call. “We wait for the police.” “He’s taking her away,” I said. “We know where he’s gone.” “We think we do.” “Niamh.” “He’s going to move the phones. The bags. Her. Everything.” Mum’s mouth tightened. She looked towards the road, then at the dark greenhouse behind us. It was not a dramatic decision. Nobody said we had no choice. We had several choices, all bad. Mum got in the car. “Seatbelts,” she said. The Quays was less than ten minutes away if you ignored several traffic laws and most of the town’s road markings. Mum did neither, though the car took corners with a sharpness that made Finn grip the door handle. “You’re driving like Dad,” I said. “That is not a compliment.” “I know.” Finn was on the phone to Ms Bains, speaking in short bursts. “Gareth left the school. Navy van. We’re following at a distance. No, not close. Mrs Patel is driving. Yes, she knows. Yes, I know that sounds bad.” Mum held out one hand without taking her eyes off the road. “Give me the phone.” Finn passed it over. “Amrita, it’s Leena. We’re heading to the Quays. Unit Fourteen, by the furniture warehouse. We are not going in unless we have to.” She listened. “Yes. I know. I know. Police are coming?” She glanced at me in the mirror. “Good.” The Quays appeared ahead, all wet glass and dead shopfronts under the orange lights. The warehouse was mostly dark. Its main shutters were down, but a delivery lorry idled at the loading bay, exhaust coughing into the rain. Gareth’s van was parked sideways across the rear exit. “There,” I said. Mum stopped the car behind a stack of recycling bins. “No one gets out until I say.” We all got out immediately. Mum swore under her breath. The loading bay smelled of diesel, damp cardboard and the river. A warehouse man in a high-vis jacket stood near the lorry, arguing into his phone. “I said I’m still waiting on the paperwork,” he was saying. “No, I can’t just send it, can I? I’m stood in the rain, Darren.” He saw us. “We’re closed.” “Is Gareth Vale here?” Mum asked. The man looked towards the van. “Yeah. Said there was an issue with some donated kit.” “Is there a girl inside?” I said. He stared at me. “What?” “A girl. Seventeen. Blonde hair. Red hoodie.” His face altered. “I heard something,” he said. “Thought it was—” A bang came from inside the building. Not loud. Metal against metal. Then another. “Sasha!” I shouted. Mum grabbed for my arm, but I was already at the fire door. It was locked. Through the narrow wired-glass panel, I could see a corridor and, at the far end, a red charity bag on the floor. It moved. “Sasha!” I shouted again. A hand hit the glass from the other side. The warehouse man came running. “Move,” he said. He shoved a key into the lock, twisted it, then swore. “Door’s jammed.” “Open it,” I said. “Very helpful.” Finn was staring at the fire alarm box on the wall. “Could pull that,” he said. “That’ll trigger the shutters,” the man said. “And the alarm.” “Good,” Finn said. He smashed the plastic cover with his elbow before anyone could stop him. The alarm began as a chirp and turned into a full-bodied scream. Inside, shutters rattled. Lights flickered on. Somewhere, a siren started wailing over the warehouse speaker system. The fire door released with a clunk. Mum pushed it open. The corridor beyond was narrow and lined with donation sacks. At the end, a back office door stood half-open. Sasha was behind it, wedged against the frame, one hand pressed to the glass panel. Her red hoodie was dark with rain and dirt. There was blood on her knuckles. “Niamh,” she said. I ran to her. The door had been blocked from the outside with a metal filing cabinet. The warehouse man and Mum dragged it away together, scraping it across the floor. Sasha stumbled out. She was shaking. That was the first thing I noticed. Not crying. Not screaming. Just shaking so hard her teeth clicked once when she tried to speak. Mum wrapped her coat around her shoulders. “Are you hurt?” she asked. “My hand.” “Anywhere else?” Sasha shook her head. Then Gareth appeared at the far end of the corridor carrying a cardboard box. Phones. I could see them through the open top. Cheap black handsets, chargers, paperwork. He stopped when he saw us. The alarm screamed on. For a moment, he looked less like a dangerous man than an exhausted one standing in a warehouse full of rubbish. Then he said, “This is not what it looks like.” Finn laughed. It came out sharp and strange. “Really?” he said. “Because it looks extremely like a man locking a girl in an office.” Gareth’s gaze moved to Sasha. “She took confidential material. She was frightened. I was trying to keep her safe until we could sort it out.” “You blocked the door,” I said. “She was trying to leave.” “Yes,” I said. “That’s generally what people do.” He stepped towards us. Mum moved in front of Sasha without thinking. The warehouse man did too. His high-vis jacket was bright under the flickering lights. “You need to put that down, mate,” he said. Gareth looked at the box. Then at the loading bay behind him. The delivery lorry had shifted during the alarm. Its rear doors now filled the exit. Outside, blue lights washed across the wet concrete. Gareth’s shoulders dropped. Jayden came running in from the main warehouse with his aunt behind him. He stopped when he saw Gareth. Nobody asked him to speak. He did anyway. “You made me do it,” he said. His voice was small. It carried through the alarm. Gareth’s face changed. “Jayden—” “You said you’d tell everyone.” “Jayden, this isn’t—” “You said about my mum. And Malik. You said nobody would believe me.” His aunt put both hands on his shoulders. Jayden looked at Gareth and did not look away. “I’m telling.” Police officers came through the loading bay door. Gareth did not run. He set the box down carefully, as though that mattered. Then he raised his hands.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 7)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 7 is the climactic confrontation chapter, and it largely succeeds at what it sets out to do while maintaining the story's commitment to credible, unglamorous danger. Let me assess it across multiple dimensions. VOICE AND NARRATION Niamh's voice is consistently maintained throughout. The narration is sharp, observational and grounded. Lines like "He had said them before. Maybe to Jayden. Maybe to Sasha. Maybe to himself in the mirror" demonstrate the close first-person interiority the plan called for. The voice avoids melodrama while still conveying genuine fear. The small comic beats — "You're driving like Dad" / "That is not a compliment" / "I know" — land naturally and feel earned rather than forced. The warehouse man's phone argument about Darren and the paperwork is a perfect piece of British mundane texture that the plan specifically called for. DIALOGUE The dialogue is one of the chapter's strongest elements. Gareth's lines are exactly as planned: smooth, deflecting, designed to make others feel childish. "This has got out of hand" and "She was trying to leave" / "Yes. That's generally what people do" is a genuinely good exchange. Gareth's attempt to weaponise Finn's guilt ("I would have thought you'd understand the damage rumours do") is chilling precisely because it's so economical. The dialogue avoids the villain-explains-everything trap the plan warned against. Jayden's testimony — "You made me do it" / "You said about my mum. And Malik. You said nobody would believe me" — is appropriately fragmented and emotionally devastating without being overwrought. Sasha's first words being "Niamh" rather than something dramatic, and then her first full sentence being "You took your time" (as planned) — this is handled well, though the plan specified "You took your time" as the end beat and the chapter actually ends on Gareth raising his hands. The plan's end beat is moved earlier in the chapter, which is a minor deviation but works structurally. PLOT EXECUTION The chapter follows the plan closely. Gareth confronts them at the greenhouse, tries to talk his way out, leaves when Mum calls police, they follow to the Quays, find Sasha locked in the back office, Finn pulls the fire alarm, Jayden arrives and speaks. The plan is executed with fidelity. The detail of the delivery lorry blocking the exit is a nice piece of environmental problem-solving that avoids the "convenient gadgets" trap. The chapter avoids the "climactic pile-up of convenient gadgets, alarms, police and comic mishaps" the plan warned against — though the fire alarm is pulled, it's done with a degree of self-awareness (the warehouse man's "Very helpful" response to Niamh's "Open it" is good). The confrontation is messy rather than choreographed, as intended. CHARACTERISATION Gareth is rendered exactly as planned: ordinary, exhausted, using institutional language as a weapon. His final gesture — setting the box down carefully "as though that mattered" — is a quietly excellent character beat. Mum moving in front of Sasha without thinking is consistent with her profile. Jayden's testimony is handled with appropriate fragility and courage. Finn's laugh coming out "sharp and strange" is a good character note. EMOTIONAL ENGAGEMENT The chapter is emotionally effective. The moment Sasha's hand hits the glass from the other side is genuinely tense. The description of Sasha shaking so hard "her teeth clicked once when she tried to speak" is precise and affecting without being overwrought. The reunion is appropriately understated — Mum wrapping her coat around Sasha's shoulders, asking "Are you hurt?" — which is consistent with Leena's character profile (love appearing as preparation). WEAKNESSES The chapter is very competent but occasionally tips toward the expected. The fire alarm solution, while grounded, is a fairly standard thriller beat. The blue lights washing across wet concrete at the end is a slightly generic image. "His shoulders dropped" when Gareth sees the lorry blocking the exit is a touch on-the-nose. The chapter doesn't quite reach the level of genuine surprise or creative distinction that would push it into masterpiece territory — it executes the plan faithfully and well, but rarely exceeds it. The line "He looked less like a dangerous man than an exhausted one standing in a warehouse full of rubbish" is good but the "warehouse full of rubbish" qualifier slightly undercuts the image. "Rain ticked against the greenhouse roof" is a solid opening image. There's no significant purple prose or forced metaphor — the writing is disciplined. The plan specified the end beat as Sasha saying "You took your time" — this is actually delivered mid-chapter rather than as the closing line, which is a deviation. The chapter ends on Gareth raising his hands, which is effective but slightly anticlimactic as a final image compared to the planned Sasha line. OVERALL This is a well-executed chapter that honours the plan's commitment to unglamorous, credible danger, consistent characterisation and earned emotional beats. The dialogue is strong, the voice is consistent, and the humour is appropriately placed. It doesn't quite transcend its genre or its plan, but it executes both with genuine skill. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 16 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 4 Tell-Don't-Show: 4 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 7 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 2 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 3 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 16 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 8

Ok now finish the story with chapter 8. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
## Chapter 8: One Reply By Monday, the school had sent an email. It arrived at 7:12 a.m., because school emails always arrived at moments designed to make breakfast inedible. **Dear Parents and Carers,** **We are aware of a recent safeguarding matter involving an external community partner. The welfare of our students remains our absolute priority. Support is available for any student affected.** There were three more paragraphs about confidentiality, ongoing investigations and not spreading speculation online. At the bottom was a link to a wellbeing leaflet featuring a stock-photo girl in a yellow jumper staring optimistically at a fern. Mum read it over my shoulder while making tea. “‘Recent safeguarding matter,’” she said. “That’s one way of putting it.” “Maybe they ran out of words.” “They had plenty. They just chose the least useful ones.” The town Facebook group had plenty too. MEREFORD COMMUNITY CHAT had become an emergency ward full of people shouting incorrect information at each other. According to various posts, Sasha had been found in a warehouse in Leeds, Gareth had escaped to Spain, the school greenhouse contained “a drugs operation,” and the police had confiscated seventeen laptops from a secret bunker beneath the Methodist hall. Someone blamed teenagers. Someone blamed the council. Someone blamed the fact the old bus station had been demolished in 2009. Mum made a noise of disgust and put her phone face-down. “No more,” she said. “I wasn’t reading it.” “You were hovering.” “I was assessing public misinformation.” “You were hovering with intent.” I took my tea upstairs. My bedroom looked exactly as it had the morning Sasha disappeared: flashcards, cold mug stain on the desk, half-open history book. It annoyed me that the room had not changed. The world had managed to become awful, and my laundry was still on the chair where I had left it. I opened my phone. There was a message from Finn. **they found the account.** Below it: **ms bains told police. obviously.** Then, after a few minutes: **fair enough.** I typed several things. *Are you okay?* Too kind, perhaps. *You deserve it.* True, but not useful. Eventually I wrote: **what happens now?** His reply took longer than usual. **delete it. give them passwords. probably get suspended. dad is doing that silent thing where he’s more disappointed than angry.** I imagined Finn’s dad standing in the kitchen with his arms folded, saying nothing while Finn filled the silence with increasingly bad jokes. **you should have deleted it ages ago,** I wrote. **yes.** **but you sent the screenshots.** Another pause. **doesn’t cancel it out.** **no.** That was all. It felt insufficient. Most true things did. At school, everybody was pretending not to stare at me. This was worse than being stared at openly. Teachers spoke softly when I entered rooms. People I barely knew said things like, “You all right?” with the expectant expression of people hoping you would say yes so they could stop feeling awkward. The Bridge was closed. A paper sign had been taped to its front door: **TEMPORARILY UNAVAILABLE.** As if it were a library printer. Ms Bains told us in assembly that there would be support available, that nobody should feel pressured to talk, and that rumours could cause further harm. She looked tired. More tired than usual. Afterwards, I found her outside her office, holding a stack of forms and a travel mug the colour of old toothpaste. “Did you know?” I asked. It came out harsher than I meant. She looked at me for a long moment. “Not what he was doing,” she said. “You trusted him.” “Yes.” The answer stopped me. “I trusted The Bridge because it helped students,” she continued. “It did help students. That makes this harder, not easier. Good things can be used badly by people who know they will be believed.” I wanted to say something clever. Something that proved I understood institutional failure and adult complacency. Instead I said, “I’m sorry.” Ms Bains adjusted the folders in her arms. “You do not need to apologise to me.” “I should have told someone sooner.” “Yes,” she said. There was no softness in it. Not cruelty either. Then she added, “You did tell someone. You kept telling people. That matters too.” It was not forgiveness. It was probably better. Sasha came home on Wednesday. Not back to school. Home. Donna texted Mum, who told me in the kitchen while slicing peppers for dinner. “She said you can visit,” Mum said. “Today?” “If you want.” “What if she doesn’t actually want?” Mum looked at me. “You could ask.” This was a deeply annoying thing for her to say, mainly because it was sensible. I bought salt-and-vinegar crisps, a bottle of orange squash and a packet of plasters, though Mum made me put the plasters back because Sasha had enough medical supplies without me turning up like a badly run minor-injuries unit. At Sasha’s door, I stood for nearly five minutes without knocking. Then Donna opened it from inside. “You going to come in?” she asked. “Or are you haunting the landing now?” Sasha was on the sofa under a blanket, one hand bandaged. Her hair was tied up in a messy knot. She looked pale and thinner somehow, though that might have been because I had spent days imagining her behind warehouse doors. The television was on mute. Some daytime programme showed a man arguing about conservatories. “Hi,” I said. “Hi.” I held up the crisps. “I brought these.” “Brave choice. My mouth tastes like hospital.” “I also brought orange squash.” “That’s less brave.” I sat in the armchair opposite her because sitting beside her felt like pretending everything was normal. For a while, neither of us spoke. Donna clattered around in the kitchen, loudly not listening. Sasha looked at my hands. “You still doing that thing?” she said. “What thing?” “Trying to peel your own fingerprints off when you’re stressed.” I stopped pressing my thumbnail into my finger. “Apparently.” She nodded. Then she looked at the television. “You took your time.” It was not funny, exactly. “I know.” Sasha’s face twisted. For a second I thought she might cry. Then she laughed once, sharply. “I sent the code.” “I know.” “You knew what it meant.” “I know.” “I thought you’d come.” The words landed without drama. That made them worse. “I should have,” I said. “I was angry. And I thought…” I stopped. There was no version of the sentence that did not sound terrible. “I thought you were making it into something. I decided you were before I even asked.” Sasha picked at the edge of her bandage. “You were right about some things.” I waited. “I do that,” she said. “Make it loud when I want someone to care. Sometimes I don’t know how to ask without sounding pathetic.” “You don’t sound pathetic.” “I know you think I do.” “I don’t.” She looked at me. I took a breath. “Sometimes I think you make me responsible for everything. And I hate it. But that doesn’t mean I get to ignore you when you say you’re scared.” Sasha’s mouth pulled sideways. “That was nearly emotionally healthy.” “Don’t ruin it.” “No, sorry. Go on.” “I’m sorry,” I said. She nodded once. Not forgiveness. Not yet. “Okay,” she said. It was enough to make my eyes sting, which was unacceptable. I looked down at the crisps. After a while, Sasha said, “Jayden asked about you.” “What did he say?” “He said you were horrible but useful.” “That’s basically a compliment.” “He said you’d say that.” “Then he knows me quite well for someone who’s spoken to me three times.” “He notices things.” “So do I.” Sasha glanced at me. “Yeah,” she said. “Sometimes.” On my way out, Donna handed me the old biscuit tin. “For you,” she said. The lid was scratched and dented. In purple pen, twelve-year-old Sasha had written: **RED ALERT RULES** 1. NO PARENTS 2. NO SNITCHING 3. BRING SNACKS 4. DON’T DIE Underneath, in smaller writing, was a new addition. **5. ANSWER YOUR PHONE, IDIOT** I looked at Sasha. She gave me a tiny smile. “Fair,” I said. A week later, I was at my desk trying to revise the causes of the English Civil War when my phone lit up. A message from Sasha. **can you answer** My chest tightened before I could stop it. I answered immediately. **yes. what happened?** The typing dots appeared. Stopped. Appeared again. Then: **cut my thumb opening beans. one blood. not three.** A photograph followed. Her thumb wrapped in kitchen roll. A tin opener on the counter like a murder weapon. I stared at it for a moment. Then I wrote: **i’m coming round. keep the tin opener where i can see it.** Sasha replied with a single thumbs-up. I grabbed my coat. “Mum,” I called, heading for the stairs. “Where are you going?” “Sasha cut herself.” “Badly?” “One blood.” Mum paused. Then, from the kitchen: “Take plasters. And eat before you go.” I took the plasters. I went anyway.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 8)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] Chapter 8 is the denouement of a darkly comic British YA mystery, and it largely succeeds at what it sets out to do: provide emotional resolution without false tidiness, maintain the voice established throughout, and honour the complexity of the characters as designed. VOICE AND STYLE The narrative voice is consistently Niamh's: dry, observant, self-aware without being self-congratulatory. The Facebook group passage is genuinely funny and specific ("Someone blamed the fact the old bus station had been demolished in 2009"), and it earns its comedy because it's grounded in recognisable British social behaviour rather than generic satire. The email opening—"school emails always arrived at moments designed to make breakfast inedible"—is a small, precise observation that works. The voice doesn't strain for effect; it finds humour in the texture of ordinary life, which is exactly what the plan called for. The prose is clean and controlled.
