Be explicit about the choices you have made about plot, voice, stylistic choices, things you intend to aim for & avoid.> # Chapter Planning <Write a brief chapter plan for all {n_chapters} chapters.>
Model Output:
# Intention This will be an eight-chapter contemporary romance set in **Porth Awen**, a fictional small coastal town in West Wales. The setting will feel lived-in rather than picturesque by default: wet pavements, cramped parking, unreliable deliveries, bilingual notices, harbour gossip, tourist-season irritation, damp boxes of donated books, and a community where privacy is both protected and impossible. The central location is **The Foxed Page**, a small, slightly chaotic second-hand bookshop owned by **Elin Morgan**. The shop is not a magical refuge in abstract terms; it is Elin’s workplace, responsibility, inheritance, and occasional burden. She knows the regulars’ reading habits, keeps unofficial tabs for people who cannot pay immediately, worries about bills, and privately wonders whether remaining in Porth Awen was a choice or an avoidance of risk. The actor is **Rhys Vale**, internationally famous and Welsh-born, though “Rhys Vale” is a stage name polished for public consumption. He is charming, funny, observant, and used to being welcomed. His flaw is not simple vanity: he has made likability into a form of emotional survival. He fills silences, flatters reflexively, makes jokes when serious feeling threatens, and is far more affected by indifference than he wishes to be. His charm will be genuinely enjoyable, but it will sometimes be tiring, badly timed, or quietly manipulative. He should feel like a man who has been rewarded for performing himself so often that he is unsure what remains when performance fails. Elin recognizes Rhys immediately when he ducks into her shop to escape photographers. She refuses to react as his audience. Her dry replies are not evidence that she is effortlessly immune; they are a practical defence against a man who arrives with chaos around him and assumes he can win the room. Inwardly, she is flustered by him, fascinated by him, and irritated that his attention affects her at all. She will not always be right. She can use her restraint as a kind of superiority, and she may enjoy being the person who can make him work for a response. Her emotional arc is not about becoming softer for Rhys, but about admitting that independence has become, in part, a way of avoiding desire and disappointment. The story will be dialogue-led, especially in the opening chapter, as required by the prompt. Dialogue will be distinct rather than uniformly witty: - **Rhys** speaks quickly, with polished jokes, sideways compliments, and occasional theatrical flourishes. When he is anxious, he becomes more charming, not less. - **Elin** speaks more sparingly. Her humour is dry, literal, and often delivered after a pause. When she is affected, she may become brisk rather than tender. - Their banter will be punctured by mundane interruption, misunderstanding, failed jokes, and moments where one of them says too much. Narration will be restrained and sensory, used to reveal what the characters conceal: Rhys scanning faces for reactions, Elin being annoyed by the sound of his laugh because she likes it, the smell of wet wool and old paper, a stack of books slipping as the shop bell rings. Wales will not be treated as decorative scenery. Welsh language, town dynamics, local memory, and the friction between a famous person’s marketable “Welshness” and ordinary Welsh life will appear naturally through character and situation. The plot will remain intimate. Rhys is in Porth Awen between publicity obligations after a highly public breakup with his co-star, **Kit Mercer**. He ducks into Elin’s shop when photographers spot him. Their first encounter is the engine of the story: he expects charm to buy goodwill; she refuses to reward the performance; both are immediately, inconveniently attracted. Rhys returns, partly because he genuinely wants to see Elin and partly because he cannot bear leaving a person unconvinced by him. Over a handful of encounters, Elin sees his kindness and humour, but also the nervous dependence beneath them. Rhys sees that Elin is not simply resistant: she has a full, difficult life that does not revolve around whether he is appealing. A photograph or local post will bring unwanted attention to Elin and the shop. The conflict will not be solved by a grand gesture. Rhys will initially behave badly in a recognizably human way: he will panic, try to manage the situation, and make Elin’s distress partly about his own guilt and fear of being blamed. Elin will be justified in her anger but not flawlessly wise; she may say something deliberately cruel about the artificiality of his life. Rhys’s growth will be partial and demonstrated through behaviour. He will not suddenly become a perfectly self-possessed man who no longer cares what anyone thinks. Instead, he will learn to tolerate not being forgiven immediately, to stop turning Elin into a test of his worth, and to make one public choice that protects her privacy without claiming moral credit for it. The ending will be romantic but open-ended. Rhys will return to The Foxed Page openly, but not with a perfect speech or a promise that fame will never intrude again. Elin will not reward him merely for showing up. The final resolution will be that they choose one small next step—a walk, a meal, an evening together after the shop closes—while acknowledging that the difficult parts have not vanished. The piece will avoid: - A flawless “wise bookseller” heroine who exists only to heal a famous man. - A brooding or cruel actor whose charm is merely cosmetic. - Generic small-town cosiness and postcard-Wales imagery. - Heavy-handed theme statements about authenticity, fame, or being “truly seen.” - A villainous ex, saintly publicist, or all-knowing elderly mentor. - A plot where Rhys rescues the bookshop financially. - Overly tidy symmetry, perfect aphorisms, and a grand public romantic declaration. # Chapter Planning ## Chapter 1: Shelter Rhys Vale bursts into The Foxed Page during heavy rain, pursued by two photographers who have tracked him into Porth Awen. He is in a cap and sunglasses that make him more conspicuous, soaked through and trying to sound amused rather than alarmed. Elin is in the middle of accepting several damp boxes of donated books and dealing with a regular customer who wants to return a detective novel because “nothing happened except sadness.” Rhys asks whether there is a back way out. Elin tells him there is, if he is prepared to tunnel through the stockroom wall. The scene is driven primarily by dialogue. Rhys attempts a series of instinctive charm tactics: apologetic smiles, jokes about being a nuisance, compliments that are too smooth to be accidental. Elin recognizes him but neither names him nor treats him as special. She gives him a towel only because he is dripping on the floor, then hands him a stack of books to move so he is not standing uselessly behind her counter. He is unexpectedly bad at mundane shop work and increasingly intrigued by her refusal to be impressed. She catches him holding a Welsh poetry collection upside down; he turns it over with exaggerated dignity. He tries to discover whether she knows who he is. She says, “I know you are blocking the local history.” Outside, camera shutters click. Rhys’s humour briefly slips, revealing genuine dread. Elin notices but does not comfort him automatically. Before he leaves through the front door after the photographers move on, he buys the poetry collection. He asks whether she would recommend it. She says she would recommend reading it the right way up. ## Chapter 2: The Book He Did Not Finish Rhys returns two days later, this time without photographers but with a flimsy excuse: he has questions about the poetry book. Elin assumes he has come to prove he can return and win her over. She is irritated by how pleased she is to see him. He has not finished the collection, but he has read enough to be unsettled by one poem. He asks about it badly, disguising sincerity with jokes. Elin corrects him on a mistranslated word and is surprised that he listens rather than posing as knowledgeable. Their conversation is interrupted repeatedly by the ordinary business of the shop: a courier, a child looking for stories about sea monsters, a customer who insists a paperback should be cheaper because the cover is bent. Rhys is charming with everyone, but Elin notices that he watches their responses too closely. When one customer fails to recognize him, he seems relieved; when she later recognizes him and becomes excited, he brightens despite himself. Rhys asks Elin whether she has seen his films. She says no. He laughs as if that is funny, then becomes brittle when she does not soften it. He makes a defensive joke about booksellers thinking films are beneath them. Elin replies that she has no opinion on his films because she has not seen them, which somehow offends him more. He apologizes, but awkwardly. Elin does not let him charm his way out of it. Before leaving, Rhys notices a bilingual poster for an evening reading in the shop. He asks whether it is open to strangers. Elin says, “Depends how strangers behave.” ## Chapter 3: The Reading Room Rhys attends the small after-hours reading, sitting at the back with a cap in his pocket rather than on his head. The audience is mostly local: Elin’s cousin Teleri, who runs the café next door; Nansi, Elin’s teenage Saturday assistant; Dafydd, an elderly retired teacher with difficult opinions about poetry; and several regulars. Rhys initially tries to disappear, but Teleri recognizes him almost at once and reacts with delighted lack of discretion. Elin is annoyed with her, though secretly relieved that the recognition happens among people who are more curious than cruel. Rhys adapts quickly, making the room laugh, but Elin sees the effort in it. He is invited—more or less cornered—into reading a short passage from a local writer. He reads beautifully, not showily. For a moment, Elin understands why strangers believe they know him. Then Dafydd comments, mildly, that he was better when he stopped trying to sound important. The room laughs. Rhys laughs too, a second late. Later, while they wash mugs and stack chairs, Elin asks why he looks as if he has been insulted. Rhys says he has spent years being paid to look important. Elin says that sounds exhausting. He answers, “It is worse when nobody believes you are.” The conversation grows quieter. Elin nearly tells him that she did believe him, while he was reading. Instead, she tells him to take the bins out if he wants to be useful. He does, and makes a mess of the recycling. They both laugh too hard at it. He offers to walk her home. She says no, then looks at the rain and adds, “You may carry the umbrella. That is not the same thing.” ## Chapter 4: A Bad Idea in Good Weather The rain has stopped by the time they leave, but the town is slick and wind-bright. Rhys and Elin walk through Porth Awen with one umbrella between them, talking in uneven bursts. Rhys is less polished when he is not facing a room. He asks about the shop, and Elin explains the unromantic parts: inherited debts, online orders, local people who expect discounts because they knew her grandmother, and the fact that old books are mostly dust, mildew, and lifting boxes. Rhys tells her that sounds more honest than most interviews he has given. They talk about Wales in a way that exposes tension. Elin points out that journalists always describe him as “Wales’s favourite son,” as though the country is a charming backstory rather than a place with people in it. Rhys admits he sometimes feels he is wheeled out as proof of a Wales that is easy for outsiders to consume. He is fluent in Welsh, which surprises Elin; she assumes, unfairly, that he has traded everything local for fame. At the harbour, Rhys gets a call from Sian, his publicist. He lets it ring twice, then answers in a different voice: brisk, professionally charming, empty. Elin hears enough to understand that his next week has already been scheduled around him. After he hangs up, he jokes that she now knows he is managed like an expensive appliance. Elin says, “You don’t have to make everything funny.” He goes quiet. They nearly kiss, but Elin steps back first, unsettled by how much she wants to. As Rhys leaves, a tourist at the far end of the harbour takes a photo, perhaps innocently, perhaps not. Neither of them notices. ## Chapter 5: The Wrong Story The following morning, a social-media post identifies Rhys in Porth Awen and includes a blurry photograph of him and Elin near the harbour. It is not a kiss, but it is enough. By afternoon, entertainment sites have identified The Foxed Page and called Elin a “mystery bookseller.” The consequences are mundane and invasive rather than melodramatic. People linger outside the shop. Someone films through the window. Nansi’s mother worries about her working there. A customer asks Elin whether she is “going to be in the papers.” The shop receives both supportive messages and ugly accusations that Elin is chasing attention. Rhys arrives with Sian’s number on his phone and three solutions already proposed: security, a statement, a hotel away from town. Elin is furious that he has come in ready to solve a problem she has barely had time to name. He insists he is trying to help. She says he is trying to stop feeling helpless. Rhys panics and turns defensive. He says she knew who he was when she let him in. Elin, hurt and frightened, replies that he has spent so long selling a version of himself he may not know where the acting ends. The cruelty of it lands. Neither apologizes. Rhys leaves when Elin asks him to. Outside, he sees the people watching the shop and realizes that, for once, he cannot charm the situation into submission. ## Chapter 6: What Not to Say Rhys spends several days at a rented cottage outside town. His publicist urges him to reclaim the narrative: a controlled interview, a statement denying romance, a staged appearance with Kit Mercer at an upcoming Cardiff premiere. Rhys is tempted by the clarity of it. A public story, however false, feels easier than being disliked in private. Kit is not a villain. In a brief phone call, Kit tells Rhys that he cannot keep treating every uncomfortable emotion like a public-relations problem. Rhys dismisses this, then later repeats it to himself with irritation. Dafydd sends Rhys a text—not a wise speech, only a line from the poem Rhys struggled with, followed by: “You rushed the last stanza.” Rhys rereads the poem and understands, imperfectly, that he has been demanding an ending before doing the difficult middle. He does not contact Elin. Instead, he tells Sian he will not name the shop, deny the photograph theatrically, or use the situation to promote the film. He agrees to legal support for harassment only if it is offered to Elin through a local solicitor, with no expectation that she accept it. He records a short, unglamorous request to fans and press to leave the town alone, then refuses further comment. Elin learns of this indirectly. She does not interpret it as a grand romantic act; she is still hurt. But she recognizes that he has finally done something without needing her to witness or praise him. At closing time, she finds the poetry collection pushed through the letterbox. In its margins are not declarations, but a few sincere, uncertain notes. On the title page: “I was trying to be forgiven before I had listened. I am sorry.” ## Chapter 7: The Question He Won’t Answer Rhys attends the Cardiff premiere under pressure from studio executives and the press. The expected questions are about Kit, the film, and the “woman in Wales.” He is anxious, overprepared, and visibly tempted to use humour as cover. When asked whether the bookshop owner is his new girlfriend, he starts with a joke, stops halfway through it, and says he will not discuss someone who did not choose this attention. The interviewer pushes. Rhys becomes awkward rather than eloquent, repeats that she is entitled to privacy, and moves on. The refusal is not universally praised. Some commentators accuse him of being evasive; others assume it confirms the romance. His studio team are frustrated because he has failed to give them a clean, marketable moment. Rhys has to sit with that discomfort rather than trying to turn it into a winning line. Back in Porth Awen, Elin watches a clip only because Teleri sends it with several inappropriate heart emojis. She is not suddenly convinced that Rhys has changed. But she notices he did not make himself the injured party, did not name her, and did not seek applause for restraint. She sends him one message: “You were less unbearable than usual.” He replies several minutes later: “I am printing that on a T-shirt.” She does not answer. But she does smile, and then becomes annoyed with herself for it. ## Chapter 8: No Exit Several weeks later, the immediate attention has eased but has not vanished entirely. The Foxed Page is busy, the heating has failed again, and Elin is balancing on a small ladder trying to reach a shelf when Rhys walks in without disguise. He does not expect the shop to stop for him. It does not. Elin tells him to hold the ladder rather than stand there looking decorative. He does, carefully this time. Their first conversation is neither a grand apology nor an immediate reconciliation. Rhys asks after Nansi, the shop, and the legal complaint. Elin asks whether his premiere survived his refusal to perform. He says, “Tragically, the world continued.” He admits he has missed her, but does not ask her to reassure him or forgive him. He says he will be in Wales for another week, and asks whether she would consider having dinner with him—somewhere ordinary, at a time that suits her, without photographers if possible. Elin says that is a surprisingly normal question from a man who once entered her shop wearing sunglasses in a storm. He replies that he is learning. She says, “Slowly.” The final note remains open. Elin does not promise a relationship; she agrees to dinner after the shop closes on Thursday, provided he reads the rest of the poetry book first. Rhys says he has. She tests him on the final poem. He gets part of it wrong, and they argue gently over the meaning while he holds the ladder steady.
Perfect. Now with the outline more crystallised, and bearing in mind the discussion on human writing vs LLM pitfalls, we will flesh out our characters. Lets go through each of our main characters: - Write about their background, personality, idiosyncracies, flaws. Be specific and come up with examples to anchor & ground the character's profile (both core and trivial) - Briefly describe their physicality: appearance, how they carry themselves, express, interact with the world. - Concisely detail their motives, allegiences and existing relationships. Think from the perspective of the character as a real breathing thinking feeling individual in this world. - Write a couple quotes of flavour dialogue / internal monologue from the character to experiment with their voice. Output like this: # Character 1 name <character exploration> # Character 2 name <character exploration> etc
Model Output:
# Rhys Vale **Real name:** Rhys Vaughan **Age:** 38 **Occupation:** Internationally famous film actor, currently promoting a prestige historical drama while recovering from a very public split with his co-star, Kit Mercer. Rhys grew up in a fairly ordinary Welsh household outside Cardiff: a father who repaired boilers and an aunt who ran a hair salon, a mother who worked shifts in a care home and had an embarrassing faith in his school plays long before anyone else did. He was not born into the industry. He learned early that making people laugh, charming adults, and becoming the centre of a room could get him attention and goodwill. At thirteen, he played Puck in a youth theatre production and discovered two things at once: he was good at being watched, and being watched made him feel briefly invulnerable. His original surname, Vaughan, was changed by an early agent who said it was “too common to remember.” Rhys went along with it because he wanted the part. He has spent years insisting the change did not matter, though he keeps old letters addressed to “Rhys Vaughan” in a drawer in whichever flat or hotel he is currently calling home. He has not told many people this. He became famous in his late twenties through a glossy romantic drama in which he played an emotionally unavailable aristocrat in a damp overcoat. His public image is highly curated but built around real ingredients: Welsh charm, self-mockery, beautiful cheekbones, expensive suits, an apparent ability to flirt with anyone while making them feel uniquely noticed. He is much better at interviews than at silence. Rhys’s central flaw is that he requires approval with an intensity he finds humiliating. He calls it professionalism, courtesy, gratitude to fans, or simply good manners, but he is always reading people. He notices a smile that arrives late. He notices when a waiter treats him with bored indifference. He notices if someone at a party turns away before he has finished speaking. He can become obsessive about a lukewarm review, even while pretending not to know critics’ names. He is not insincere exactly. His compliments are often true. He does find people interesting, and he does enjoy making strangers feel welcome. The trouble is that he needs the return: the laugh, the softened face, the approval. When it does not come, he feels rejected and then becomes angry with himself for caring. His charm becomes most dangerous when he is afraid. He can make a joke in the middle of someone else’s distress because he cannot bear the feeling of being unable to fix it. His breakup with Kit was not a betrayal or a scandal in the simple sense. Their relationship was real, though it became tangled with their shared public image. Kit was tired of having every disagreement converted into a charming anecdote and every difficult conversation delayed until Rhys could make it funny. The split was mutual in theory, but Kit was the person who finally ended it. Recent publicity surrounding their film has made the breakup newly visible, and Rhys is wounded not only because he misses Kit, but because he suspects Kit’s criticism was accurate. Rhys is generous in ways that are both genuine and complicated. He remembers assistants’ birthdays. He sends flowers too often. He tips extravagantly. He has paid for a crew member’s emergency dental work and then felt irrationally upset when the crew member later seemed unimpressed by him. He wants to be good, but sometimes he mistakes being needed for being loved. His idiosyncrasies include: - He checks mirrors without appearing to, using dark windows, kettle reflections, and the backs of spoons. - He always knows where the nearest exit is, partly from security habits and partly because he hates feeling trapped. - He owns expensive clothes but steals hotel slippers and wears them until they fall apart. - He hates voice notes but sends them when drunk or lonely. - He has a pocket notebook full of lines from books, overheard remarks, and character observations. Most entries begin as research and end as accidental self-portraiture. - He speaks fluent Welsh but uses it less publicly than people assume. English has become his professional reflex. When he is tired, startled, or genuinely emotional, Welsh slips out before he can stop it. - He cannot cook beyond eggs, pasta, and a surprisingly good cawl learned from his mother. He tells people he cannot cook at all because the truth invites praise. Physically, Rhys is striking in the polished, slightly unfair way associated with film stars: tall, dark-haired, lean rather than muscular, with a face that photographs beautifully and a habit of looking as though he has just been caught in interesting weather. He is beginning to show fine lines around his eyes, and he worries about them more than he admits. His hair is usually styled for cameras, but in Porth Awen it is flattened by rain or shoved under a cap, which makes him look younger and less composed. He carries himself as though he has been taught to be looked at. He angles his body toward whoever is speaking, touches an elbow lightly when he laughs, and knows how to hold eye contact a second longer than is strictly necessary. In private, though, his confidence becomes visibly less stable: he rubs the heel of his palm over the back of his neck, fidgets with rings he does not normally wear, and becomes restless in empty rooms. Rhys’s immediate motive in entering The Foxed Page is simple: get away from the cameras. His next motive is less flattering: make Elin like him, because her lack of response feels like an unfinished problem. His deeper motive, which he cannot name at first, is to find a place where he can be ordinary without being ignored, and be known without being consumed. His primary allegiances are divided: - To his family, though fame has made contact complicated and irregular. - To Kit, despite the breakup. He still cares for them and resents that caring. - To Sian, his publicist, who is one of the few people who tells him no. - To the version of himself he has constructed for public survival. - Gradually, and uneasily, to Elin—not as someone he needs to win, but as someone whose boundaries he must learn to respect. **Flavour dialogue:** > “I’m not hiding. I’m conducting a rapid and deeply dignified reassessment of my route home.” > “You haven’t seen any of my films? That’s—good. Healthy. Probably excellent for you. I don’t know why I asked.” > “I can be normal. I’m very normal. I own three identical saucepans and have strong feelings about parking.” **Flavour internal monologue:** > She had looked at him as though he were not a man whose face had been blown up on billboards, but a wet inconvenience standing in front of the history section. It should not have mattered. It mattered with humiliating force. > Do not make a joke. Do not make a joke. If you make a joke now, she will know you are frightened. > > He made a joke. # Elin Morgan **Age:** 35 **Occupation:** Owner of The Foxed Page, a second-hand and small-press bookshop in Porth Awen. Elin was born and raised in Porth Awen, though she spent several years in Swansea studying literature and working in a chain bookshop, where she became very good at pretending that celebrity memoirs had not arrived three weeks before publication. She returned home when her grandmother, Mair, became ill. At first, she intended to stay only long enough to help with the shop. Then Mair died, the shop’s future became uncertain, and Elin found herself buying it from the estate with a small inheritance, a bank loan, and more determination than practical sense. She does not quite describe the shop as her grandmother’s legacy because the phrase embarrasses her. The truth is that the shop is the place where she feels most competent. It has bills, leaks, impossible customers, bad heating, and endless dust, but it is also a world she understands. There are systems to repair, books to sort, people to help, and rules that make sense. Romance does not fit neatly into those systems. Elin’s previous long relationship was with Gareth, a local graphic designer who had wanted to leave Porth Awen for Bristol. She did not go. The breakup was not a melodramatic betrayal; it was worse in a quieter way. Gareth eventually told her that she turned every choice into an obligation and every desire into an item on a list. Elin has spent years insisting he was wrong, because the accusation landed too close to something true. She has not been in a serious relationship since. Her central flaw is control disguised as self-sufficiency. She likes to think she is difficult to impress because she has standards. Often she simply does not want to need anyone. She is good at caring for people in practical ways—finding books, making tea, remembering allergies, extending tabs—while avoiding being cared for in return. She can be cutting when flustered, then replay her words for days. Rhys unsettles her because he makes her feel both seen and manipulated. She knows he is used to attention; she does not want to become another person who hands it to him. Yet her resistance gives her a role she finds seductive: the woman who cannot be charmed. She has to recognize that she is not above the dynamic. Sometimes she holds back because she enjoys watching him scramble for a reaction. Sometimes she judges him before he has finished speaking because that feels safer than liking him. Elin has a serious relationship with books but not a reverential one. She loves literature, but she also thinks people who treat every novel as sacred are exhausting. Her favourite reading is crime fiction, coastal ghost stories, essays about labour, and badly translated European paperbacks with strange covers. She knows Welsh poetry well because it was important to her grandmother, but she is impatient with people who name-drop poets to sound profound. She runs The Foxed Page with a mixture of competence and improvisation: - She keeps financial records in a spreadsheet but writes the passwords on the back of old till receipts. - She knows the exact location of almost every book, except the ones customers ask for. - She resents online marketplaces but relies on them for enough sales to keep the heating on. - She has a shelf of books she refuses to sell because they belonged to Mair, though she claims she is “still deciding their value.” - She allows certain customers to pay later, then complains about them to Teleri while quietly forgiving the debt. - She hates moths in books and is deeply suspicious of people who fold page corners. - She can identify damp damage by smell with alarming accuracy. Her idiosyncrasies include: - She makes tea, forgets it, reheats it, and then drinks it cold anyway. - When angry, she tidies. When truly upset, she stops tidying altogether. - She has a habit of tapping book spines into alignment while she thinks. - She keeps a pencil behind one ear and loses it several times a day. - She wears old silver rings that belonged to her grandmother and twists one around her finger whenever she is uncertain. - She is not especially sentimental about birthdays but remembers exactly which books people bought before important events. - She mutters in Welsh when lifting heavy boxes, dealing with technology, or being unexpectedly moved. Physically, Elin is medium height, solidly built, and more comfortable in practical clothes than anything she would call flattering. She has dark brown hair that is often pinned up with a pencil or clipped carelessly out of her face, usually escaping by late afternoon. Her eyes are brown with amber flecks that show in strong light, though Rhys notices them before she would ever think to describe them. She dresses in soft jumpers, worn jeans, boots that can manage wet streets, and an old waxed jacket that smells faintly of paper and rain. She carries herself with the compact efficiency of someone who is always midway through three tasks. She stands with one hand on her hip when listening, leans on the counter when she is trying to appear casual, and moves quickly between shelves without looking where she is going because she knows the shop by instinct. She dislikes being touched unexpectedly. When Rhys gets too close, she does not retreat so much as become still. Her immediate motive is to protect her shop, her employees, and her own privacy from the chaos that follows Rhys. Her secondary motive is to avoid admitting that he is affecting her. Beneath both is a quieter wish: to be chosen without having to become a different, easier version of herself. Her allegiances are: - To The Foxed Page and the memory of her grandmother, though she resists making either sentimental. - To Teleri, her cousin and closest friend, even when Teleri is impossible. - To Nansi, whom she mentors more gently than she realizes. - To Porth Awen, though she sometimes feels trapped by it. - To her own hard-won independence, which she mistakes for safety. - Eventually, cautiously, to Rhys—but only if he learns that her life cannot become a convenient refuge from his. **Flavour dialogue:** > “You may stand there being famous if you like, but you are dripping on the biographies.” > “I don’t dislike you. I simply don’t find you as remarkable as you seem to hope.” > “That is not an apology. That is a monologue wearing an apology’s coat.” **Flavour internal monologue:** > He smiled as though smiles had always worked on doors, difficult people, bad weather, governments. The irritating thing was that, on a less sensible day, it might have worked on her. > She did not want to be impressed. She especially did not want to be impressed by a man who had entered her shop looking like an expensive burglary. # Sian Bowen **Age:** 42 **Occupation:** Rhys’s publicist and long-term professional protector. Sian is Welsh, based primarily in London, and has represented Rhys since his career moved from promising theatre actor to genuine celebrity. She is excellent at her work: calm in crises, hard to embarrass, difficult to manipulate, and able to identify a brewing scandal from a single badly phrased Instagram caption. She is neither cold nor villainous, but she has learned to speak in practical terms because emotional language creates loopholes that tabloids and studios exploit. She grew up in Newport, the eldest of four children in a family where competence was a form of affection. Her mother was a nurse, her father worked in local government, and everyone relied on Sian to remember deadlines, directions, school forms, and who had upset whom. She built a career in entertainment publicity partly because she enjoys strategy and partly because she likes the clean satisfaction of solving a problem no one else has noticed yet. Sian genuinely cares about Rhys. She has seen him through bad auditions, family illness, a stalker, his first major award, his first public breakup, and more interviews than either of them can count. She knows his charm is partly generosity and partly fear. She has also had to clean up after the consequences often enough that she is less enchanted by it than most people. Her flaw is that she can mistake control for care. When Rhys is hurt, she offers a plan. When he is ashamed, she offers messaging. When he wants to speak honestly, she hears potential damage. She is not wrong about the risks, but she sometimes forgets that keeping him protected has helped keep him emotionally stalled. Her idiosyncrasies include: - She carries a portable charger, plasters, painkillers, mints, safety pins, and a spare tie in a tote bag that looks too ordinary to contain a crisis-management unit. - She says “fine” when she means “this will become catastrophic if you do not listen to me.” - She has an intense dislike of people who call publicists “spin doctors.” - She texts in complete sentences, including punctuation, and uses no emojis except a single thumbs-up when she is furious. - She watches cooking programmes after difficult days because nobody is supposed to answer a hostile question while making pastry. - She has a teenage son, Iwan, whose total disinterest in Rhys’s fame keeps her sane. Physically, Sian is sharply put together without looking flashy: close-cropped dark hair beginning to silver at the temples, dark trousers, good coats, sensible boots, and glasses she removes when someone is testing her patience. Her face is expressive despite her controlled manner; she can communicate a full professional warning with a raised eyebrow. She moves with speed and purpose. She does not sit if a meeting is going badly. She holds her phone like it has personally offended her. Unlike Rhys, she does not scan a room for approval; she scans it for exits, cameras, and people pretending not to listen. Her motive is to protect Rhys’s career and, more sincerely, to protect him from the parts of fame that have already hurt him. She is loyal to him, but her allegiance is also to the staff, crew, contracts, and livelihoods connected to his work. She is not interested in becoming the antagonist of his romance; she is interested in preventing him from mistaking a personal feeling for a strategy. **Flavour dialogue:** > “You are allowed to have emotions, Rhys. You are not allowed to make them a press release.” > “No, I am not asking you to lie. I am asking you not to answer a question designed by a man who thinks a microphone is a personality.” > “If you say ‘I’ll just speak from the heart’ on a red carpet, I will personally have you sedated.” # Kit Mercer **Age:** 39 **Occupation:** Actor; Rhys’s former partner and current co-star. Kit is a successful actor in their own right, known for sharp, intense performances and an unwillingness to package themselves into easy charm. They met Rhys during a film shoot several years earlier. Their relationship became public almost by accident and then useful to everyone around them: audiences loved the pairing, studios liked the publicity, and both actors initially enjoyed the sense of being understood by someone who knew the industry from inside. Kit’s relationship with Rhys ended because affection became confused with presentation. They loved Rhys, but they grew tired of always being asked to forgive the joke, soften the interview answer, attend the event, look happy in photographs, and accept that Rhys’s anxiety needed to become everyone else’s emotional weather. Kit did not leave because Rhys was monstrous. They left because they recognized that staying would make them cruel. Kit is not part of the main romance plot as a threat or rival. Their role is to show that Rhys’s patterns have consequences beyond Elin and to make clear that a public breakup does not require a villain. Kit is also under media pressure, tired of being framed as either the abandoned ex or the icy careerist. Their idiosyncrasies include: - They read scripts with a mechanical pencil and erase holes through the pages. - They have a habit of asking brutally practical questions at the worst possible moment. - They keep a running list of bad notes received from producers and rate them out of ten. - They are excellent at remembering names but rarely use them when annoyed. - They knit between takes, mostly hats, and give them away without explanation. Physically, Kit has an androgynous, elegant presence: cropped dark hair, an angular face, expressive eyebrows, and an economy of movement that makes them seem calmer than they are. They dress with deliberate simplicity—black, cream, dark green—and wear a single battered watch. They do not perform warmth for cameras, which has often been interpreted as aloofness. Their motive is to get through the premiere and publicity cycle without allowing the breakup to become a marketing tool. They still want Rhys to be well, but they do not want to resume the emotional labour of managing him. Their allegiance is to their own boundaries, their work, and a small circle of friends who knew them before fame. **Flavour dialogue:** > “You don’t need a better explanation, Rhys. You need to stop treating every explanation as a way back in.” > “I am not angry that you care what people think. Most people do. I am tired that everybody else has to live inside it with you.” # Teleri Morgan **Age:** 37 **Occupation:** Owner of the café beside The Foxed Page; Elin’s cousin and closest friend. Teleri—usually “Tel”—runs **Caffi Awen**, a narrow, busy café that supplies tea, soup, toast, and gossip to half the town. She and Elin grew up close enough to feel like sisters, which means they are affectionate, competitive, and capable of irritating one another with frightening accuracy. Tel is warmer and more demonstrative than Elin. She loves people, knows too much about everyone, and regards secrets as things that require careful storage rather than permanent burial. She is not frivolous, though Elin sometimes calls her that when she is embarrassed. The café is a real business, and Tel works hard: early mornings, unreliable staff, rising food costs, customers who want “just a small change” to every order. Her flaw is that she confuses interest with help. She genuinely wants Elin to be happy, but she can push too hard, gossip too loosely, and treat a complicated emotional situation as though it is a serial she has been following with excellent snacks. She may initially be delighted by Rhys’s presence before fully understanding what the attention does to Elin. Tel has a strong protective streak. Once she realizes strangers are harassing the shop, she becomes fierce and practical: refusing photographers service, calling people out, getting the local community involved. She is not interested in Rhys’s fame once she sees Elin hurt by it. At the same time, she can see qualities in Rhys that Elin refuses to acknowledge. Her idiosyncrasies include: - She calls everyone “love,” including people she dislikes. - She has a chalkboard menu written in beautiful handwriting and changes it according to mood rather than ingredients. - She sings under her breath while making coffee, usually half a line from a song and never the correct lyrics. - She keeps emergency cake in a labelled freezer container, although she denies this. - She has an ongoing feud with a tourist who left a two-star review because the café did not serve oat milk in 2019. - She sends Elin voice messages that begin with “Don’t be cross,” which guarantees Elin will be cross. Physically, Tel is vibrant and expressive, with dark curls she ties in a scarf when working, bright lipstick even at seven in the morning, and flour or coffee grounds often on one sleeve. She hugs people without warning, talks with her hands, and can make a crowded room feel as though she is speaking personally to each person in it. Her motive is to protect Elin, keep the café afloat, and enjoy a little drama where it does not cause genuine harm. Her loyalty is overwhelmingly to Elin, though she is capable of liking Rhys before Elin is ready to admit she does. **Flavour dialogue:** > “I’m not saying he’s handsome. I’m saying the entire female population of the harbour nearly walked into a bollard when he took his coat off.” > “You don’t have to marry him, El. You could simply stop looking as though he has personally offended you by having eyelashes.” > “If a photographer comes in here, they’re paying for tea. I don’t care if they’re hiding behind a potted fern.”

Chapter 1

Great. Now let's write this. Follow your plan. Output chapter 1, without any additional commentary. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 1: Shelter The bell above the door of The Foxed Page gave a tired little clang, and a man in a black cap came in sideways. He shut the door too quickly behind him, flattening himself against it as if the shelves might swallow him. Elin looked up from a box labelled **MISC. / POSSIBLY MILDEW**. The man wore dark sunglasses despite the rain and an expensive-looking coat dripping steadily onto the worn blue mat. His hair had escaped beneath the cap in damp black curls. He was taller than the doorway seemed designed for, and for one ridiculous second Elin thought: *That is Rhys Vale.* Then he took off the sunglasses. It was Rhys Vale. Outside, through the steamed glass, two figures stood beneath umbrellas on the pavement opposite. One raised a camera. The man at Elin’s door exhaled. “Hello,” he said. Elin glanced down at the spreading puddle beneath him. “You’re wet.” “Yes. I’d noticed.” “You’re dripping on the biographies.” He looked at the floor, then back at her. A smile arrived—quick, apologetic, almost boyish, and so practised it made her suspicious before it made her anything else. “I’m terribly sorry.” “Are you?” “No,” he said, with disarming honesty. “I’m mostly cold.” From behind the counter, Mr Pritchard cleared his throat. Elin had almost forgotten he was there. He stood clutching a paperback crime novel in both hands, wearing his usual brown cardigan and the expression of a man about to complain on principle. “Nothing happened,” he announced. Elin blinked. “In the book?” “Except sadness.” “It is set in Finland.” “That’s no excuse.” The wet man’s mouth twitched. Elin shot him a look. “Mr Pritchard has returned *The Ice Widow*.” “Again,” Mr Pritchard said. “The woman spends two hundred pages remembering a dead horse.” “It was her sister.” “Then it was worse than I thought.” The actor put a hand over his mouth. Not quickly enough. Mr Pritchard turned toward him. “You all right, boy?” “Yes,” said Rhys Vale, lowering his hand. “I’m sorry. I’ve never heard a more devastating review.” “You should read it,” Mr Pritchard told him. “Then you’ll understand.” “I’ll add it to my list.” “You do that.” Mr Pritchard deposited the book on the counter. Elin slid a different crime novel toward him from the returns pile. “This one has a body on page four.” “Good.” “And no horses.” “Better.” As Mr Pritchard tucked the book beneath his cardigan, the stranger—though he was not a stranger, not exactly—looked over Elin’s shoulder toward the back of the shop. “Is there another way out?” Elin followed his glance. Beyond the counter lay the narrow stockroom, several sagging shelves, the kettle, a fire door permanently blocked by boxes of donations, and a wall. “There is,” she said. “If you’re prepared to tunnel through the stockroom.” He looked at her. “You’re joking.” “No. The wall’s old. You might get lucky.” Outside, the camera flashed. Rhys flinched before he could stop himself. It was small. The sort of movement most people would have missed: a tightening through his shoulders, his hand closing once at his side. Then the smile returned, a little brighter. “Right,” he said. “Fine. Excellent. I’ll take the wall, then.” “You’ll need a spade.” “I’m Welsh. Surely there’s one behind the counter.” Mr Pritchard, who had been inching toward the door, stopped and looked at him more closely. “Oh,” he said. Rhys’s smile faltered. Mr Pritchard squinted. “You’re that actor.” “I’ve been called worse.” “The one with the eyebrows.” “Also true.” Mr Pritchard nodded, apparently satisfied. “My daughter likes you.” “That’s kind of her.” “She says you were very good in that thing with the train.” Rhys’s face lit with involuntary relief. “*The North Line*?” “No, the other one.” The light went out so quickly Elin almost laughed. Mr Pritchard frowned, trying to remember. “You know. There was a train.” “I’ve done several trains.” “That’s probably it.” Then he left, the bell clanging overhead. Through the glass, he passed the waiting photographers without so much as a glance. Rhys watched him go. “You all right?” Elin asked before she could stop herself. He looked at her as if she had caught him doing something embarrassing. “Perfectly,” he said. “Never better. Nothing steadies a man’s soul like being remembered for his eyebrows.” “You have memorable eyebrows.” “Thank you. That’s the sort of thing I come into independent bookshops hoping to hear.” “You came in because you were being followed.” “Yes,” he said. “But I’m trying to grow.” Elin looked again at the people outside. “You should probably not stand in front of the window.” “Where would you suggest?” “The local history section.” He glanced behind him. “Am I allowed?” “Only if you promise not to drip on Owain Glyndŵr.” “I would never.” “People say that, and then suddenly he’s got rainwater in his binding.” Rhys stepped away from the door, ducking instinctively as another flash went off. He moved through the shop with the careful, slightly absurd gait of someone attempting to look like he had simply wandered into a second-hand bookshop in a coastal town on a Tuesday afternoon. He stopped beneath the sign marked **WALES / LOCAL INTEREST**. Elin returned to the donation box. She lifted out three hardbacks, one cookbook from 1987, and a copy of *Fifty Shades of Grey* swollen to twice its usual size. “Do you need help?” he asked. “No.” “I’m quite useful.” “You are standing in a puddle in a hat indoors.” “I can do both things.” Elin looked at him properly then. Up close, he was less finished than he was on screens. There were shadows beneath his eyes. His jaw had a faint blue darkness under the skin. Rain had darkened the collar of his coat. He looked, she thought with some annoyance, less like a film star than a person who had had a difficult morning and had not known where else to put himself. The thought made her soften. Only slightly. “Fine,” she said, nudging the box toward him with her boot. “Those go on the trolley. Anything with mould goes in the bin bag. Anything that looks expensive, give me.” Rhys peered into the box. “What counts as expensive?” “You’ll know.” “That is an alarming amount of faith in my judgment.” “You’re an actor. Pretend you’re a bookseller.” He crouched beside the box, long legs folding awkwardly. “Do booksellers have a particular emotional history I should prepare?” “Mostly lower back pain.” “Ah. I’ve done that.” “You’ve played lower back pain?” “Twice. One of them got awards.” Elin hated that she laughed. He looked up at the sound, and there it was again—that quick, hungry attention. Not vanity exactly. Something more exposed. He wanted the laugh to mean something. He wanted it to prove he had got through. She turned away before he could see that it had. “Put those on the trolley,” she said. “Yes, boss.” “Don’t call me that.” “Right. Sorry.” He paused. “Elin?” Her head came up. He had read it from the small brass badge pinned to her jumper. Of course he had. “That is my name.” “It suits you.” She stared at him. He lifted both hands. “Was that too much?” “It was nothing.” “Worse.” She bent over the next box so he would not see her face. For several minutes, they worked in silence. Or near enough. The rain hissed against the windows. Somewhere in the back room, the kettle clicked off. Rhys picked up books, sorted them into piles with surprisingly earnest concentration, and occasionally held one up for judgment. “*The Da Vinci Code*?” “Sell.” “*The Secret*?” “Bin.” “Is that harsh?” “No.” “A book called *How to Make Any Man Want You*.” Elin looked over. “That’s definitely bin.” “Cruel. It might be educational.” “It might be contagious.” He laughed again, quieter this time. Then he reached into the bottom of the box and drew out a slim green volume. He turned it in his hand, studying the cover with a seriousness he had not earned. “Is this expensive?” he asked. Elin looked. He was holding it upside down. “It’s poetry.” “That wasn’t my question.” “It’s also upside down.” Rhys inspected the book, rotated it with solemn care, and read the title aloud. “*Cerddi’r Glaw.* Poems of the Rain.” “Bilingual edition.” “Appropriate.” “It’s five pounds.” He looked at the front window. The photographers were still there. One of them had moved closer, pretending to study the charity shop opposite. Rhys held the book against his chest. “I’ll take it.” “You don’t have to buy something to stand indoors.” “I know.” His smile was smaller now. “I’d like to.” Elin took it from him and carried it to the till. “You read Welsh?” she asked. “When I’m trying to impress someone.” “I’m not impressed.” “No,” he said, watching her. “I’m beginning to gather that.” The bell over the door rattled in the wind. Elin rang up the book. “Five pounds.” He handed over a twenty. “Do you have anything smaller?” “I’m afraid I’m offensively wealthy in notes and profoundly poor in coins.” “That sounds like a personal problem.” “It usually is.” She gave him his change. Their fingers touched briefly, and Elin pulled her hand back as if the coins had burned her. Rhys noticed. Of course he did. But he only tucked the money away and put the poetry book beneath his coat. “Thank you,” he said. “For the book?” “For not screaming.” “I was busy.” “That’s not why.” Elin met his eyes. They were darker than they looked on screen. Tired, too. “No,” she said. “It isn’t.” Outside, one of the photographers lifted his camera again. Rhys glanced toward the window, then back at her. “What time do you open tomorrow?” “The hours are printed on the door.” “I saw that.” “Then you have your answer.” He smiled, but something in it had changed. Less certain. More interested. “Right,” he said. “I’ll try reading it the right way up.” “Ambitious.” He opened the door. The photographers straightened. Before stepping into the rain, Rhys looked back once. “Elin?” “What?” “Try not to sell all the good books before I get back.” Then he was gone, cap pulled low, shoulders set beneath the expensive wet coat. The bell gave its tired clang. Elin stood behind the counter with the donation boxes still open at her feet. After a moment, she picked up the copy of *How to Make Any Man Want You*, looked at its swollen pink cover, and put it very firmly in the bin.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 1)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This is an exceptionally well-executed opening chapter that demonstrates genuine craft across nearly every dimension. Let me work through the key elements critically. **Dialogue and Voice Distinction** The dialogue is the chapter's greatest strength. Rhys and Elin speak in genuinely distinct registers. Rhys's lines have that polished, slightly-too-smooth quality the prompt demands: "I'm offensively wealthy in notes and profoundly poor in coins" is exactly the kind of line a man who has been rewarded for wit his whole life would produce. Elin's responses are spare, dry, and often delivered as deflections that are themselves a form of engagement. The exchange about the spade ("I'm Welsh. Surely there's one behind the counter") and her response about the tunnel through the stockroom wall both land cleanly. Mr. Pritchard's subplot about the Finnish crime novel is a masterstroke of comic interruption that also serves character: it gives Rhys an audience he can charm, lets Elin observe him doing it, and provides the "train" exchange where his ego briefly flares and deflates in a single beat. That moment—the light going on and off in his face—is beautifully observed. **Character Consistency** Rhys is written precisely as the profile demands: charming, reflexively performing, but with genuine cracks showing. The shoulder-flinch at the camera flash is exactly right—small, involuntary, quickly papered over. His "hungry attention" when Elin laughs is rendered without being over-explained. The detail of him holding the poetry book upside down and then rotating it "with solemn care" is both funny and revealing. Elin's internal experience is conveyed through action and restraint rather than exposition: she pulls her hand back from the coin exchange, she turns away before he can see that his laugh landed, she puts the pink self-help book in the bin at the end. These are all earned character moments. **The Ending** The final image—Elin binning "How to Make Any Man Want You"—is genuinely good. It's funny, it's character-consistent, it's not over-explained, and it carries a light ironic charge without being smug about it. This is the kind of ending that trusts the reader. **Potential Weaknesses** The chapter is almost too polished. The wit is consistent to the point where one might question whether any line truly misfires in the way the plan promises ("failed jokes, misunderstanding, moments where one of them says too much"). Every exchange lands. This is impressive but slightly at odds with the plan's stated intention to puncture the banter with genuine awkwardness. The closest we get to a real misfire is the "It suits you" exchange, which is handled well but briefly. The line "He looked, she thought with some annoyance, less like a film star than a person who had had a difficult morning and had not known where else to put himself" is the chapter's one moment of slight over-explanation—it tells us what we've already been shown. Similarly, "Not vanity exactly. Something more exposed" edges toward telling rather than showing, though it's brief. The Mr. Pritchard scene, while excellent, does slightly delay the central dynamic. This is a minor structural quibble. **Prose Quality** The prose is clean, controlled, and sensory without being purple. "The bell above the door of The Foxed Page gave a tired little clang" is a good opening—specific, slightly comic, establishing tone immediately. The rain is present without being over-described. The shop feels real. There are no strained metaphors or forced poetic leaps. The writing earns its lightness. **Faithfulness to Plan and Prompt** The chapter follows its plan closely: the donation boxes, Mr. Pritchard's complaint, the back-exit joke, the upside-down poetry book, the purchase, the final exchange. The prompt's requirements—dialogue-led, chemistry with undercurrent, Rhys's charm meeting Elin's apparent immunity, her inward flustering shown through action—are all met. The Welsh setting is present without being postcard-ish. Overall, this is high-quality commercial literary fiction executed with genuine skill. Its main limitation is that it's almost too controlled—the seams don't show enough to feel fully alive to risk. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 17 Emotionally Engaging: 17 Compelling Plot: 15 Coherent: 19 Weak Dialogue: 2 Tell-Don't-Show: 3 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 4 Amateurish: 1 Purple Prose: 1 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 1 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 17 Characters Consistent with Profile: 18 Followed Chapter Plan: 18 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 19

Chapter 2

Ok now chapter 2. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 2: The Book He Did Not Finish Rhys Vale came back on Thursday at half past eleven, when Elin was on a ladder arguing with a shelf. The shelf had been installed by her grandmother’s brother in 1978 and possessed the moral certainty of all things built by men who believed screws were a personal insult. It leaned two inches to the left, creaked when anyone looked at it, and had just dropped a row of travel guides onto Elin’s head. “Traitor,” she muttered. The bell above the door rang. “If that’s you, Mr Pritchard, I’ve told you, I don’t stock Scandinavian happiness—” “It’s only me,” said Rhys. Elin looked down. He stood beneath the ladder in a dark green jumper and jeans, no sunglasses, no cap, no visible entourage. His hair was dry and untidy in a way that had almost certainly taken effort. He had one hand in his coat pocket and the green poetry book under his arm. For a moment neither of them spoke. Then Elin said, “You’re blocking the exit.” Rhys glanced behind him. “I can move.” “Please do.” He stepped obediently to one side. Elin climbed down, carrying the fallen guides. One slid from her arm—*Walking the Pembrokeshire Coast Path*—and Rhys caught it before it hit the floor. “Careful,” he said. “It’s a guidebook.” “Still.” “You’ve become sentimental overnight.” “I read poetry.” “You bought poetry.” “Details.” She took the book from him, more sharply than necessary. His fingers brushed hers. Again, irritatingly warm. “What do you want?” she asked. Rhys lifted the green volume. “I have a question.” “That sounds ominous.” “It’s about page twenty-three.” Elin looked at the book. “You’ve read to page twenty-three?” “Twenty-seven.” “That is not the triumph you think it is.” “It’s bilingual. I read every line twice.” “Why?” “In case the Welsh was better.” “It is.” He looked pleased with himself. “I knew it.” “You didn’t. You guessed.” “I guessed correctly. That should count for something.” “It counts as guessing correctly.” Rhys leaned one shoulder against the counter. The movement was casual. His eyes were not. He was watching her as if waiting for a door to open. Elin went behind the till. “What’s your question?” He thumbed through the book, stopping at a page marked with a receipt. Her receipt, from Tuesday: **CERDDI’R GLAW — £5.00**. “This line,” he said. “It says, ‘The sea keeps its promises by breaking them.’ That can’t be right.” “It’s not exactly right.” “Good. I was worried I’d understood it.” “The Welsh is closer to… the sea keeping faith by changing shape.” Rhys considered this. “That’s worse.” “Worse?” “More annoying.” “Elaborate.” “I don’t know.” He looked down at the page. “Promises are supposed to be fixed. Otherwise they’re just weather.” Elin felt something in her chest shift, small and unwelcome. “The poet would probably say weather is more reliable than people.” “That’s bleak.” “It’s Wales. We’ve earned bleak.” He smiled, but not for show this time. It appeared as if against his better judgment. The bell rang again, and Nansi came in backward through the door, dragging a carton of second-hand books behind her. “Morning,” she said, before turning around. “Oh.” Rhys straightened. Nansi, seventeen and constitutionally incapable of disguising any emotion for longer than three seconds, stared at him. “You’re Rhys Vale.” Rhys gave her a small nod. “I am.” “You were in *The Queen’s Man*.” “I was.” “My mum watched that six times when Dad was in hospital.” The answer caught him off guard. His easy expression altered. “Oh,” he said. “I’m sorry your dad was ill.” “He’s fine now.” Nansi tucked a strand of purple hair behind her ear. “She still watches it, though. Says you look at people like you mean it.” Rhys opened his mouth. Then shut it. “Tell your mum,” he said finally, “that I’m glad he’s all right.” Nansi nodded, suddenly shy. She looked at Elin, then at the book under Rhys’s hand. “Are you buying poetry?” “I’m attempting it.” “Good luck,” Nansi said, with the grave sympathy of someone who had failed an exam in the same subject. She hauled the carton toward the back room. Rhys watched her go. His face had softened in a way Elin suspected he would hate being caught in. “You’re very good at that,” she said. “At what?” “Making people feel as though you’ve known them longer than thirty seconds.” His expression closed a fraction. “Is that a criticism?” “It’s an observation.” “Those are often worse.” Before Elin could reply, the door flew open again and a small boy in a yellow raincoat marched in, followed by his mother, looking damp and apologetic. “Sorry,” the woman said. “He’s after dragons.” “Sea dragons,” the boy corrected. “Not ordinary ones.” Elin came around the counter. “We can do sea dragons.” “Big ones?” “Terrifyingly big.” Rhys moved aside, letting them pass. The boy stopped in front of him. “Are you famous?” His mother made a noise of pure maternal despair. Rhys crouched until they were level. “Only on Tuesdays.” “It’s Thursday.” “Then I’m in trouble.” The boy considered this. “You look like the man from the telly.” “That’s because I owe him money.” The boy accepted this explanation immediately. Elin led him toward the children’s shelves, biting the inside of her cheek to stop herself smiling. Behind her, she heard Rhys say quietly to the mother, “Sorry. I’m not trying to make things difficult.” The woman glanced at him, recognized him properly, and went bright with surprise. “Oh, no. No, of course not. My sister loves your work.” Rhys’s shoulders eased. There it was again. That tiny, almost invisible response to being liked. Like someone had put a hand under his chin and lifted his face toward the light. Elin disliked that she noticed. She found the boy a battered copy of *The Sea-Serpent’s Daughter*, complete with a lurid painting of green scales and a frightened lighthouse keeper. “Does it have teeth?” he asked. “Several pages’ worth.” “Good.” The mother paid. As she turned to leave, she looked back at Rhys. “Sorry to bother you, but could we have a picture?” Rhys smiled at once. “Of course.” He stood beside the boy, bending slightly into frame. The mother took two photographs, then three. The boy grinned as though he had met an actual sea dragon. When they had gone, Rhys stood by the door, still smiling. Then he looked at Elin. “Well?” “Well what?” “You’re going to tell me I made a scene.” “I was going to tell you that you were nice to him.” His smile changed. Smaller. Uncertain. “Oh.” “Don’t look so alarmed.” “I’m not.” “You are.” “I just didn’t expect a compliment.” “It wasn’t one. It was an observation.” “Those are often worse.” Elin looked away, pretending to straighten a display of local maps. Rhys came closer to the counter. “Have you seen any of my films?” There it was. Too casual. Too quick. “No,” she said. He laughed. Not naturally. It was a bright sound with no warmth in it. “None?” “I don’t go to the cinema much.” “Right. Of course. Books. Serious things.” “I read plenty of nonsense.” “Do you?” “Yes.” “Such as?” “Elmore Leonard. Old Agatha Christie. Things with bodies on page four.” He nodded, but the brightness had gone hard around the edges. “You think films are nonsense?” “I didn’t say that.” “You didn’t have to.” Elin set down the map she was holding. “You asked if I’d seen them. I said no.” “And you said you don’t go to the cinema much.” “Because I don’t.” “Fine.” The word landed between them with too much force. Rhys picked up the poetry book. “I should go.” “You don’t have to.” “No, I know. I’m excellent at taking up space voluntarily.” Elin’s irritation flared. “You are making this much stranger than it needs to be.” He looked at her then, properly. Not charming. Not smooth. Just tired and faintly embarrassed. “Yes,” he said. “I often do.” The honesty of it took the next line from her. Rhys glanced toward the door. “Sorry. That was ridiculous.” “It was.” “Thank you.” “You’re welcome.” He gave a short laugh, more real this time. “You don’t make it easy to recover.” “I’m not responsible for your recovery.” “No,” he said. “I’m beginning to understand that too.” He put the book on the counter, then hesitated. “The reading,” he said. “Tonight. Is it open to strangers?” Elin looked at the poster in the window. **POETRY AFTER HOURS. 7:30. TEA PROVIDED.** “It depends,” she said. “On what?” “How strangers behave.” Rhys nodded, solemn as a man accepting legal terms. “I can be very well behaved.” “I doubt it.” “So do I,” he said. For the first time since he had walked in, Elin smiled without meaning to. Rhys saw it. He did not pounce on it. Did not turn it into a victory. He only looked briefly pleased, then picked up the green book. “Seven-thirty,” he said. “Bring your own mug.” “Is that a local custom?” “It is now.” He reached the door, then paused. “Elin?” “What?” “I did read more than twenty-seven pages.” She folded her arms. “How many?” “Thirty-two.” “Remarkable.” “I know. Don’t tell anyone. It’ll ruin my reputation.” The bell clanged as he left. Elin watched him cross the wet pavement, hands in his pockets, head down against the wind. Nobody waited outside with cameras. Nobody called his name. For some reason, that made the shop feel quieter after he had gone.
+ Judge Evaluation (Chapter 2)
Judge Response:
[Analysis] This chapter demonstrates considerable craft and largely succeeds at what it sets out to do. Let me examine it systematically. **Dialogue and Voice Distinction** The dialogue is the chapter's strongest element. Rhys and Elin speak in genuinely distinct registers. Rhys's lines have a polished, slightly performative quality that occasionally slips into something more honest: "I often do" is a good moment of unguarded admission. Elin's responses are spare, dry, and often delivered as corrections rather than conversation. The exchange about the poetry translation ("That's worse." / "Worse?" / "More annoying.") is genuinely good — it reveals character through apparent triviality. The rhythm of their sparring feels earned rather than mechanical. The "Only on Tuesdays" exchange with the boy is charming without being cloying, and the mother's recognition of Rhys followed by his visible relief is a small, precise character beat that shows rather than tells his approval-dependency. **Character Consistency** Rhys's fragile ego is well-rendered. The moment where he asks "Have you seen any of my films?" is flagged as "Too casual. Too quick" — a nice piece of free indirect discourse that shows Elin reading him accurately. His brittleness when she says no, the way the brightness "goes hard around the edges," and his defensive joke about booksellers thinking films beneath them all track with the character profile. The moment where he sees Nansi's mother's recognition and his shoulders ease is subtle and effective. Elin's internal resistance is handled well. She notices things she doesn't want to notice — his warmth with the boy, his unguarded face — and the narration captures her irritation at her own responses without over-explaining. "Elin disliked that she noticed" is clean and effective. **Following the Plan** The chapter follows its plan closely. The courier and child with sea monsters appear (the courier is absent, but the child substitutes effectively). The film question and Elin's "no" leading to Rhys's brittleness is executed as planned. The reading invitation at the end lands correctly. The plan called for Rhys to apologize "awkwardly" and for Elin not to let him charm his way out — this is delivered. The plan mentioned a customer wanting a cheaper bent-cover paperback; this is absent, replaced by the sea-dragon boy, which works better dramatically. **Weaknesses** The chapter is not without flaws. Some of the narration edges toward over-explanation. "His eyes were not [casual]. He was watching her as if waiting for a door to open" is a slightly strained metaphor — doors don't quite capture what's meant. "Something in her chest shift, small and unwelcome" is functional but not distinguished. The observation about Rhys's hair being "untidy in a way that had almost certainly taken effort" is a well-worn observation about attractive men's grooming that doesn't feel fresh. The shelf-backstory opening ("installed by her grandmother's brother in 1978 and possessed the moral certainty of all things built by men who believed screws were a personal insult") is clever but slightly overwritten — it's trying a little hard for a comic opening. The phrase "moral certainty" applied to a shelf is the kind of forced whimsy that can grate. The Nansi scene is well-handled, but the moment where Rhys's "face had softened in a way Elin suspected he would hate being caught in" is telling rather than showing — we're being told what to think about his expression rather than seeing it. The ending is well-judged. Rhys not pouncing on her smile, the "bring your own mug" exchange, and the final image of the quiet shop after he leaves are all restrained and effective. The closing line — "For some reason, that made the shop feel quieter after he had gone" — is good without being overwrought. **Overall Assessment** This is a strong chapter that executes its plan with skill. The dialogue is the best element; the narration is competent but occasionally over-explains or reaches for a metaphor that doesn't quite land. The characters are consistent with their profiles, the chemistry is present without being forced, and the chapter advances the relationship credibly. It's not a masterpiece — there are moments of slight strain and a few too-easy observations — but it's genuinely accomplished work. [Scores] Nuanced Characters: 16 Emotionally Engaging: 15 Compelling Plot: 13 Coherent: 18 Weak Dialogue: 3 Tell-Don't-Show: 5 Unsurprising or Uncreative: 6 Amateurish: 3 Purple Prose: 4 Forced Poetry or Metaphor: 5 Well-earned Lightness or Darkness: 15 Characters Consistent with Profile: 17 Followed Chapter Plan: 16 Faithful to Writing Prompt: 17

Chapter 3

Ok now chapter 3. Follow your plan. 1000 words.
Model Output:
# Chapter 3: The Reading Room By seven o’clock, The Foxed Page smelled of wet coats, orange peel, and the faint electrical worry of the kettle. Elin had moved the display table against the wall, set out twelve mismatched chairs, and arranged the good mugs at the front in the optimistic belief that people would return them without chips. Nansi was taping a handwritten sign to the door. **POETRY AFTER HOURS TEA, £1 NO APOLOGIES FOR FEELINGS** “You wrote that last bit,” Elin said. Nansi pressed the tape down with a thumbnail. “It’s welcoming.” “It’s threatening.” “Same thing, really.” From the adjoining café, Teleri pushed through the connecting door with a tray of flapjacks balanced on one hand. “You’ve got eleven coming,” she announced. “Twelve if Dafydd decides his gout is cultural oppression again.” “Don’t put those on the rare-books table.” “They’re not on the rare-books table. They’re beside it.” “That’s worse.” Teleri rolled her eyes. She had tied her dark curls into a scarf, though several had escaped around her face. “You’re tense.” “I’m arranging chairs.” “You’re moving the same chair half an inch at a time.” “It was crooked.” “It was a chair, Elin.” The bell above the front door clanged. Both of them looked up. Rhys stood just inside, rain beading on the shoulders of his navy coat. He held a red enamel mug in one hand. “I brought my own,” he said. Teleri made a small sound. Elin closed her eyes briefly. Rhys looked between them. “Was that wrong?” “No,” said Teleri, too brightly. “No, love. It’s very right.” “Elin?” Rhys asked. “Elin thinks everything is wrong,” Teleri said. “Elin thinks you’re early.” “I was told seven-thirty.” “It is seven-thirty.” Rhys looked at his watch. “It’s seven twenty-eight.” “You’re early.” “I’m a nightmare, I know.” Teleri put the tray down and marched straight toward him, hand extended. “Teleri Morgan. Caffi Awen. Next door. I make the coffee that keeps this place from becoming a crime scene.” Rhys shook her hand. “Rhys.” “Oh, I know.” He smiled automatically, then glanced at Elin as if checking whether he had stepped into a trap. Teleri turned to her cousin. “You didn’t tell me he was this tall.” “I didn’t make a list.” “You should have. It would have saved me the surprise.” Rhys’s smile wavered between amusement and the wary recognition of a man who had met someone capable of making him work for it. “Would you like tea?” Elin asked, because the alternative was allowing Teleri to continue. “I have my mug.” “That wasn’t an answer.” “Yes, please.” “Milk?” “Whatever you’re having.” “I’m having no tea because I’m busy.” “Then whatever Nansi recommends.” Nansi appeared from the front door, took one look at him, and stopped. “Oh,” she said. Rhys lifted his mug slightly. “Hello again.” “You came.” “I did. I’m committed to page thirty-three and beyond.” Nansi looked at Elin. “Is he staying?” “For the reading, apparently.” “Right.” She turned back to Rhys. “Tea’s in the back. Don’t touch anything with a red sticker. That means it’s either sold or dangerous.” “Useful system.” “I designed it.” Rhys nodded seriously. “I can tell.” He followed her toward the kettle. Teleri leaned close to Elin. “He is absurdly handsome.” “Please don’t start.” “I’m not saying you should marry him.” “Thank God.” “I’m saying you might stop staring at him like he’s brought a rat into the stockroom.” “I’m not staring.” Teleri smiled with all the confidence of someone who had known Elin since they were five. “Of course not.” The first regulars began arriving: Mrs Bowen with her knitted hat still on indoors; Alun from the post office; two women from the harbour committee; a university student home for the week; and finally Dafydd Price, moving slowly but with the expression of a man refusing to be defeated by his knees. He took in the rearranged shop, the chairs, the flapjacks. “Too many cushions,” he said. “There are no cushions,” Elin replied. “That’s the spirit.” Then he saw Rhys by the kettle. Dafydd’s gaze lingered for a moment. “You’re new.” “I am.” “Do you read poetry?” Rhys looked down at the green book in his coat pocket. “I’m trying.” “Dangerous habit.” “So I’ve been told.” Dafydd nodded once, as though this answer had met a private standard, and limped toward the chair nearest the radiator. By half past seven, every chair was taken. Three people stood near the travel section. Rhys had chosen the back corner by the Welsh-language shelves, red mug cradled in both hands. He looked almost ordinary there, except that people kept glancing at him and then pretending they had not. Elin hated that she noticed the way he noticed them. She stood beside the till with a slim book open in her hands. “Right,” she said. “Thank you for coming. Tea is a pound, flapjacks are free because Teleri has made too many, and if anyone spills on the first editions, I will ask you to leave.” “Threatening,” Nansi whispered from behind her. “Welcoming,” Elin whispered back. The first reader was Mrs Bowen, who read a poem about her husband’s gardening shed with such grave feeling that no one knew whether to laugh. The second was Alun, whose poem about a dead mackerel was unexpectedly beautiful. By the time Dafydd read a short piece in Welsh about the sea in winter, the shop had gone still. Rhys did not look at anyone. He looked at the floor. When the applause came, he clapped last. Teleri, who had never met a silence she could not improve, stood from her chair. “Rhys reads, doesn’t he?” she said. Elin turned. “Tel.” “What? He’s an actor.” Rhys stared at Teleri over the rim of his mug. “No, absolutely not,” he said. “Go on,” said Mrs Bowen. “You’ve got a nice voice.” He looked at Elin. It was not a plea exactly. More like a question: *Do I have to?* Elin should have rescued him. Instead, because he had made her feel ridiculous twice already that week and because she was not above a little revenge, she handed him the next book from the stack. “Page forty-two,” she said. Rhys looked at the cover. A local collection, small press, thick cream paper. “You’re enjoying this,” he murmured. “Immensely.” He stood. For the first few words, he was too careful. His voice was smooth, the cadence lovely, every sentence placed with the faint shine of training. Elin felt herself bristle. Then he stopped, took a breath, and began again. The second time, he read more quietly. Not like he was trying to make the poem important. Like he had found something in it and was holding it out for the room to consider. It was only a page: a woman standing at a bus stop, waiting for a son who had moved away. Nothing dramatic happened. By the end, Elin had forgotten to breathe. Rhys looked up. The silence held. Then people clapped. He sat down quickly, as if escaping. Dafydd nodded toward him. “Better when you stopped trying to sound important.” A few people laughed. Rhys did too. A second late. Elin saw it. The tiny stiffening in his jaw. The way his thumb pressed hard against the rim of the mug. Dafydd, apparently unaware or unconcerned, leaned back in his chair. “You should read more.” Rhys smiled at him. “I’ll put it on my list.” The reading carried on. Nansi read a short poem she had written about a gull stealing chips. Teleri read nothing but interrupted twice to refill mugs. Elin read a paragraph from a novel by an author no one had heard of and then wished she had chosen something less earnest. By nine, the shop was a confusion of folded chairs, crumbs, damp coats, and people promising to come next month. Rhys stayed. Of course he did. He stacked chairs without being asked, though he did it badly, putting two small ones beneath a large one until the whole arrangement leaned dangerously. “Elbows in,” Elin said. “I am trying.” “You’re making it worse.” “That’s a very common accusation.” She took the chairs from him and restacked them. For a moment, they worked side by side in the narrow gap between the counter and the shelves. Teleri and Nansi were in the back room washing mugs loudly enough to suggest they were listening. Elin kept her eyes on the chairs. “You looked annoyed,” she said.
